The web-ui CLI's only existing\ngate (`--repo-path → repo`) continues to apply only to the\nimplementer module.\n\n## §G Docs\n\n- `reference/services/web-ui/README.md` gains an \"Evaluator module\n  (chunk 9d)\" section after the implementer-module section.\n  Documents the spec-to-code map for `POST /evaluator/{task_id}/submit`,\n  the trial_id ownership note, the metrics-validation parity, the\n  `artifacts_uri` non-hyperlinking note, and the per-error recovery\n  table from §C-recovery.\n- `AGENTS.md` (root): a new \"Phase 9 chunk 9d complete\" status block\n  prepended to the phase chain, mirroring the 9c one.\n- `reference/README.md`: bump status to reflect 9d.\n- `docs/roadmap.md`: mark 9d complete in the Phase 9 chunk list.\n\n## §H CI\n\nNo new gates. The existing `markdownlint` / `schema-parity` /\n`ruff` / `pyright` / `pytest` jobs all cover chunk 9d\nautomatically. The new `pytest.mark.e2e` test under\n`reference/services/web-ui/tests/test_evaluator_e2e.py` runs in the\nsame `uv run pytest -m e2e` job.\n\n## §I Tests\n\nFive new test files under\n`reference/services/web-ui/tests/`, paralleling 9c:\n\n1. **`test_evaluator_routes.py`** — per-route validation in\n   isolation (TestClient with the in-memory store fixture).\n   - GET `/evaluator/` lists pending tasks, hides claimed ones,\n     surfaces the banner from `?banner=…`.\n   - POST `/evaluator/<task_id>/claim` rejects missing/wrong CSRF,\n     200 on success, redirects to `/evaluator/<task_id>/draft`,\n     stashes `(token, trial_id)` in `_CLAIMS`.\n   - POST `/evaluator/<task_id>/claim` on an already-terminalized\n     task surfaces a wire-error banner.\n   - GET `/evaluator/<task_id>/draft` without a stashed claim\n     redirects with `claim+missing+from+session` banner.\n   - GET `/evaluator/<task_id>/draft` renders one input per\n     `metrics_schema` entry with the correct `type` attribute\n     (`number step=1` for integer, `number step=any` for real,\n     `text` for text).\n   - POST `/evaluator/<task_id>/submit` rejects status outside the\n     three allowed values.\n   - POST submit with `status=success` and zero metric values →\n     400 + form re-render with overall error.\n   - POST submit with `status=error` and zero metrics → 200 +\n     submitted page (zero-metric error allowed).\n   - POST submit with `status=eval_error` and zero metrics → 200.\n   - POST submit with a metric value of wrong type (e.g. `1.5`\n     for an integer key) → 400 + field-level error on that key.\n   - POST submit with a metric key outside the schema (hand-\n     crafted body) → 400 + field-level error.\n   - POST submit with a `nan` for a real metric → 400.\n   - POST submit with a forged `trial_id` form field → ignored\n     (the recorded submission carries the server-pinned trial_id).\n   - POST submit on a `success` with one valid metric →\n     `submitted` page; recorded `EvaluateSubmission` matches\n     `(status=\"success\", trial_id=<server-pinned>, metrics={...},\n     artifacts_uri=None)`.\n\n2. **`test_evaluator_flow.py`** — multi-request sequences.\n   - Full happy path: claim → draft (form rendered) → submit\n     (200 confirmation page). Verify `_CLAIMS` cleared, task\n     state is `submitted` (the orchestrator-side terminal\n     transition is out of the UI's scope).\n   - Validation-recovery: claim → submit with a missing required\n     metric for `status=success` → re-render with form_state\n     preserving the operator's other inputs → fix the metric →\n     submit → 200.\n   - Stranded-claim recovery via the chunk-1 sweeper: claim with a\n     1-second TTL → wait → call `eden_dispatch.sweep_expired_claims`\n     directly → original session's submit gets `WrongToken` →\n     orphan page (`recovery_kind=\"auto\"`).\n   - `eval_error` path: claim → submit with `status=eval_error`\n     and partial metrics → 200; recorded submission's metrics is\n     present (orchestrator discards it on terminal transition,\n     but that's the orchestrator's behavior — at the store the\n     submission is recorded as-typed).\n   - Conflict path: claim from session A; manually call\n     `store.submit` directly with a *different* metrics value\n     under a *different* claim token (simulating an operator-\n     side replay or a different role-binding); session A's\n     submit raises `WrongToken` → orphan page `recovery_kind=\"auto\"`.\n\n3. **`test_evaluator_partial_write.py`** — recovery branches via\n   monkeypatched `store.submit` failures, mirroring 9c's\n   partial-write tests.\n   - **Sub-case A** (server committed, response lost): mock\n     `store.submit` as commit-AND-raise on every call (per the\n     codified\n     `feedback_retry_readback_test_mocks.md` pattern). The\n     route exhausts retries, the read-back finds task in\n     `submitted` and submission equivalent → success page (200).\n   - **Sub-case A'** (server committed, then terminalized): mock\n     `store.submit` as commit-AND-raise; on the first call also\n     drive `store.accept(task, EvaluateSubmission(...))` with a\n     *different* `metrics` to push the task to `completed` with\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module/20260424T232413/0.jsonl:19:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_9","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' spec/v0/03-roles.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Role Contracts\n\nThis chapter defines the four role contracts that a conforming EDEN deployment implements: **planner**, **implementer**, **evaluator**, and **integrator**. For each, it pins what the role observes, what it produces, and which invariants its output MUST honor.\n\nA role contract does **not** specify how the role is hosted. A conforming planner MAY run as a local subprocess, a long-lived service reached over HTTP, an in-process adapter, or a human at a keyboard. The protocol constrains the *observable effects* a role has on the task store, the event log, and the artifact store; how a role is invoked is a deployment concern and is deferred to a later-phase *role-binding* specification. Within v0, implementations MAY document their own binding by defining additional top-level fields on the experiment config per [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4.\n\nThe behavioral state machine that all worker roles participate in — claim, execute, submit, reclaim — is defined in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). This chapter describes only the *role-specific* part of each worker's job: what it reads, what it produces, and what guarantees its output must carry. Where a rule depends on a state-machine transition, this chapter cites the transition by name and points to Chapter 04 for its semantics.\n\n## 1. Common worker-role lifecycle\n\nThe planner, implementer, and evaluator are **worker roles**: they participate in the task protocol as consumers. Each worker role progresses through the same outer lifecycle for every task it handles:\n\n1. **Discover.** The worker becomes aware of a pending task whose `kind` matches its role. Mechanisms include polling the task store, subscribing to an event stream, or receiving a dispatch call — all are permitted; none are mandated.\n2. **Claim.** The worker atomically moves the task from `pending` to `claimed` and receives a claim token. Semantics are in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.\n3. **Execute.** The worker performs its role-specific work using the task payload and any context the protocol grants it (§2–§4 below). The worker MAY report progress. It MUST NOT mutate any protocol-owned object other than (a) the task it holds a claim on and (b) the outputs its role is specified to produce (§2.2, §3.2, §4.2). The restrictions in §1.2 apply to every worker role in addition to the per-role rules below.\n4. **Submit.** The worker atomically moves the task from `claimed` to `submitted`, presenting its claim token and a result (§2–§4 below). A submitted task then advances to `completed` or `failed` per the rules in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.\n5. **Release on reclaim.** If the task store reclaims the task before submit (task store policy per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5), the worker's token is invalidated. A worker that discovers its token has been invalidated MUST NOT subsequently attempt to submit against that task; it MUST discard any partial result.\n\nIntegrator behavior does **not** follow this worker-role lifecycle. The integrator is event-driven and is defined separately in §5 below and in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n\n### 1.1 What a worker MAY assume about its inputs\n\nBefore a task is dispatched to a worker-role queue, the orchestrator MUST ensure the task's payload references (proposal, trial, parent commits) satisfy the dispatch preconditions listed per role below. A worker MAY therefore rely on those preconditions and is not required to re-validate them. A worker that nevertheless discovers a violation (e.g. a referenced proposal has been retracted) MUST fail the task with an error status; it MUST NOT repair store state itself.\n\n### 1.2 What a worker MUST NOT do\n\n- A worker MUST NOT write to any protocol-owned object other than the task it holds a claim on and the outputs that task's role is defined to produce (§2–§4).\n- A worker MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage (`trial/*`); that is the integrator's exclusive authority ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9, [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).\n- A worker MUST NOT advance a task's state beyond `submitted`. Transitions to `completed` or `failed` are the orchestrator's responsibility ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4).\n\n## 2. Planner\n\nThe planner proposes what to try. Every planner invocation consumes one `plan` task and produces zero or more proposals.\n\n### 2.1 Inputs\n\nA planner receives:\n\n- The task object (`kind == \"plan\"`, `payload.experiment_id == E`).\n- Read access to the experiment config for `E` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2), including the `metrics_schema` and `objective`.\n- Read access to the set of proposals and trials already persisted for `E`. The mechanism (direct store read, orchestrator-supplied snapshot, event-log replay) is a role-binding concern.\n\nA planner MAY additionally use any context the experiment config surfaces to it via role-binding-specific fields. The protocol does not require the planner to read beyond what is listed above.\n\n### 2.2 Outputs\n\nFor each proposal the planner produces, it MUST:\n\n1. Persist a proposal object (via the protocol's proposal store — mechanism is binding-specific) that validates against [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json), with `experiment_id == E` and `state == \"drafting\"`.\n2. Upload any artifacts (plan text, rationale, supporting files) to the artifact store and populate the proposal's `artifacts_uri`.\n3. Transition the proposal's `state` to `\"ready\"` once the proposal is dispatchable. The transition from `drafting` to `ready` signals that the proposal's metadata is stable and that the implementer MAY consume it.\n\nA planner MAY create multiple proposals under a single `plan` task. A planner MAY also produce zero proposals if it has no viable change to suggest; the task still completes normally (see §2.4).\n\n### 2.3 Proposal invariants\n\nThe planner MUST honor the structural invariants of proposals ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5):\n\n- `parent_commits` MUST contain at least one SHA that names a commit reachable from either the experiment's starting commit on `main` or a completed trial on the canonical lineage ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9).\n- `slug` MUST match the documented pattern.\n- `priority` is the planner's own ordering hint; higher values SHOULD dispatch earlier.\n\n### 2.4 Submission\n\nWhen its work on a `plan` task is complete, the planner MUST submit the task (§1 step 4). The submission payload MUST include:\n\n- `proposal_ids` — the set of identifiers of every proposal the planner created under this task. Order is not significant; dispatch ordering is determined by each proposal's `priority` field ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1). MAY be empty.\n- `status` — one of `\"success\"` (the planner completed normally, regardless of proposal count) or `\"error\"` (the planner could not complete; any partially-written proposals MUST remain in `\"drafting\"` state and MUST NOT be dispatched).\n\nA planner MUST NOT submit a `plan` task while any of its referenced proposals is still in `\"drafting\"` state.\n\n## 3. Implementer\n\nThe implementer realizes a proposal as a working-tree change on a per-trial branch.\n\n### 3.1 Inputs\n\nAn implementer receives:\n\n- The task object (`kind == \"implement\"`, `payload.proposal_id == P`).\n- Read access to the proposal `P`. The orchestrator MUST ensure `P.state == \"ready\"` before dispatch.\n- Read and write access to the git repository associated with `P.experiment_id`, at a revision reachable from `P.parent_commits`. How the repository is located and how access is granted is a role-binding concern (§6, [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4); the protocol constrains only that the implementer's writes land under `work/*` and that no commit is introduced whose history does not descend from `P.parent_commits` (§3.3).\n- Read access to the artifacts at `P.artifacts_uri`.\n\n### 3.2 Outputs\n\nThe implementer produces a **trial** object ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7). It MUST:\n\n1. Persist a trial with `status == \"starting\"`, `experiment_id == P.experiment_id`, `proposal_id == P`, and `parent_commits == P.parent_commits`, before making any repository write observable to other roles.\n2. Write the implementation on a branch under `work/*` ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9) whose parent(s) are the proposal's `parent_commits`. Set the trial's `branch` field to this branch name.\n3. On successful completion, set the trial's `commit_sha` to the tip of the `work/*` branch. Optionally set `artifacts_uri` and `description`.\n\nThe implementer MUST NOT write to `trial/*` or `main`; those are owned by the integrator. The `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial MUST NOT be set by the implementer.\n\n### 3.3 Worker-branch invariants\n\n- The `work/*` branch MUST be unique to this trial. Two trials MUST NOT share a worker branch name.\n- Every commit on the worker branch MUST be reachable from the declared `parent_commits`. An implementer MUST NOT introduce commits whose history does not descend from the proposal's declared parents.\n- The implementer MAY produce multiple commits on the worker branch. The evaluator consumes only the tip (`commit_sha`); the integrator's squash rule is defined in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n\n### 3.4 Submission\n\nThe implementer submits with:\n\n- `trial_id` — the trial it created.\n- `status` — one of:\n  - `\"success\"` — the `work/*` branch tip is `commit_sha` and is ready to be evaluated.\n  - `\"error\"` — the implementer could not realize the proposal. The trial MUST be persisted with `status == \"error\"`. No evaluate task is dispatched against an errored trial.\n- `commit_sha` — required when `status == \"success\"`; MUST equal the tip of the worker branch.\n\nA resubmission of the same `implement` task MUST be idempotent: a duplicate submit presenting the same `trial_id` and `commit_sha` MUST be accepted without side effect. A duplicate submit that disagrees with the already-recorded result MUST be rejected ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2).\n\n## 4. Evaluator\n\nThe evaluator measures a completed trial against the experiment's metrics schema.\n\n### 4.1 Inputs\n\nAn evaluator receives:\n\n- The task object (`kind == \"evaluate\"`, `payload.trial_id == T`).\n- Read access to the trial `T`. The orchestrator MUST ensure `T.status == \"starting\"` and `T.commit_sha` is set before dispatch.\n- Read access to the git repository at `T.commit_sha` on the worker branch (`T.branch`). Repository location and access are a role-binding concern, as for the implementer (§3.1).\n- Read access to the experiment's `metrics_schema` and `objective`.\n\n### 4.2 Outputs\n\nThe evaluator MUST:\n\n1. Produce a `metrics` object whose keys are a subset of the declared `metrics_schema` keys and whose values satisfy the per-metric type rules ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3, §7.2).\n2. Optionally upload supporting artifacts (logs, captured outputs, diagnostic files).\n\nThe evaluator MUST NOT modify the worker branch or any protocol-owned mutable state other than the trial fields the submission writes (§4.4) and the task it holds a claim on. In particular, the evaluator MUST NOT write to the trial's `completed_at`, `metrics`, `artifacts_uri`, `description`, or `status` directly; those writes are performed by the orchestrator when the submitted task reaches its terminal state (§4.4, [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).\n\n### 4.3 Non-interference\n\nThe evaluator reads the repository at the trial's worker-branch tip but MUST NOT push, rewrite, or delete that branch, and MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation (test caches, build outputs, captured logs) MUST NOT be observable through any protocol-owned store, ref, or artifact location except via the evaluator's declared outputs (§4.2).\n\n### 4.4 Submission\n\nThe evaluator submits with:\n\n- `trial_id` — the trial it evaluated.\n- `status` — one of:\n  - `\"success\"` — the trial ran and produced metrics.\n  - `\"error\"` — the trial could not be evaluated for reasons attributable to the trial's own code (build failure, test failure, etc.). The evaluator MAY still include partial metrics.\n  - `\"eval_error\"` — the evaluator itself failed for reasons unrelated to the trial's code (infrastructure fault, evaluator bug). While a fresh evaluate task MAY still be created for this trial, the trial's status MUST remain `\"starting\"`. If the orchestrator's retry policy is exhausted (or the operator abandons evaluation), the orchestrator MUST transition the trial's status to `\"eval_error\"`, making that status terminal for the trial ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).\n- `metrics` — the metrics object described in §4.2. MAY be absent when `status == \"eval_error\"`.\n- `artifacts_uri` — OPTIONAL. A URI the evaluator uploaded supporting artifacts to.\n\nOn a `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed` transition (per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3), the orchestrator MUST write the following trial fields atomically with the event:\n\n- `status` — the trial status implied by the submission: `\"success\"` when the submission's `status == \"success\"`; `\"error\"` when the submission's `status == \"error\"`; unchanged from `\"starting\"` when the submission's `status == \"eval_error\"` (see §4.4 above for the terminal-retry case).\n- `metrics` — set to the submission's `metrics` when `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When `status == \"eval_error\"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write `metrics` on the trial; any submission-carried `metrics` is discarded.\n- `artifacts_uri` — set to the submission's `artifacts_uri` when provided and `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When `status == \"eval_error\"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write `artifacts_uri` on the trial; any submission-carried `artifacts_uri` is discarded. (An evaluator that wishes to retain diagnostic artifacts from a failed attempt MAY reference them in the `task.failed` event for that evaluate task; that channel is defined in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md).)\n- `completed_at` — set to the time of the terminal trial transition, i.e. written exactly once, when the trial's status leaves `\"starting\"` (either on a `\"success\"`/`\"error\"` submission, or on the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"` transition). Intermediate `eval_error` submissions MUST NOT advance `completed_at`.\n\nOn the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"` terminal transition itself, the orchestrator MUST NOT graft metrics or artifacts from any prior `eval_error` submission onto the trial; the trial's `metrics` and `artifacts_uri` fields remain unset. This keeps the trial object canonical: a trial either carries the outputs of a successful or code-level-failed evaluation, or it carries nothing.\n\nResubmission is idempotent under the same rules as §3.4: identical `trial_id` + `status` + `metrics` + `artifacts_uri` MUST be accepted; inconsistent resubmission MUST be rejected.\n\n## 5. Integrator\n\nThe integrator promotes successfully evaluated trials into the canonical trial lineage.\n\nThe integrator's full contract — the squash rule, the eval-manifest shape, conflict-resolution policy — is deferred to [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md). This section pins only the boundary rules that every other role must honor.\n\n### 5.1 Exclusive authority\n\nThe integrator is the **sole writer** of the canonical trial lineage (`trial/*` branches, `trial_commit_sha` fields on trials). Every other role MUST treat `trial/*` as read-only and MUST NOT set or modify a trial's `trial_commit_sha`.\n\n### 5.2 Inputs\n\nThe integrator observes trials transitioning to `status == \"success\"` (as recorded by the evaluator in §4.4). The mechanism of observation (event subscription, store poll, dispatch) is a binding concern.\n\n### 5.3 Outputs\n\nThe integrator writes a single commit on a `trial/*` branch for each promoted trial and records the resulting SHA in the trial's `trial_commit_sha`. Exact topology invariants are in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n\n### 5.4 Why integration is separate from evaluation\n\nEvaluation and integration are separate roles because a successful evaluation is a necessary but not sufficient condition for promoting a trial: the integrator applies experiment-level policy (squash shape, conflict resolution with concurrent trials, manifest attachment) that is not meaningful to the evaluator. A conforming implementation MAY collapse evaluator and integrator into the same process, but MUST still honor both contracts independently.\n\n## 6. Role binding (deferred)\n\nHow a role is invoked, addressed, or configured is **not specified in v0**. A conforming deployment MAY:\n\n- Run each role as a separate long-lived service reached over some wire protocol.\n- Spawn each role as a short-lived subprocess per task.\n- Collapse multiple roles into a single process that consumes its own task queue.\n- Host a role as a WebAssembly module, a browser tab, or a human interface.\n\nRole-binding information that an implementation needs to invoke its roles MAY be carried as additional top-level fields on the experiment config ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4). A future v0 addition or a v1 chapter will specify a standardized role-binding representation; until then, the protocol constrains only what each role observes and produces, not how it is reached.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Errors propagate as canonical wire-error names so the user\nsees an honest banner.\n\nThe Phase-3 retry policy on ``submit`` leverages chapter 07 §2.4 /\n§8.1: a content-equivalent resubmit returns 200, so transport\nfailures are retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff before\nthe orphaned-proposals error page is reached.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport time\nimport uuid\nfrom collections.abc import Callable\nfrom datetime import timedelta\nfrom typing import Any\n\nfrom eden_contracts import Proposal\nfrom eden_storage import (\n    ConflictingResubmission,\n    DispatchError,\n    IllegalTransition,\n    InvalidPrecondition,\n    PlanSubmission,\n    WrongToken,\n)\nfrom fastapi import APIRouter, Form, Request\nfrom fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse, RedirectResponse\n\nfrom ..artifacts import write_proposal_artifact\nfrom ..forms import ProposalDraft, parse_proposal_rows\nfrom ._helpers import csrf_ok, get_session, htmx_aware_redirect, is_htmx_request\n\nrouter = APIRouter(prefix=\"/planner\")\n\n\n# In-memory mapping of (csrf_token, task_id) -> claim token. The CSRF\n# token is per-session (rotates on session-secret rotation), so two\n# browser sessions with the same configured worker_id cannot reuse\n# each other's claims. We deliberately do not persist this across\n# restarts — a UI restart drops claim tokens and the TTL sweep\n# recovers stranded tasks (eden_dispatch.sweep_expired_claims).\n_CLAIMS: dict[tuple[str, str], str] = {}\n\n\ndef _claim_key(session_csrf: str, task_id: str) -> tuple[str, str]:\n    return (session_csrf, task_id)\n\n\ndef _list_recent_proposals(store: Any, *, limit: int = 20) -> list[Any]:\n    items = store.list_proposals()\n    return items[-limit:]\n\n\ndef _list_recent_trials(store: Any, *, limit: int = 20) -> list[Any]:\n    items = store.list_trials()\n    return items[-limit:]\n\n\n@router.get(\"/\", response_class=HTMLResponse, response_model=None)\nasync def list_pending(request: Request) -> HTMLResponse | RedirectResponse:\n    session = get_session(request)\n    if session is None:\n        return RedirectResponse(url=\"/signin\", status_code=303)\n    store = request.app.state.store\n    pending = store.list_tasks(kind=\"plan\", state=\"pending\")\n    config = request.app.state.experiment_config\n    return request.app.state.templates.TemplateResponse(\n        request,\n        \"planner_list.html\",\n        {\n            \"session\": session,\n            \"pending\": pending,\n            \"objective\": config.objective,\n            \"metrics_schema\": config.metrics_schema.root,\n            \"recent_proposals\": _list_recent_proposals(store),\n            \"recent_trials\": _list_recent_trials(store),\n            \"banner\": request.query_params.get(\"banner\"),\n        },\n    )\n\n\n@router.post(\"/{task_id}/claim\", response_model=None)\nasync def claim(\n    task_id: str,\n    request: Request,\n    csrf_token: str = Form(\"\"),\n) -> RedirectResponse | HTMLResponse:\n    session = get_session(request)\n    if session is None:\n        return RedirectResponse(url=\"/signin\", status_code=303)\n    if not csrf_ok(session, csrf_token):\n        return _csrf_failure_response(request)\n    store = request.app.state.store\n    now: Callable[[], Any] = request.app.state.now\n    expires_at = now() + timedelta(seconds=request.app.state.claim_ttl_seconds)\n    try:\n        result = store.claim(task_id, session.worker_id, expires_at=expires_at)\n    except (IllegalTransition, InvalidPrecondition) as exc:\n        banner = _wire_error_banner(exc)\n        return RedirectResponse(url=f\"/planner/?banner={banner}\", status_code=303)\n    _CLAIMS[_claim_key(session.csrf, task_id)] = result.token\n    return RedirectResponse(url=f\"/planner/{task_id}/draft\", status_code=303)\n\n\n@router.get(\"/{task_id}/draft\", response_class=HTMLResponse, response_model=None)\nasync def draft_form(\n    task_id: str,\n    request: Request,\n) -> HTMLResponse | RedirectResponse:\n    session = get_session(request)\n    if session is None:\n        return RedirectResponse(url=\"/signin\", status_code=303)\n    token = _CLAIMS.get(_claim_key(session.csrf, task_id))\n    if token is None:\n        return RedirectResponse(\n            url=\"/planner/?banner=claim+missing+from+session\",\n            status_code=303,\n        )\n    config = request.app.state.experiment_config\n    return request.app.state.templates.TemplateResponse(\n        request,\n        \"planner_claim.html\",\n        {\n            \"session\": session,\n            \"task_id\": task_id,\n            \"objective\": config.objective,\n            \"metrics_schema\": config.metrics_schema.root,\n            \"errors\": None,\n            \"form_state\": [_empty_row()],\n            \"row_indices\": [0],\n        },\n    )\n\n\n@router.post(\"/{task_id}/add_row\", response_model=None)\nasync def add_row(task_id: str, request: Request):\n    \"\"\"Append one empty proposal row to the draft form.\n\n    Two transports, same end state:\n\n    - With JS, htmx posts here with ``HX-Request: true``. We respond\n      with the rendered ``_proposal_row.html`` fragment for the new\n      row only; htmx swaps it as ``beforeend`` of ``#proposal-rows``\n      so the user's existing input is untouched. Redirect/error\n      branches send ``HX-Redirect`` on a 204 so htmx does a full\n      client-side navigation rather than swapping a redirected page\n      into the rows container.\n    - Without JS, the same button submits the form normally and we\n      re-render the whole ``planner_claim.html`` with the\n      collected state plus one more empty row, or 303 to the\n      sign-in / planner list on auth/claim failures.\n    \"\"\"\n    session = get_session(request)\n    if session is None:\n        return htmx_aware_redirect(request, \"/signin\")\n    form = await request.form()\n    if not csrf_ok(session, form.get(\"csrf_token\")):  # type: ignore[arg-type]\n        return _csrf_failure_response(request)\n    if _CLAIMS.get(_claim_key(session.csrf, task_id)) is None:\n        return htmx_aware_redirect(\n            request, \"/planner/?banner=claim+missing+from+session\"\n        )\n\n    if is_htmx_request(request):\n        # The htmx-enhanced path: figure out where the new row goes\n        # by counting existing rows in the form, then render only\n        # the partial. The user's existing rows are already on the\n        # page; htmx appends ours to ``#proposal-rows``.\n        existing = max(\n            len(form.getlist(\"slug\")),\n            len(form.getlist(\"priority\")),\n            len(form.getlist(\"parent_commits\")),\n            len(form.getlist(\"rationale\")),\n        )\n        return request.app.state.templates.TemplateResponse(\n            request,\n            \"_proposal_row.html\",\n            {\"i\": existing, \"row_state\": _empty_row(), \"row_errs\": {}},\n        )\n\n    config = request.app.state.experiment_config\n    slugs = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"slug\")]\n    priorities = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"priority\")]\n    parents = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"parent_commits\")]\n    rationales = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"rationale\")]\n    state = _form_state_from_inputs(slugs, priorities, parents, rationales)\n    state.append(_empty_row())\n    return request.app.state.templates.TemplateResponse(\n        request,\n        \"planner_claim.html\",\n        {\n            \"session\": session,\n            \"task_id\": task_id,\n            \"objective\": config.objective,\n            \"metrics_schema\": config.metrics_schema.root,\n            \"errors\": None,\n            \"form_state\": state,\n            \"row_indices\": list(range(len(state))),\n        },\n    )\n\n\n@router.post(\"/{task_id}/submit\", response_model=None)\nasync def submit_plan(task_id: str, request: Request) -> HTMLResponse | RedirectResponse:\n    session = get_session(request)\n    if session is None:\n        return RedirectResponse(url=\"/signin\", status_code=303)\n    form = await request.form()\n    if not csrf_ok(session, form.get(\"csrf_token\")):  # type: ignore[arg-type]\n        return _csrf_failure_response(request)\n    status = form.get(\"status\")\n    if status not in (\"success\", \"error\"):\n        return _bad_request(\"invalid submit status\")\n\n    token = _CLAIMS.get(_claim_key(session.csrf, task_id))\n    if token is None:\n        return RedirectResponse(\n            url=\"/planner/?banner=claim+missing+from+session\",\n            status_code=303,\n        )\n\n    store = request.app.state.store\n    config = request.app.state.experiment_config\n\n    if status == \"error\":\n        try:\n            store.submit(task_id, token, PlanSubmission(status=\"error\"))\n        except DispatchError as exc:\n            return _render_error(request, _wire_error_banner(exc))\n        _CLAIMS.pop(_claim_key(session.csrf, task_id), None)\n        return _render_submitted(request, task_id, status=\"error\", proposal_ids=())\n\n    slugs = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"slug\")]\n    priorities = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"priority\")]\n    parents = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"parent_commits\")]\n    rationales = [str(v) for v in form.getlist(\"rationale\")]\n    drafts, errors = parse_proposal_rows(slugs, priorities, parents, rationales)\n    if errors:\n        return request.app.state.templates.TemplateResponse(\n            request,\n            \"planner_claim.html\",\n            {\n                \"session\": session,\n                \"task_id\": task_id,\n                \"objective\": config.objective,\n                \"metrics_schema\": config.metrics_schema.root,\n                \"errors\": errors,\n                \"form_state\": _form_state_from_inputs(slugs, priorities, parents, rationales),\n                \"row_indices\": list(range(max(1, len(slugs) or 1))),\n            },\n            status_code=400,\n        )\n\n    proposal_ids: list[str] = []\n    artifacts_dir = request.app.state.artifacts_dir\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

MAY be absent when `status == \"eval_error\"`.\nspec/v0/03-roles.md:153:- `status` — the trial status implied by the submission: `\"success\"` when the submission's `status == \"success\"`; `\"error\"` when the submission's `status == \"error\"`; unchanged from `\"starting\"` when the submission's `status == \"eval_error\"` (see §4.4 above for the terminal-retry case).\nspec/v0/03-roles.md:154:- `metrics` — set to the submission's `metrics` when `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When `status == \"eval_error\"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write `metrics` on the trial; any submission-carried `metrics` is discarded.\nspec/v0/03-roles.md:155:- `artifacts_uri` — set to the submission's `artifacts_uri` when provided and `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When `status == \"eval_error\"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write `artifacts_uri` on the trial; any submission-carried `artifacts_uri` is discarded. (An evaluator that wishes to retain diagnostic artifacts from a failed attempt MAY reference them in the `task.failed` event for that evaluate task; that channel is defined in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md).)\nspec/v0/03-roles.md:156:- `completed_at` — set to the time of the terminal trial transition, i.e. written exactly once, when the trial's status leaves `\"starting\"` (either on a `\"success\"`/`\"error\"` submission, or on the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"` transition). Intermediate `eval_error` submissions MUST NOT advance `completed_at`.\nspec/v0/03-roles.md:158:On the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"` terminal transition itself, the orchestrator MUST NOT graft metrics or artifacts from any prior `eval_error` submission onto the trial; the trial's `metrics` and `artifacts_uri` fields remain unset. This keeps the trial object canonical: a trial either carries the outputs of a successful or code-level-failed evaluation, or it carries nothing.\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:85:| `declare_trial_eval_error` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/declare-eval-error` |\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:86:| `integrate_trial` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/integrate` |\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:88:`integrate_trial` binds [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4 and carries additional idempotency rules (§5 below); the other endpoints are transport-only bindings of their [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.7 operations.\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:90:## 5. `integrate_trial` — same-value idempotency\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:95:- **Same-value idempotency.** A repeated call whose `trial_commit_sha` equals the value already stored on the trial MUST return 200 and MUST NOT append a second `trial.integrated` event. This is what lets a client safely retry an `integrate_trial` request after a transport- indeterminate failure without double-commit.\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:99:A client that issues `integrate_trial` and does not receive a 2xx response (connection reset, read timeout, proxy disconnect) MUST treat the outcome as indeterminate and MUST NOT assume the server has not committed. The reference reconciliation procedure is a read-back (`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}`) that resolves to one of three outcomes:\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:153:| `eden://error/invalid-precondition` | 409 | referenced entity not in required state; also the different-SHA branch of `integrate_trial` (§5) |\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:165:Same-value idempotency on `integrate_trial` is pinned in §5.\nspec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md:194:- Resolves transport-indeterminate `integrate_trial` failures via the read-back procedure in §5 and does not compensate ref docs/plans/review/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module/20260424T232413/0.jsonl:31:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_15","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Task Protocol\n\nThis chapter specifies the behavioral contract for tasks: the state machine they advance through, the claim tokens that grant exclusive execution, the idempotency rules for submission, and the reclamation policy that bounds worker liveness. It pins *what* observable behavior a conforming task store and a conforming orchestrator must exhibit; it does not pin *how* either is implemented.\n\nThe task object's shape is defined in [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3; its JSON Schema is [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json). The role contracts that drive the transitions defined here are in [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md).\n\n## 1. State machine\n\n### 1.1 States\n\nA task's `state` field takes one of five values. Each has a well-defined meaning in the state machine:\n\n| State | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `pending` | The task has been created and is eligible to be claimed. No worker currently holds it. |\n| `claimed` | A worker holds an active claim on the task and is expected to execute it. The task carries a `claim` object. |\n| `submitted` | The claiming worker has presented a result. The task awaits processing by the orchestrator. |\n| `completed` | The orchestrator has accepted the result. The task is terminal. |\n| `failed` | The orchestrator has rejected the result, the worker declared failure, or a policy limit (e.g. retry budget) has been exceeded. The task is terminal. |\n\n`completed` and `failed` are **terminal**: a conforming task store MUST NOT transition a task out of either state.\n\n### 1.2 Transitions\n\nThe permitted transitions are exactly:\n\n```text\n          claim                 submit                accept\npending ─────────▶ claimed ─────────────▶ submitted ─────────▶ completed\n   ▲                 │                       │\n   │ reclaim         │ reclaim               │ reject\n   └─────────────────┴───────────────────────┤           ──▶ failed\n                                             │\n```\n\n- **claim** — `pending → claimed`. Atomic. Issues a fresh claim token. §3.\n- **submit** — `claimed → submitted`. Requires presenting the current claim token. Idempotent under identical payload. Performed for every completed attempt, whether the worker declares success or failure. §4.\n- **reclaim** — `claimed → pending` or `submitted → pending`. Invalidates the prior claim token. §5.\n- **accept** — `submitted → completed`. Orchestrator-initiated. §4.3.\n- **reject** — `submitted → failed`. Orchestrator-initiated, including when the worker's own submission declared failure. §4.3.\n\nAny transition not listed above MUST be rejected by the task store. A conforming task store MUST treat concurrent transition attempts as serialized: whichever attempt wins the race succeeds, and the others MUST receive a well-defined rejection that lets the caller distinguish \"invalid\" from \"raced\".\n\n### 1.3 Event emission\n\nEvery state transition MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event appended to the event log, atomically with the state change. The event types (e.g. `task.claimed`, `task.submitted`, `task.failed`) and the transactional invariant are specified in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md). This chapter does not enumerate the event shapes; it requires only that they exist and that no state change is observable without its event.\n\n## 2. Creating a task\n\nA task enters the system in the `pending` state. A conforming orchestrator MUST:\n\n- Populate `task_id`, `kind`, `state = \"pending\"`, `payload`, `created_at`, `updated_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1).\n- Ensure the payload satisfies the dispatch preconditions for the task's `kind`:\n  - `plan` — `payload.experiment_id` names an experiment the planner is authorized to work on.\n  - `implement` — `payload.proposal_id` names a proposal with `state == \"ready\"` at creation time. Creating the implement task and transitioning the proposal from `\"ready\"` to `\"dispatched\"` MUST be atomic; a proposal's `\"dispatched\"` state signals that exactly one implement task has been created for it.\n  - `evaluate` — `payload.trial_id` names a trial with `status == \"starting\"` and `commit_sha` set at creation time.\n- Emit the corresponding `task.created` event ( [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) atomically with the task insert.\n\nA worker MAY rely on these preconditions ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1.1). A task created without them is a protocol violation by the orchestrator.\n\n## 3. Claim\n\n### 3.1 Atomicity\n\nThe `pending → claimed` transition MUST be atomic with respect to competing claim attempts: at most one worker MAY succeed for any given task. A conforming task store MUST provide this guarantee as part of its durability contract ([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).\n\n### 3.2 Claim token\n\nA successful claim issues a `claim` object ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4) containing:\n\n- `token` — an opaque value that MUST be:\n  - **unique** — no two distinct claims (on the same or different tasks) share the same token within an experiment;\n  - **unforgeable** — a worker that did not receive the token MUST NOT be able to construct a byte-equal value with practical effort. Implementations commonly use a cryptographically random value; the protocol does not mandate the mechanism.\n- `worker_id` — an identifier the worker supplies at claim time. The task store MAY record it for audit but MUST NOT use it as a substitute for the token when authorizing subsequent operations.\n- `claimed_at` — the time the claim was issued.\n- `expires_at` — OPTIONAL. If present, the task store MAY reclaim the task when the current time exceeds this value (§5).\n\n### 3.3 Authorization by token\n\nEvery subsequent operation on a claimed task (progress update, submit, release) MUST present the current claim token. The task store MUST reject any such operation whose token does not match the token currently recorded on the task. This is the sole mechanism the protocol relies on to guarantee single-writer access to a claimed task.\n\n### 3.4 No re-claim while claimed\n\nA conforming task store MUST reject a claim attempt against a task whose `state` is not `pending`. In particular, a claimed task cannot be \"re-claimed\" by a different worker; the prior claim must first be released (by submission) or invalidated (by reclamation).\n\n## 4. Submit\n\n### 4.1 Submission operation\n\nA submit operation requires:\n\n- The `task_id`.\n- The current claim `token`.\n- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind` ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).\n\nA successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` atomically with persisting the result payload. The terminal transition to `completed` or `failed` is the orchestrator's subsequent step (§4.3), even when the submission itself declared failure.\n\n### 4.2 Idempotency\n\nA resubmission is a second submit carrying the token recorded on the task's `claim` object (which is retained through `submitted` per [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). A resubmit against a task whose claim has been cleared — because the task was reclaimed or reached a terminal state — MUST be rejected regardless of the presented token (§4.4, §5.2).\n\nWhen the token matches, the task store MUST handle the resubmission as follows:\n\n- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. \"Content equivalence\" means the normative fields identified per role agree:\n  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (compared as sets; order is not significant per [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4) and `status`.\n  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha` (when present).\n  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as JSON values; key order does not matter).\n- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent, the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is the committed result.\n\nThis rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a network or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or corrupting the recorded result.\n\n### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal\n\nA submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions to a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:\n\n- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's `status` is `\"success\"` and the result satisfies the role's success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a reachable `commit_sha`).\n- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:\n  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).\n  - The worker itself declared failure: - `plan` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`. - `implement` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`. - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed` AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to `\"error\"`. - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"eval_error\"` → `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain `\"starting\"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad; only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the protocol does not mandate automatic retry. If the orchestrator's policy exhausts retries for this trial (or the operator abandons it), the orchestrator MUST transition the trial's `status` from `\"starting\"` to `\"eval_error\"`, at which point the trial status is terminal.\n\nEvery terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event in the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) and MUST clear the task's `claim` object ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). The orchestrator MAY implement the `claimed → submitted → completed` (or `failed`) sequence as two distinct persisted states or as a single store transaction whose event stream includes both transitions, but subscribers MUST observe both events in order.\n\n### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited\n\nOnce a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its task fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task store chooses to expose outside the normative schema ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a resubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of content equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal transition is no longer valid.\n\n## 5. Reclamation\n\n### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted\n\nA conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from `claimed` back to `pending` — in any of the following cases:\n\n- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed.\n- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.\n- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker is unreachable.\n\nA task in `submitted` state MAY be reclaimed **only** by an explicit operator action; automatic reclaim (expires_at, health policy) MUST NOT apply to `submitted` tasks. After submit the worker's reachability is no longer a relevant liveness signal; the orchestrator is expected to advance the task to a terminal state (§4.3).\n\nA task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.\n\n### 5.2 Effect of reclamation\n\nReclamation:\n\n1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations presenting it MUST be rejected.\n2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.\n3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.\n4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically with the state change.\n\nAny partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is no longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker created store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting trials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are subject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not delete them.\n\n### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation\n\nA worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its task's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its token has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task and MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).\n\n### 5.4 Reclamation effect on role outputs\n\nWhen a reclaimed task had created role-owned objects during its prior execution, the orchestrator MUST reconcile those objects as follows:\n\n- **Implement reclamation.** If the prior execution created a trial with `status == \"starting\"`, the orchestrator MUST transition that trial's `status` to `\"error\"` atomically with the reclaim event. A subsequent re-claim of the implement task produces a **new** trial; starting trials are never shared across implementer attempts.\n- **Plan reclamation.** If the prior execution persisted proposals in `\"drafting\"` state, those proposals remain in `\"drafting\"` and are not dispatched. Implementations MAY expose them to operators for inspection or removal; the task protocol does not mandate automatic cleanup.\n- **Evaluate reclamation.** The trial the task referenced is unchanged by reclamation (it was produced by the implementer, not the evaluator).\n\n## 6. Ordering and concurrency\n\n### 6.1 Per-task serialization\n\nAll transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable history of a task is a total order. A conforming task store MUST NOT expose a state to readers that has not yet been accompanied by its event.\n\n### 6.2 Cross-task concurrency\n\nDistinct tasks MAY progress concurrently. The protocol imposes no global ordering on transitions across tasks beyond the causal order preserved by the event log ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3, [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)).\n\n### 6.3 Observability\n\nA subscriber that reads the event log MUST be able to reconstruct every task's state-machine history exactly. Implementations MAY provide faster paths (direct task reads, push notifications) but MUST NOT let those paths expose states that the event log does not record.\n\n## 7. Proposal and trial lifecycle interactions\n\nThe task state machine is not the only lifecycle in the system. Proposals ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1) and trials ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7) have their own state fields. The task protocol interacts with these lifecycles at the following points:\n\n- **Plan submit.** A successful plan-task submission MAY reference proposals that have been transitioned to `state == \"ready\"` by the planner ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2). The task protocol does not itself transition proposal state; it treats the proposal as a value produced by the planner.\n- **Implement submit.** A successful implement-task submission requires a trial created and advanced by the implementer ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3.2). On any terminal transition of the implement task (whether `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed`), the orchestrator MUST transition the referenced proposal's `state` from `\"dispatched\"` to `\"completed\"`, atomically with the task's terminal event. The proposal's `\"completed\"` state therefore means *\"the implementer's attempt on this proposal has finished\"*, not *\"the attempt succeeded\"*; the trial's `status` field records the outcome. A proposal in `\"dispatched\"` always names a non-terminal implement task; no proposal remains in `\"dispatched\"` after its implement task has reached a terminal state.\n- **Evaluate submit.** A successful evaluate-task submission populates the trial's `metrics`, `completed_at`, and (per worker-declared status) the trial's `status`. The integrator's subsequent promotion of the trial into the canonical lineage is out of this chapter's scope ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).\n\n## 8. Implementation latitude\n\nThe protocol leaves to implementations:\n\n- The mechanism by which a worker discovers claimable tasks (polling, subscription, dispatch).\n- The representation of claim tokens, worker IDs, and task IDs.\n- The timeout and reclamation policy, including whether to set `expires_at` by default.\n- Whether retries are automatic (new task on reclamation) or operator-driven.\n- Whether `submitted → completed` is fully synchronous with submit or a subsequent orchestrator step, as long as the observable state machine above is preserved.\n\nWhat the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:\n\n- The set of states and the permitted transitions among them (§1).\n- The atomicity of claim (§3.1) and the token-authorization rule (§3.3).\n- The idempotency rule for resubmission (§4.2).\n- The requirement that every state change be accompanied by an atomic event (§1.3) and that the event log is the normative observability channel (§6.3).\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module/20260424T232413/0.jsonl:34:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_17","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' reference/services/web-ui/src/eden_web_ui/app.py\"","aggregated_output":"\"\"\"FastAPI application factory for the reference Web UI service.\n\n``make_app`` constructs the ASGI app from the configured store +\nexperiment context. The CLI layer (``cli.py``) builds the deps and\nhands them in; tests construct the app directly with an in-memory\nstore.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom collections.abc import Callable\nfrom datetime import UTC, datetime\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nfrom eden_contracts import ExperimentConfig\nfrom eden_git import GitRepo\nfrom eden_storage import Store\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI, Request\nfrom fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse\nfrom fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles\nfrom fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates\n\nfrom .routes import auth as auth_routes\nfrom .routes import implementer as implementer_routes\nfrom .routes import index as index_routes\nfrom .routes import planner as planner_routes\nfrom .sessions import SessionCodec\n\n_TEMPLATES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / \"templates\"\n_STATIC_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / \"static\"\n\n\ndef _now_factory() -> Callable[[], datetime]:\n    def _now() -> datetime:\n        return datetime.now(UTC)\n\n    return _now\n\n\ndef make_app(\n    *,\n    store: Store,\n    experiment_id: str,\n    experiment_config: ExperimentConfig,\n    worker_id: str,\n    session_secret: str,\n    claim_ttl_seconds: int,\n    artifacts_dir: Path,\n    secure_cookies: bool = False,\n    now: Callable[[], datetime] | None = None,\n    repo: GitRepo | None = None,\n) -> FastAPI:\n    \"\"\"Construct the FastAPI app.\n\n    ``now`` is injected so tests can pin time deterministically; the\n    CLI passes a real wall-clock factory. ``repo`` gates the\n    implementer module: when ``None`` the ``/implementer/*`` routes\n    are not registered and the navigation hides the entry.\n    \"\"\"\n    app = FastAPI(title=\"EDEN reference Web UI\", version=\"0.0.1\")\n    app.mount(\n        \"/static\",\n        StaticFiles(directory=str(_STATIC_DIR)),\n        name=\"static\",\n    )\n    templates = Jinja2Templates(directory=str(_TEMPLATES_DIR))\n    templates.env.globals[\"experiment_id\"] = experiment_id\n    templates.env.globals[\"implementer_enabled\"] = repo is not None\n\n    app.state.store = store\n    app.state.experiment_id = experiment_id\n    app.state.experiment_config = experiment_config\n    app.state.worker_id = worker_id\n    app.state.session_codec = SessionCodec(session_secret)\n    app.state.claim_ttl_seconds = claim_ttl_seconds\n    app.state.artifacts_dir = Path(artifacts_dir)\n    app.state.secure_cookies = secure_cookies\n    app.state.now = now or _now_factory()\n    app.state.templates = templates\n    app.state.repo = repo\n\n    app.include_router(index_routes.router)\n    app.include_router(auth_routes.router)\n    app.include_router(planner_routes.router)\n    if repo is not None:\n        app.include_router(implementer_routes.router)\n\n    @app.exception_handler(404)\n    async def _not_found(request: Request, _exc: object) -> HTMLResponse:\n        return templates.TemplateResponse(\n            request,\n            \"_error.html\",\n            {\"title\": \"Not found\", \"message\": \"The page you requested does not exist.\"},\n            status_code=404,\n        )\n\n    return app\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
