Optionally upload supporting artifacts (logs, captured outputs,\n   diagnostic files).\n\nThe evaluator MUST NOT modify the worker branch or any\nprotocol-owned mutable state other than the trial fields the\nsubmission writes (§4.4) and the task it holds a claim on. In\nparticular, the evaluator MUST NOT write to the trial's\n`completed_at`, `metrics`, `artifacts_uri`, `description`, or\n`status` directly; those writes are performed by the orchestrator\nwhen the submitted task reaches its terminal state (§4.4,\n[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).\n\n### 4.3 Non-interference\n\nThe evaluator reads the repository at the trial's worker-branch tip\nbut MUST NOT push, rewrite, or delete that branch, and MUST NOT\nwrite to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation\n(test caches, build outputs, captured logs) MUST NOT be observable\nthrough any protocol-owned store, ref, or artifact location except\nvia the evaluator's declared outputs (§4.2).\n\n### 4.4 Submission\n\nThe evaluator submits with:\n\n- `trial_id` — the trial it evaluated.\n- `status` — one of:\n  - `\"success\"` — the trial ran and produced metrics.\n  - `\"error\"` — the trial could not be evaluated for reasons\n    attributable to the trial's own code (build failure, test\n    failure, etc.). The evaluator MAY still include partial\n    metrics.\n  - `\"eval_error\"` — the evaluator itself failed for reasons\n    unrelated to the trial's code (infrastructure fault,\n    evaluator bug). While a fresh evaluate task MAY still be\n    created for this trial, the trial's status MUST remain\n    `\"starting\"`. If the orchestrator's retry policy is\n    exhausted (or the operator abandons evaluation), the\n    orchestrator MUST transition the trial's status to\n    `\"eval_error\"`, making that status terminal for the trial\n    ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).\n- `metrics` — the metrics object described in §4.2. MAY be absent\n  when `status == \"eval_error\"`.\n- `artifacts_uri` — OPTIONAL. A URI the evaluator uploaded\n  supporting artifacts to.\n\nOn a `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed` transition\n(per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3), the\norchestrator MUST write the following trial fields atomically with\nthe event:\n\n- `status` — the trial status implied by the submission: `\"success\"`\n  when the submission's `status == \"success\"`; `\"error\"` when the\n  submission's `status == \"error\"`; unchanged from `\"starting\"`\n  when the submission's `status == \"eval_error\"` (see §4.4 above\n  for the terminal-retry case).\n- `metrics` — set to the submission's `metrics` when\n  `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When `status == \"eval_error\"`\n  the orchestrator MUST NOT write `metrics` on the trial; any\n  submission-carried `metrics` is discarded.\n- `artifacts_uri` — set to the submission's `artifacts_uri` when\n  provided and `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When\n  `status == \"eval_error\"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write\n  `artifacts_uri` on the trial; any submission-carried\n  `artifacts_uri` is discarded. (An evaluator that wishes to\n  retain diagnostic artifacts from a failed attempt MAY reference\n  them in the `task.failed` event for that evaluate task; that\n  channel is defined in\n  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md).)\n- `completed_at` — set to the time of the terminal trial\n  transition, i.e. written exactly once, when the trial's status\n  leaves `\"starting\"` (either on a `\"success\"`/`\"error\"`\n  submission, or on the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"`\n  transition). Intermediate `eval_error` submissions MUST NOT\n  advance `completed_at`.\n\nOn the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"` terminal transition itself,\nthe orchestrator MUST NOT graft metrics or artifacts from any\nprior `eval_error` submission onto the trial; the trial's\n`metrics` and `artifacts_uri` fields remain unset. This keeps the\ntrial object canonical: a trial either carries the outputs of a\nsuccessful or code-level-failed evaluation, or it carries nothing.\n\nResubmission is idempotent under the same rules as §3.4: identical\n`trial_id` + `status` + `metrics` + `artifacts_uri` MUST be\naccepted; inconsistent resubmission MUST be rejected.\n\n## 5. Integrator\n\nThe integrator promotes successfully evaluated trials into the\ncanonical trial lineage.\n\nThe integrator's full contract — the squash rule, the eval-manifest\nshape, conflict-resolution policy — is deferred to\n[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md). This section pins only the\nboundary rules that every other role must honor.\n\n### 5.1 Exclusive authority\n\nThe integrator is the **sole writer** of the canonical trial lineage\n(`trial/*` branches, `trial_commit_sha` fields on trials). Every\nother role MUST treat `trial/*` as read-only and MUST NOT set or\nmodify a trial's `trial_commit_sha`.\n\n### 5.2 Inputs\n\nThe integrator observes trials transitioning to `status == \"success\"`\n(as recorded by the evaluator in §4.4). The mechanism of observation\n(event subscription, store poll, dispatch) is a binding concern.\n\n### 5.3 Outputs\n\nThe integrator writes a single commit on a `trial/*` branch for each\npromoted trial and records the resulting SHA in the trial's\n`trial_commit_sha`. Exact topology invariants are in\n[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n\n### 5.4 Why integration is separate from evaluation\n\nEvaluation and integration are separate roles because a successful\nevaluation is a necessary but not sufficient condition for promoting\na trial: the integrator applies experiment-level policy\n(squash shape, conflict resolution with concurrent trials, manifest\nattachment) that is not meaningful to the evaluator. A conforming\nimplementation MAY collapse evaluator and integrator into the same\nprocess, but MUST still honor both contracts independently.\n\n## 6. Role binding (deferred)\n\nHow a role is invoked, addressed, or configured is **not specified\nin v0**. A conforming deployment MAY:\n\n- Run d\nacross both — drift from the Protocol surfaces in tests, not in\nproduction. Restart-safety tests close and reopen a SQLite store\nmid-experiment to confirm state, event log, and claim tokens\nsurvive, and a monkey-patched `_apply_commit` failure verifies\nrollback. The artifact store (§5), `subscribe` streaming (§2.1),\nand Postgres remain non-goals for Phase 6; they land in Phase 10 /\nPhase 8 / later respectively.\n\n**Phase 7b complete.** `eden-git` now also ships the `Integrator`\nthat composes `GitRepo` with a `Store` to promote `success` trials\nper chapter 6. Given a trial with a recorded `commit_sha`,\n`Integrator.integrate` builds the §3.2 single-commit squash\n(worker-tip tree plus the eval manifest at\n`.eden/trials/<trial_id>/eval.json`), writes the\n`refs/heads/trial/<id>-<slug>` ref via zero-oid CAS, and routes the\nstore's atomic `integrate_trial` write for `trial_commit_sha` and\nthe `trial.integrated` event. On store failure the ref is\ncompensatingly deleted per §3.4, matching the post-promotion\nreading recorded in\n[`spec/v0/design-notes/integrator-atomicity.md`](spec/v0/design-notes/integrator-atomicity.md).\nRe-invocation on an already-promoted trial is a verified no-op\n(§5.3): ref SHA, squash tree shape, and manifest bytes are\nre-derived and compared. §2 preconditions (`status == success`,\n`commit_sha` reachable from `branch` tip), §1.4 reachability, and\n§2 metrics validity are all enforced up front; the new public\n`Store.validate_metrics` closes the §2 MUST-NOT-promote clause even\nif upstream orchestrator validation were bypassed. The spec itself\nwas tightened at §3.4 to make the post-promotion reading explicit.\n`eden-dispatch.run_experiment` now takes an `integrate_trial:\nCallable[[str], object]` hook in place of the Phase 5\nplaceholder `integrator_commit_factory` parameter. Eval-manifest\nbytes are deterministic (sorted keys, `indent=2`, trailing newline)\nto make §5.3 idempotency re-derivation stable.\n\n**Phase 7a complete.** `eden-git`'s subprocess wrapper ships\n`GitRepo` covering ref/object inspection (`rev_parse`, `resolve_ref`,\n`list_refs`, `is_ancestor`, `ls_tree`), plumbing (`write_blob`,\n`write_tree_from_entries`, `write_tree_with_file`, `commit_tree`,\n`create_ref`, `update_ref`), worktree management, and branch management.\nAuthor identity and `commit.gpgsign=false` are pinned per-invocation so\nthe user's ambient git config never leaks into integrator commits. See\n[`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) for the full 13-phase plan.\n\n## Commands\n\nAt Phase 8a, markdown linting, JSON Schema validation, and the Python\ntoolchain for the `eden-contracts`, `eden-dispatch`, `eden-git`,\n`eden-storage`, and `eden-wire` reference packages are wired up.\n\n| Command | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `npx --yes markdownlint-cli2@0.14.0 \"**/*.md\" \"#node_modules\" \"#.venv\" \"#docs/archive/**\" \"#docs/plans/review/**\"` | Lint all tracked markdown (pinned to CI's version; matches CI exactly) |\n| `pipx run 'check-jsonschema==0.29.4' --check-metaschema spec/v0/schemas/*.schema.json` | Validate each schema file against the Draft 2020-12 meta-schema (version pinned to CI) |\n| `pipx run 'check-jsonschema==0.29.4' --schemafile spec/v0/schemas/experiment-config.schema.json tests/fixtures/experiment/.eden/config.yaml` | Validate the fixture experiment config against its schema |\n| `uv sync` | Install/refresh the workspace virtualenv (root + `reference/packages/eden-contracts` + `reference/packages/eden-dispatch` + `reference/packages/eden-git` + `reference/packages/eden-storage` + `reference/packages/eden-wire`) |\n| `uv run ruff check .` | Lint Python (config in root `pyproject.toml`) |\n| `uv run pyright` | Type-check the reference Python packages |\n| `uv run pytest -q` | Run the reference-package test suite (includes schema ↔ model parity) |\n| `uv run pytest reference/packages/eden-contracts/tests/test_schema_parity.py` | Run only the schema ↔ Pydantic model parity check |\n| `python3 scripts/spec-xref-check.py` | Validate every `§N.M` reference in `spec/v0/*.md` resolves to a real section heading in its target chapter. Run before committing a normative spec change. |\n\n### Commands that will exist in later phases\n\nThese are listed for orientation; the tooling is not wired up yet.\n\n| Command | Lands in |\n|---|---|\n| `docker compose up` end-to-end | Phase 10 |\n\n## Contribution conventions\n\n### Spec edits\n\n+ Every cross-component contract (wire format, state machine, invariant)\n  is defined in `spec/` first, then implemented in `reference/`. When\n  they disagree, the spec wins and the impl gets a bug.\n+ Spec chapters use RFC 2119 language: MUST, SHOULD, MAY.\n+ Spec versions are frozen once stable (`spec/v0/` is a single lineage;\n  breaking changes go to `spec/v1/`).\n+ Schema changes must update the spec prose, the JSON Schema file, and\n  (from Phase 3 onward) the Pydantic bindings in lockstep. CI will\n  enforce schema ↔ model parity once both exist.\n\n### Reference implementation\n\n+ Normal code-review discipline.\n+ Normative behavior is tested against the conformance suite (once it\n  exists in Phase 11).\n+ The reference impl is explicitly not a monopoly on correctness. A\n  third-party implementation that passes conformance is equally valid.\n\n### Adding or extending a JSON Schema + Pydantic binding\n\nThe `schema-parity` CI job is only as strong as what both sides of the\ntest actually enforce. Several Pydantic and `jsonschema` defaults let\ndrift through silently. When adding a new schema — or a new field type\nto an existing one — evaluate each of the following; reusable\nimplementations live in\n[`reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/_common.py`](reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/_common.py).\n\n+ **Strict numeric parsing.** Top-level models set\n  `ConfigDict(strict=True, extra=\"allow\")`. Non-strict mode coerces\n  `True`/`\"2\"` into int, but the schemas treat `type: integer` /\n  `type: number` as strict JSON types. New models must keep\n  `strict=True`.\n+ **Format assertions on both sides.** `format` keywords (`uri`,\n  `date-time`, …) are advisory by default in both the `jsonschema`\n  library and Pydantic. The schema-side validator wires a custom\n  `FormatChecker` in\n  [`tests/conftest.py`](reference/packages/eden-contracts/tests/conftest.py),\n  and the model side uses the reusable types in `_common.py`. A new\n  `format` keyword in any schema requires handlers on *both* sides;\n  the `test_format_coverage` test fails loudly if the schema-side\n  handler is missing.\n+ **Real date-time / URI validation, not just regex or `urlparse`.**\n  The regex on `DateTimeStr` accepts impossible values like\n  `2026-99-99T…Z`; an `AfterValidator` runs `datetime.fromisoformat`\n  to reject those. `UriStr` uses `rfc3986-validator`, not\n  `urllib.parse.urlparse`, which accepts malformed schemeful URIs\n  (e.g., spaces in the host).\n+ **Null vs absent.** JSON Schema's `type: X` rejects explicit `null`,\n  but Pydantic's `X | None = None` accepts it. Wrap every optional\n  typed field with `NotNone` from `_common.py` so absent is accepted\n  and explicit null is rejected.\n+ **Round-trip emission.** `model.model_dump(mode=\"json\",\n  exclude_none=True)` must re-validate against the schema. Add any\n  new model to `tests/test_roundtrip.py` so this is checked.\n+ **Corpus coverage.** Parity is asserted over the fixture corpus in\n  `tests/cases.py`. A new field type deserves at least one accept\n  fixture and one reject fixture per constraint the schema imposes\n  (required, pattern, enum, min/max, format, cross-field).\n\n## Commit guidelines\n\n+ Short imperative subjects (e.g., \"Add event protocol chapter\",\n  \"Pin task state machine\").\n+ For pull requests, include: what the change is, which phase it\n  advances, and any spec ↔ impl implications.\n\n## Related docs\n\n+ [`docs/naming.md`](docs/naming.md) — what EDEN is, at the concept level.\n+ [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) — the 13-phase build-up plan with\n  unit-level decomposition.\n+ [`docs/plans/`](docs/plans/) — active implementation plans.\n+ [`docs/archive/`](docs/archive/) — historical reference docs.\n+ [`STYLE_GUIDE.md`](STYLE_GUIDE.md) — formatting and naming conventions.\n+ [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](CONTRIBUTING.md) — setup and PR workflow.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-phase-8a-wire-protocol/20260424T115133-impl/0.jsonl:21:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_10","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' spec/v0/07-wire-protocol.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Wire Protocol — HTTP binding\n\nThis chapter specifies an **HTTP binding** for the behaviors\n[chapter 4](04-task-protocol.md) (task state machine),\n[chapter 5](05-event-protocol.md) (event log), and\n[chapter 8](08-storage.md) (storage-side contract) already pin\nnormatively. It does not re-specify those behaviors; every endpoint\nbelow maps 1:1 to a store-side operation named in those chapters and\ninherits that operation's semantics.\n\nA conforming EDEN deployment is **not required** to use this HTTP\nbinding. The protocol constrains observable behavior; the transport\nthat carries it is a deployment choice. This chapter exists so that\nHTTP-based deployments — including the Phase-8a reference\ndeployment — can interoperate byte-for-byte.\n\nThe binding intentionally omits the reference orchestrator's\ndecision helpers (`validate_terminal`, `validate_metrics`). Those\nare reference-impl conveniences, not chapter-4/5/8 operations. A\nconforming third-party orchestrator is free to implement their\ndecision logic inline; see §9.\n\n## 1. Transport\n\n### 1.1 HTTP version and content type\n\nA conforming server MUST accept HTTP/1.1 over TCP on a\ndeployment-chosen host and port. HTTP/2 is OPTIONAL. Requests and\nresponses that carry a body MUST use media type\n`application/json; charset=utf-8`, except for error responses which\nuse `application/problem+json; charset=utf-8` (§6). Empty responses\n(e.g. 204) MUST NOT carry a body.\n\n### 1.2 Path versioning\n\nAll normative paths are rooted at `/v0/`. Paths under\n`/_reference/` are explicitly non-normative and carry\nreference-implementation-specific behavior; a conforming client\nMUST NOT rely on them being present or on their semantics.\n\nBreaking changes to the binding produce a new root (`/v1/`), the\nsame way breaking changes to the spec produce a new directory\n(`spec/v1/`). Non-breaking additions (new endpoints, new optional\nfields in existing payloads) stay under `/v0/`.\n\n### 1.3 Experiment scoping\n\nEvery normative path segment below `/v0/` begins with\n`experiments/{experiment_id}/`, and every request MUST additionally\nsend the header `X-Eden-Experiment-Id: {experiment_id}` with a\nvalue equal to the path segment. A server MUST reject a request\nwhose header disagrees with its path segment with\n`eden://error/experiment-id-mismatch` (HTTP 400); see §6. The\nheader is defense-in-depth against misrouted clients and proxies.\n\n## 2. Task operations (chapter 4)\n\nThe following endpoints bind the task-store operations in\n[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §§1–5 and\n[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.1. Each endpoint inherits the\nnormative semantics of the underlying operation; this section lists\nonly the wire shape.\n\n| Operation | HTTP | Path |\n|---|---|---|\n| `create_task` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` |\n| `list_tasks` | `GET`  | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` |\n| `read_task` | `GET`  | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}` |\n| `read_submission` | `GET`  | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submission` |\n| `claim` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/claim` |\n| `submit` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submit` |\n| `accept` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/accept` |\n| `reject` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reject` |\n| `reclaim` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reclaim` |\n\n### 2.1 Create\n\n`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` accepts a JSON request body whose\nshape matches\n[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json) with\n`state == \"pending\"` and no `claim`. On success the server returns\n200 with the created task as the response body (same schema). The\ncomposite-commit rules in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)\n§2.2 apply: creating an `implement` task transitions the referenced\nproposal atomically with the task insert; the server MUST perform\nboth effects in a single transaction.\n\n### 2.2 List and read\n\n- `GET /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` returns an array of tasks\n  matching optional query parameters `kind` (one of `plan`,\n  `implement`, `evaluate`) and `state` (one of the five task\n  states). Ordering is implementation-defined per\n  [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.1.\n- `GET /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}` returns the task object with\n  id `T`, or 404 `eden://error/not-found`.\n\n### 2.3 Claim\n\n`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/claim` accepts a JSON body with\nat minimum the field `worker_id` and an OPTIONAL `expires_at`\ntimestamp. On success the server returns 200 with a `claim` object\nmatching the shape from [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4\n(see also [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json)) —\nnotably including the fresh `token`. On a non-`pending` task the\nserver returns 409 `eden://error/illegal-transition`. The\nclaim-token secrecy rule ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md)\n§3.2) implies that intermediary proxies and logs MUST NOT persist\nclaim tokens in plaintext; that is a deployment concern and the\nbinding does not enforce it.\n\n### 2.4 Submit\n\n`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submit` accepts a JSON body\ncontaining the current `token` and a role-specific `payload`\n(matching the claim's task `kind`). Atomicity, idempotency, and\ncontent-equivalence rules from [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md)\n§4 apply unchanged. A resubmit with a content-equivalent payload\nMUST return 200; a resubmit with a divergent payload MUST return\n409 `eden://error/conflicting-resubmission`. A wrong token MUST\nreturn 403 `eden://error/wrong-token`.\n\n### 2.5 Accept and reject\n\n- `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/accept` transitions\n  `submitted → completed` per\n  [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3. The request\n  body is empty (the orchestrator has already persisted the\n  submission; the server re-reads it to apply the composite-commit\n  effects).\n- `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reject` transitions\n  `submitted → failed` per\n  [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3. The request body carries a single\n  field `reason` drawn from the closed v0 vocabulary\n  ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §3.1):\n  `\"worker_error\"`, `\"validation_error\"`, or `\"policy_limit\"`.\n\n### 2.6 Reclaim\n\n`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reclaim` transitions a\n`claimed` or (operator-invoked) `submitted` task back to `pending`\nper [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5. The request\nbody carries a single field `cause` drawn from the closed v0\nvocabulary: `\"expired\"`, `\"operator\"`, or `\"health_policy\"`.\n\n## 3. Proposal operations\n\n| Operation | HTTP | Path |\n|---|---|---|\n| `create_proposal` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals` |\n| `list_proposals` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals` |\n| `read_proposal` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals/{P}` |\n| `mark_proposal_ready` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals/{P}/mark-ready` |\n\nRequest and response bodies match\n[`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json).\n`list_proposals` accepts an optional `state` query parameter.\n`mark-ready` has an empty request body.\n\n## 4. Trial operations\n\n| Operation | HTTP | Path |\n|---|---|---|\n| `create_trial` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials` |\n| `list_trials` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials` |\n| `read_trial` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}` |\n| `declare_trial_eval_error` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/declare-eval-error` |\n| `integrate_trial` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/integrate` |\n\n`integrate_trial` binds [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4\nand carries additional idempotency rules (§5 below); the other\nendpoints are transport-only bindings of their\n[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.7 operations.\n\n## 5. `integrate_trial` — same-value idempotency\n\nThe `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/integrate` endpoint has\nadditional semantics beyond transport-binding because\n[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4's atomicity invariant\nspans the process boundary. The request body carries the single\nfield `trial_commit_sha`.\n\n- **Success (first call).** The server atomically writes\n  `trial_commit_sha` on the trial and appends\n  `trial.integrated` per [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)\n  §3.4. Response: 200, empty body.\n- **Same-value idempotency.** A repeated call whose\n  `trial_commit_sha` equals the value already stored on the trial\n  MUST return 200 and MUST NOT append a second\n  `trial.integrated` event. This is what lets a client safely\n  retry an `integrate_trial` request after a transport-\n  indeterminate failure without double-commit.\n- **Different-value rejection.** A call whose `trial_commit_sha`\n  differs from the value already stored MUST return 409\n  `eden://error/invalid-precondition`. A conforming client MUST\n  surface this as an atomicity violation (the chapter 6 §1.2\n  sole-writer rule has been violated somewhere upstream);\n  operator intervention is required.\n- **Other preconditions.** Standard\n  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4 preconditions\n  continue to apply: the trial MUST be in `status == \"success\"`,\n  and the `trial_commit_sha` MUST be a well-formed commit SHA;\n  violations return `invalid-precondition`.\n\nA client that issues `integrate_trial` and does not receive a\n2xx response (connection reset, read timeout, proxy disconnect)\nMUST treat the outcome as indeterminate and MUST NOT assume the\nserver has not committed. The reference reconciliation procedure\nis a read-back (`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}`) that resolves\nto one of three outcomes:\n\n- observed `trial_commit_sha` equals the sent value → treat as\n  success;\n- observed `trial_commit_sha` is set but differs → raise the\n  same atomicity-violation error the server would have;\n- observed `trial_commit_sha` is absent, or the read-back itself\n  fails → raise an indeterminate-result error. Absence does **not**\n  prove the request failed; it may still be in flight.\n\n## 6. Event log\n\n| Operation | HTTP | Path |\n|---|---|---|\n| `read_range` / `replay` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/events` |\n| `subscribe` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/events/subscribe` |\n\n### 6.1 Read-range and replay\n\n`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/events` returns the log in order. It\naccepts one query parameter:\n\n- `cursor` — cumulative count of events the caller has already\n  observed. Omitted or `0` triggers `replay` per\n  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.4.\n\nThe response is `{\"events\": [...], \"cursor\": N}` where\n`events` is the delivered batch (possibly empty) and `cursor` is\nthe new cumulative count the caller should pass to the next call.\nThe endpoint does not block; it returns whatever is available\nimmediately.\n\n### 6.2 Long-poll subscribe\n\n`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/events/subscribe` binds the chapter 8 §2.1\n`subscribe` operation as an HTTP long-poll. It accepts the same\n`cursor` query parameter. The server holds the connection open\nuntil one or more events are available after `cursor`, then\nreturns them. If no event arrives within a server-chosen timeout\n(RECOMMENDED: 30 seconds), the server returns an empty batch so\nthe caller can reconnect. The response body shape is identical to\n§6.1.\n\nSubscribers iterate this endpoint in a loop, advancing `cursor` by\nthe length of each returned batch. The at-least-once and total-\norder guarantees from [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)\n§4 are preserved because the underlying `read_range` semantics\npreserve them.\n\nPush transports (Server-Sent Events, WebSocket) are OPTIONAL\nalternatives a deployment MAY expose alongside long-poll. They do\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Task store\n    25\t\n    26\tThe task store holds the set of tasks and their current state for an\n    27\texperiment. It MUST support the operations below, each with the\n    28\tlisted atomicity and failure semantics. Wire-level transport is a\n    29\tbinding concern and is not pinned here.\n    30\t\n    31\t### 1.1 Operations\n    32\t\n    33\t| Operation | Arguments | Effect |\n    34\t|---|---|---|\n    35\t| `create_task` | fully-formed `task` object with `state == \"pending\"` | Atomically inserts the task and appends `task.created` to the event log ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)). |\n    36\t| `claim` | `task_id`, `worker_id`, OPTIONAL `expires_at` | Atomically transitions a `pending` task to `claimed`, issues a fresh claim token, and appends `task.claimed`. |\n    37\t| `submit` | `task_id`, `token`, role-specific result | Atomically transitions a `claimed` task to `submitted`, persists the result, and appends `task.submitted`. Idempotent per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2. |\n    38\t| `accept` | `task_id`, role-specific acceptance payload | Atomically transitions `submitted → completed`, applies the role-specific effect (e.g. proposal completion, trial status write), clears `claim`, and appends `task.completed` plus any composite events. |\n    39\t| `reject` | `task_id`, `reason`, role-specific failure payload | Atomically transitions `submitted → failed`, applies the role-specific effect, clears `claim`, and appends `task.failed` plus any composite events. |\n    40\t| `reclaim` | `task_id`, OPTIONAL `cause` | Atomically transitions `claimed`/`submitted` back to `pending` (where permitted; [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5.1), invalidates the token, clears `claim`, performs §2.2 composite effects where applicable, and appends `task.reclaimed`. |\n    41\t| `read_task` | `task_id` | Returns the current task object or a well-defined not-found signal. |\n    42\t| `list_tasks` | filter (`kind`, `state`, `experiment_id`, …) | Returns matching tasks. Ordering is implementation-defined. |\n    43\t\n    44\t\"Atomically\" means: the state change, the result-payload write, any\n    45\tcross-entity effect listed in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)\n    46\t§2.2, and the event appends are all durable together or none of them\n    47\tis ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)).\n    48\t\n    49\t### 1.2 Atomic claim (serialization)\n    50\t\n    51\tFor any given `pending` task, at most one concurrent `claim`\n    52\tinvocation MUST succeed. A conforming task store MUST expose this as\n    53\ta linearizable operation: after a successful claim, every subsequent\n    54\treader of the task observes `state == \"claimed\"` with the new claim\n    55\tobject; no reader observes an intermediate state without the claim.\n    56\t\n    57\tThe serialization mechanism (row lock + update, compare-and-set on\n    58\tversion, `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) is implementation-defined.\n    59\tThe observable guarantee is not.\n    60\t\n    61\t### 1.3 Token-authorization rule\n    62\t\n    63\t`submit` and any other claim-holder operation MUST reject calls whose\n    64\tpresented token does not equal the token currently recorded on the\n    65\ttask's `claim` object\n    66\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.3). A conforming\n    67\ttask store MUST NOT use `worker_id` as a substitute for the token\n    68\twhen authorizing.\n    69\t\n    70\t### 1.4 Idempotent resubmit\n    71\t\n    72\tThe idempotency rule for `submit` is specified normatively in\n    73\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2. A conforming task\n    74\tstore MUST implement it: a resubmit carrying the current token, with\n    75\ta content-equivalent payload, MUST succeed without mutating state;\n    76\tan inconsistent resubmit MUST be rejected.\n    77\t\n    78\t### 1.5 Terminal immutability\n    79\t\n    80\tOnce a task is `completed` or `failed`\n    81\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.4), no subsequent\n    82\toperation MAY change any task field except audit-only metadata the\n    83\ttask store documents outside the normative schema. In particular,\n    84\t`reclaim` MUST be rejected on terminal tasks.\n    85\t\n    86\t### 1.6 Per-task serialization\n    87\t\n    88\tAll mutating operations on a single task MUST be serialized: the\n    89\tobservable history of a task is a total order\n    90\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §6.1). A conforming\n    91\ttask store MUST NOT expose a state that has not yet been accompanied\n    92\tby its event ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)).\n    93\t\n    94\t### 1.7 Proposal and trial persistence\n    95\t\n    96\tProposals ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5) and trials\n    97\t(§7) are persisted alongside tasks. A conforming deployment MAY\n    98\tback them with the task store itself, with a separate store, or\n    99\twith any combination; the protocol constrains the observable\n   100\tcontract, not the physical layout. Every conforming deployment MUST\n   101\texpose, for both proposals and trials, at minimum:\n   102\t\n   103\t- **Create** — durably insert a new object whose fields validate\n   104\t  against the corresponding schema\n   105\t  ([`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json),\n   106\t  [`schemas/trial.schema.json`](schemas/trial.schema.json)).\n   107\t- **Read** — return the current object or a well-defined not-found\n   108\t  signal.\n   109\t- **List** — return objects matching a filter (by `experiment_id`,\n   110\t  `state`/`status`, etc.). Ordering is implementation-defined.\n   111\t- **Update** — apply the field writes the role contracts and task-\n   112\t  terminal transitions specify ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2,\n   113\t  §3.2, §4.4; [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3,\n   114\t  §5.4).\n   115\t\n   116\tAll proposal and trial writes MUST commit atomically with the\n   117\taccompanying event(s) per §6. The durability (§3.1), read-after-write\n   118\t(§3.2), crash-recovery (§3.3), and no-fabrication (§3.4) rules apply\n   119\tuniformly. Terminal immutability applies to proposals in\n   120\t`completed` and trials in `success`/`error`/`eval_error` — the\n   121\t`trial_commit_sha` field is the **one** post-terminal write\n   122\tpermitted on a trial, written exclusively by the integrator\n   123\t([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4), and it MUST be\n   124\twritten atomically with its event and its `trial/*` ref.\n   125\t\n   126\t## 2. Event log\n   127\t\n   128\tThe event log holds the per-experiment event stream. It MUST support\n   129\tthe operations below and MUST honor the delivery guarantees specified\n   130\tin [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.\n   131\t\n   132\t### 2.1 Operations\n   133\t\n   134\t| Operation | Arguments | Effect |\n   135\t|---|---|---|\n   136\t| `append` | fully-formed event object | Atomically appends the event, together with the state change it describes ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)). |\n   137\t| `read_range` | experiment_id, OPTIONAL cursor | Returns a durable, ordered range of events. |\n   138\t| `subscribe` | experiment_id, OPTIONAL starting cursor | Returns a stream that delivers events in log order until cancelled. |\n   139\t| `replay` | experiment_id | Returns the full stream from the first event ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.4). |\n   140\t\n   141\t`append` MUST NOT be exposed as a standalone operation that the task\n   142\tstore, proposal store, or trial store can call independently of the\n   143\tstate change the event describes. The atomicity contract\n   144\t([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)) requires the append to travel\n   145\twith the state change; the event log operation is a primitive of the\n   146\tcomposite transaction, not an independent one.\n   147\t\n   148\t### 2.2 Ordering and causality\n   149\t\n   150\tThe log MUST present a single total order per experiment\n   151\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.1) that respects\n   152\tcausality ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.2). The\n   153\tordering mechanism is implementation-defined; the observable\n   154\tguarantee is not.\n   155\t\n   156\tThe log MAY provide a global ordering across experiments as an\n   157\toptimization, but MUST NOT require subscribers to depend on it\n   158\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.6).\n   159\t\n   160\t### 2.3 Delivery\n   161\t\n   162\tAt-least-once delivery is the minimum contract\n   163\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.3).\n   164\tImplementations MAY offer exactly-once via durable offset commits.\n   165\t\n   166\t### 2.4 Retention\n   167\t\n   168\tFor the lifetime of an experiment — from registration until the\n   169\texperiment reaches its operator-declared terminal state — a\n   170\tconforming event log MUST retain **every** appended event for that\n   171\texperiment and MUST serve full replay from the first event\n   172\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.4). No\n   173\tcompaction, archival, or deletion MAY occur inside this window. A\n   174\tdeployment MAY compact or archive an experiment's events once it\n   175\thas reached its operator-declared terminal state; after compaction\n   176\tthe log MUST either (a) reject replays older than the compaction\n   177\thorizon with a well-defined error, or (b) serve those replays from\n   178\tan archive that preserves order and content. It MUST NOT silently\n   179\tdrop events from a replay.\n   180\t\n   181\t## 3. Durability\n   182\t\n   183\tThe following applies uniformly to the task store and the event log.\n   184\t\n   185\t### 3.1 Write durability\n   186\t\n   187\tAn operation that the store acknowledges to its caller MUST survive\n   188\ta subsequent crash of the store's host. \"Acknowledgement\" is the\n   189\treturn value indicating success; a store MUST NOT return success for\n   190\ta write that has not yet been persisted to durable media (or the\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Creating a task\n    79\t\n    80\tA task enters the system in the `pending` state. A conforming\n    81\torchestrator MUST:\n    82\t\n    83\t- Populate `task_id`, `kind`, `state = \"pending\"`, `payload`,\n    84\t  `created_at`, `updated_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md)\n    85\t  §3.1).\n    86\t- Ensure the payload satisfies the dispatch preconditions for the\n    87\t  task's `kind`:\n    88\t  - `plan` — `payload.experiment_id` names an experiment the\n    89\t    planner is authorized to work on.\n    90\t  - `implement` — `payload.proposal_id` names a proposal with\n    91\t    `state == \"ready\"` at creation time. Creating the implement\n    92\t    task and transitioning the proposal from `\"ready\"` to\n    93\t    `\"dispatched\"` MUST be atomic; a proposal's `\"dispatched\"`\n    94\t    state signals that exactly one implement task has been\n    95\t    created for it.\n    96\t  - `evaluate` — `payload.trial_id` names a trial with\n    97\t    `status == \"starting\"` and `commit_sha` set at creation time.\n    98\t- Emit the corresponding `task.created` event (\n    99\t  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) atomically with\n   100\t  the task insert.\n   101\t\n   102\tA worker MAY rely on these preconditions ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md)\n   103\t§1.1). A task created without them is a protocol violation by the\n   104\torchestrator.\n   105\t\n   106\t## 3. Claim\n   107\t\n   108\t### 3.1 Atomicity\n   109\t\n   110\tThe `pending → claimed` transition MUST be atomic with respect to\n   111\tcompeting claim attempts: at most one worker MAY succeed for any\n   112\tgiven task. A conforming task store MUST provide this guarantee as\n   113\tpart of its durability contract\n   114\t([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).\n   115\t\n   116\t### 3.2 Claim token\n   117\t\n   118\tA successful claim issues a `claim` object\n   119\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4) containing:\n   120\t\n   121\t- `token` — an opaque value that MUST be:\n   122\t  - **unique** — no two distinct claims (on the same or different\n   123\t    tasks) share the same token within an experiment;\n   124\t  - **unforgeable** — a worker that did not receive the token MUST\n   125\t    NOT be able to construct a byte-equal value with practical\n   126\t    effort. Implementations commonly use a cryptographically\n   127\t    random value; the protocol does not mandate the mechanism.\n   128\t- `worker_id` — an identifier the worker supplies at claim time.\n   129\t  The task store MAY record it for audit but MUST NOT use it as a\n   130\t  substitute for the token when authorizing subsequent operations.\n   131\t- `claimed_at` — the time the claim was issued.\n   132\t- `expires_at` — OPTIONAL. If present, the task store MAY reclaim\n   133\t  the task when the current time exceeds this value (§5).\n   134\t\n   135\t### 3.3 Authorization by token\n   136\t\n   137\tEvery subsequent operation on a claimed task (progress update,\n   138\tsubmit, release) MUST present the current claim token. The task\n   139\tstore MUST reject any such operation whose token does not match the\n   140\ttoken currently recorded on the task. This is the sole mechanism\n   141\tthe protocol relies on to guarantee single-writer access to a\n   142\tclaimed task.\n   143\t\n   144\t### 3.4 No re-claim while claimed\n   145\t\n   146\tA conforming task store MUST reject a claim attempt against a task\n   147\twhose `state` is not `pending`. In particular, a claimed task\n   148\tcannot be \"re-claimed\" by a different worker; the prior claim must\n   149\tfirst be released (by submission) or invalidated (by reclamation).\n   150\t\n   151\t## 4. Submit\n   152\t\n   153\t### 4.1 Submission operation\n   154\t\n   155\tA submit operation requires:\n   156\t\n   157\t- The `task_id`.\n   158\t- The current claim `token`.\n   159\t- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind`\n   160\t  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).\n   161\t\n   162\tA successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` atomically\n   163\twith persisting the result payload. The terminal transition to\n   164\t`completed` or `failed` is the orchestrator's subsequent step\n   165\t(§4.3), even when the submission itself declared failure.\n   166\t\n   167\t### 4.2 Idempotency\n   168\t\n   169\tA resubmission is a second submit carrying the token recorded on\n   170\tthe task's `claim` object (which is retained through `submitted`\n   171\tper [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). A resubmit against\n   172\ta task whose claim has been cleared — because the task was\n   173\treclaimed or reached a terminal state — MUST be rejected\n   174\tregardless of the presented token (§4.4, §5.2).\n   175\t\n   176\tWhen the token matches, the task store MUST handle the\n   177\tresubmission as follows:\n   178\t\n   179\t- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to\n   180\t  the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and\n   181\t  MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. \"Content\n   182\t  equivalence\" means the normative fields identified per role\n   183\t  agree:\n   184\t  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (compared as sets; order\n   185\t    is not significant per [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4) and\n   186\t    `status`.\n   187\t  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha`\n   188\t    (when present).\n   189\t  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as\n   190\t    JSON values; key order does not matter).\n   191\t- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent,\n   192\t  the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is\n   193\t  the committed result.\n   194\t\n   195\tThis rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a\n   196\tnetwork or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or\n   197\tcorrupting the recorded result.\n   198\t\n   199\t### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal\n   200\t\n   201\tA submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions\n   202\tto a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:\n   203\t\n   204\t- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's\n   205\t  `status` is `\"success\"` and the result satisfies the role's\n   206\t  success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a\n   207\t  reachable `commit_sha`).\n   208\t- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:\n   209\t  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed\n   210\t    payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).\n   211\t  - The worker itself declared failure:\n   212\t    - `plan` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`.\n   213\t    - `implement` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`.\n   214\t    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`\n   215\t      AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to\n   216\t      `\"error\"`.\n   217\t    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"eval_error\"` →\n   218\t      `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain\n   219\t      `\"starting\"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad;\n   220\t      only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh\n   221\t      `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the\n   222\t      protocol does not mandate automatic retry. If the\n   223\t      orchestrator's policy exhausts retries for this trial\n   224\t      (or the operator abandons it), the orchestrator MUST\n   225\t      transition the trial's `status` from `\"starting\"` to\n   226\t      `\"eval_error\"`, at which point the trial status is\n   227\t      terminal.\n   228\t\n   229\tEvery terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event\n   230\tin the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) and\n   231\tMUST clear the task's `claim` object\n   232\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). The orchestrator MAY\n   233\timplement the `claimed → submitted → completed` (or `failed`)\n   234\tsequence as two distinct persisted states or as a single store\n   235\ttransaction whose event stream includes both transitions, but\n   236\tsubscribers MUST observe both events in order.\n   237\t\n   238\t### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited\n   239\t\n   240\tOnce a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its\n   241\ttask fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task\n   242\tstore chooses to expose outside the normative schema\n   243\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a\n   244\tresubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of\n   245\tcontent equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal\n   246\ttransition is no longer valid.\n   247\t\n   248\t## 5. Reclamation\n   249\t\n   250\t### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted\n   251\t\n   252\tA conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from\n   253\t`claimed` back to `pending` — in any of the following cases:\n   254\t\n   255\t- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed.\n   256\t- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.\n   257\t- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker\n   258\t  is unreachable.\n   259\t\n   260\tA task in `submitted` state MAY be reclaimed **only** by an\n   261\texplicit operator action; automatic reclaim (expires_at, health\n   262\tpolicy) MUST NOT apply to `submitted` tasks. After submit the\n   263\tworker's reachability is no longer a relevant liveness signal; the\n   264\torchestrator is expected to advance the task to a terminal state\n   265\t(§4.3).\n   266\t\n   267\tA task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.\n   268\t\n   269\t### 5.2 Effect of reclamation\n   270\t\n   271\tReclamation:\n   272\t\n   273\t1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations\n   274\t   presenting it MUST be rejected.\n   275\t2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.\n   276\t3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.\n   277\t4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log\n   278\t   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically\n   279\t   with the state change.\n   280\t\n   281\tAny partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is\n   282\tno longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker\n   283\tcreated store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting\n   284\ttrials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are\n   285\tsubject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not\n   286\tdelete them.\n   287\t\n   288\t### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation\n   289\t\n   290\tA worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its\n   291\ttask's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its\n   292\ttoken has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task\n   293\tand MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).\n   294\t\n   295\t### 5.4 Reclamation effect on role outputs\n   296\t\n   297\tWhen a reclaimed task had created role-owned objects during its\n   298\tprior execution, the orchestrator MUST reconcile those objects as\n   299\tfollows:\n   300\t\n   301\t- **Implement reclamation.** If the prior execution created a\n   302\t  trial with `status == \"starting\"`, the orchestrator MUST\n   303\t  transition that trial's `status` to `\"error\"` atomically with\n   304\t  the reclaim event. A subsequent re-claim of the implement task\n   305\t  produces a **new** trial; starting trials are never shared\n   306\t  across implementer attempts.\n   307\t- **Plan reclamation.** If the prior execution persisted\n   308\t  proposals in `\"drafting\"` state, those proposals remain in\n   309\t  `\"drafting\"` and are not dispatched. Implementations MAY expose\n   310\t  them to operators for inspection or removal; the task protocol\n   311\t  does not mandate automatic cleanup.\n   312\t- **Evaluate reclamation.** The trial the task referenced is\n   313\t  unchanged by reclamation (it was produced by the implementer,\n   314\t  not the evaluator).\n   315\t\n   316\t## 6. Ordering and concurrency\n   317\t\n   318\t### 6.1 Per-task serialization\n   319\t\n   320\tAll transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Writing the `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial to the SHA of\n   197\t   the new `trial/*` commit\n   198\t   ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n   199\t2. Appending a `trial.integrated` event to the event log\n   200\t   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §3.3).\n   201\t\n   202\tIf any of the three steps fails — git ref write, trial-object\n   203\tfield write, event append — the integrator MUST roll back any\n   204\talready-performed step. In particular, a dangling `trial/*` ref with\n   205\tno `trial_commit_sha` field and no `trial.integrated` event is a\n   206\tprotocol violation. Implementations MAY use store-level\n   207\ttransactions, outbox patterns, compensating deletes, or any other\n   208\tmechanism; the observable invariant is that a reader of any one of\n   209\tthe three artifacts (ref, field, event) MUST observe the other two.\n   210\t\n   211\tThis invariant applies to completed promotions: once a promotion has\n   212\treturned — whether by success or by rollback — the three artifacts\n   213\tMUST reconcile, with all three present on success and none present on\n   214\trollback. A promotion that is still running MAY transiently expose\n   215\tintermediate states to an external reader (for example, a `trial/*`\n   216\tref written before its compensating delete, or a store-side field\n   217\twritten before the git ref). The compensating-delete mechanism named\n   218\tabove creates such states by construction; zero-width multi-artifact\n   219\tconsistency during a running promotion is not required, and conforming\n   220\timplementations MAY rely on compensating deletes rather than two-phase\n   221\tcommit or outbox patterns.\n   222\t\n   223\tSee [`design-notes/integrator-atomicity.md`](design-notes/integrator-atomicity.md)\n   224\tfor the rationale behind this reading, the alternatives considered,\n   225\tand the conditions under which a future revision of this chapter\n   226\tmight tighten the invariant.\n   227\t\n   228\t## 4. Eval manifest\n   229\t\n   230\tEach `trial/*` commit MUST carry an **eval manifest** at a fixed path\n   231\tinside its tree. The manifest is the canonical, git-embedded record\n   232\tof the trial's evaluator outputs: what was measured, which worker\n   233\tcommit was measured, where the supporting artifacts live.\n   234\t\n   235\t### 4.1 Path\n   236\t\n   237\tThe eval manifest is a JSON file at:\n   238\t\n   239\t```text\n   240\t.eden/trials/<trial_id>/eval.json\n   241\t```\n   242\t\n   243\trelative to the root of the committed tree. The `<trial_id>`\n   244\tcomponent matches the trial's `trial_id`. An integrator MUST NOT add\n   245\tany file under `.eden/trials/<other_trial_id>/` to a trial's commit;\n   246\teach `trial/*` commit carries exactly one eval-manifest path,\n   247\tcorresponding to its own trial.\n   248\t\n   249\t### 4.2 Shape\n   250\t\n   251\tThe manifest is a JSON object with the following required fields.\n   252\tEach required field is taken from state the protocol already writes\n   253\tvia the role contracts in [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) — no role\n   254\tsubmission is extended by this chapter.\n   255\t\n   256\t| Field | Type | Source |\n   257\t|---|---|---|\n   258\t| `trial_id` | string | The trial's `trial_id` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1). |\n   259\t| `proposal_id` | string | The trial's `proposal_id` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1). |\n   260\t| `commit_sha` | string | The worker-branch tip the evaluator measured ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1). |\n   261\t| `parent_commits` | array of string | The trial's `parent_commits`, in order ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1). |\n   262\t| `metrics` | object | The evaluator's metrics payload ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.4), conforming to the experiment's `metrics_schema`. |\n   263\t| `completed_at` | timestamp | The trial's `completed_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1). UTC, RFC 3339 profile as elsewhere in the data model. |\n   264\t\n   265\tOptional fields:\n   266\t\n   267\t| Field | Type | Source |\n   268\t|---|---|---|\n   269\t| `artifacts_uri` | string (URI) | The trial's `artifacts_uri` if the evaluator supplied one ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.4). |\n   270\t| `description` | string | The trial's `description`, copied from the trial object if present. |\n   271\t| `evaluator` | object | Implementation-defined identity of the evaluator (§4.3). Present when the evaluator's role-binding surfaces this metadata. |\n   272\t| `artifacts` | object | Implementation-defined inventory of per-file artifact metadata (§4.4). Present when the role-binding surfaces it. |\n   273\t\n   274\tEvery required field's value MUST equal the corresponding field on\n   275\tthe trial object at the moment of promotion. A conforming integrator\n   276\tMUST NOT synthesize or transform required-field values; they are a\n   277\tcanonical, in-tree snapshot of the trial state, not a separate\n   278\tderivation.\n   279\t\n   280\tImplementations MAY include additional informational fields beyond\n   281\tthose listed; consumers MUST tolerate them.\n   282\t\n   283\t### 4.3 `evaluator` shape (optional)\n   284\t\n   285\tWhen present, the `evaluator` object has the form:\n   286\t\n   287\t```json\n   288\t{\n   289\t  \"name\": \"<implementation-chosen identifier for the evaluator>\",\n   290\t  \"version\": \"<version string the evaluator presented at submission>\"\n   291\t}\n   292\t```\n   293\t\n   294\tBoth fields are required when the `evaluator` object itself is\n   295\tpresent. `name` and `version` together identify *which* evaluator\n   296\tmeasured the trial. Because v0 does not surface evaluator identity\n   297\tthrough [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.4, this field is optional:\n   298\tpopulate it when a role-binding or deployment convention carries\n   299\tevaluator metadata; omit it otherwise.\n   300\t\n   301\t### 4.4 `artifacts` shape (optional)\n   302\t\n   303\tWhen present, `artifacts` is a JSON object whose values describe\n   304\tper-file metadata for individual artifacts referenced from the trial\n   305\t(commonly the files under the evaluator's `artifacts_uri`). Each\n   306\tentry has the form:\n   307\t\n   308\t```json\n   309\t{\n   310\t  \"uri\": \"<URI of the specific file>\",\n   311\t  \"sha256\": \"<hex sha256 of the file's byte content>\",\n   312\t  \"bytes\": <non-negative integer>\n   313\t}\n   314\t```\n   315\t\n   316\tKeys are operator-chosen short labels (e.g. `\"log\"`, `\"output.csv\"`)\n   317\tand MUST be unique within the manifest. This inventory is **optional\n   318\tin v0** because [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.4 does not require\n   319\tan evaluator to enumerate per-file hashes; a deployment whose role-\n   320\tbinding surfaces that inventory MAY populate this field, and\n   321\tdownstream consumers SHOULD prefer the inventory (when present) to\n   322\tblind re-fetches of `artifacts_uri` for reproducibility checks.\n   323\t\n   324\t### 4.5 Manifest immutability\n   325\t\n   326\tBecause the eval manifest lives inside the `trial/*` commit's tree,\n   327\tit inherits the immutability of the `trial/*` branch (§1.2): once\n   328\twritten, it MUST NOT be rewritten. A conforming implementation that\n   329\tdiscovers a manifest error after the fact MAY record corrective\n   330\tstate outside git (operator-level event, separate annotation store)\n   331\tbut MUST NOT mutate the `trial/*` commit.\n   332\t\n   333\t## 5. Failure modes\n   334\t\n   335\t### 5.1 Metrics-schema violation at promotion time\n   336\t\n   337\tIf the integrator re-validates metrics (per §2) and finds them in\n   338\tviolation of the experiment's `metrics_schema`, the integrator MUST\n   339\tNOT produce a `trial/*` commit, MUST NOT write `trial_commit_sha`,\n   340\tand MUST NOT append `trial.integrated`. It MUST surface the problem\n   341\tthrough an implementation-defined operator channel; since the trial's\n   342\t`status` was written to `success` by the orchestrator at the evaluate\n   343\tterminal, the promotion-side rejection is a protocol-level drift\n   344\tbetween the two that an operator MUST resolve. A conforming\n   345\torchestrator makes this case rare by enforcing schema conformance at\n   346\tthe evaluate terminal.\n   347\t\n   348\t### 5.2 Partial git write\n   349\t\n   350\tIf the underlying git ref write appears to succeed but later fails\n   351\tdurability (crashed filesystem, partial replication), the\n   352\tintegrator's atomicity contract (§3.4) is violated. Implementations\n   353\tMUST ensure ref durability before considering a promotion complete;\n   354\tconforming storage requirements are pinned in\n   355\t[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §5 (artifact store) and §3\n   356\t(durability semantics applied at the git layer by the integrator).\n   357\t\n   358\t### 5.3 Repeat promotion\n   359\t\n   360\tIf the integrator is invoked twice for the same trial (replay of a\n   361\t`trial.succeeded` event, operator retry), it MUST be idempotent:\n   362\t\n   363\t- If the trial already has `trial_commit_sha` set and that SHA\n   364\t  resolves to a `trial/*` commit whose tree satisfies §3.2 (the\n   365\t  worker-tip tree with exactly the eval-manifest path added, no other\n   366\t  path changes) and whose eval manifest matches §4, the second\n   367\t  invocation MUST be a no-op (no new ref, no new event).\n   368\t- If the trial already has `trial_commit_sha` set but the recorded\n   369\t  SHA does not resolve or disagrees with the trial, the state is\n   370\t  corrupt; the integrator MUST NOT silently overwrite. An operator\n   371\t  channel per §5.1 applies.\n   372\t\n   373\t## 6. Implementation latitude\n   374\t\n   375\tThe protocol leaves to implementations:\n   376\t\n   377\t- The git host (local bare repo, Gitea, GitHub, etc.) and the\n   378\t  transport used to write `trial/*` refs.\n   379\t- The retention policy for `work/*` branches after promotion.\n   380\t- The exact author/committer identity stamped on the integrator's\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Events:\n    99\t  `task.completed` (or `task.failed`) + `proposal.completed`, in one\n   100\t  atomic commit.\n   101\t- **Evaluate terminal (`success`/`error`)** — the `evaluate` task's\n   102\t  terminal transition plus writes to the trial's `status`, `metrics`,\n   103\t  `artifacts_uri`, and `completed_at` ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md)\n   104\t  §4.4). Events: `task.completed` (or `task.failed`) +\n   105\t  `trial.succeeded` / `trial.errored`, in one atomic commit.\n   106\t- **Implement reclaim with in-flight trial** — reclamation of an\n   107\t  `implement` task whose prior execution left a trial in `starting`\n   108\t  requires transitioning that trial to `error` atomically with the\n   109\t  reclaim ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5.4). Events:\n   110\t  `task.reclaimed` + `trial.errored`, in one atomic commit.\n   111\t- **Retry-exhausted `eval_error` terminal** — the orchestrator's\n   112\t  transition of a trial from `starting` to `eval_error`\n   113\t  ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3). When the\n   114\t  orchestrator persists this transition as a state change on the\n   115\t  trial, it MUST emit `trial.eval_errored` atomically.\n   116\t- **Trial promotion** — the integrator's write of a `trial/*` commit\n   117\t  and the `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial\n   118\t  ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). Event:\n   119\t  `trial.integrated`.\n   120\t\n   121\tA subscriber processing any of these composite events MUST therefore\n   122\teither observe the full set or observe none; partial visibility is a\n   123\tprotocol violation.\n   124\t\n   125\t### 2.3 What is *not* a state change\n   126\t\n   127\tOperations that do not change protocol-owned state do not require\n   128\tevents:\n   129\t\n   130\t- Reading a task, proposal, trial, or event.\n   131\t- Uploading artifacts to the artifact store before they are referenced\n   132\t  from a protocol-owned object. (Populating `artifacts_uri` on a\n   133\t  proposal or trial *is* a state change and is covered by the events\n   134\t  on those objects.)\n   135\t- Worker-internal progress (scratch files, local logs).\n   136\t\n   137\tImplementations MAY expose operational telemetry through other\n   138\tchannels without running it through the event log.\n   139\t\n   140\t## 3. Event registry\n   141\t\n   142\tEvery v0 event `type` defined by the protocol is listed below. For\n   143\teach, the `data` object's required fields are pinned; optional fields\n   144\tare called out per entry. The JSON Schema in\n   145\t[`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json) enforces\n   146\tthese shapes via `if/then` dispatch on `type`.\n   147\t\n   148\tImplementations MAY emit additional event types outside this registry\n   149\t(§3.5). The `type` pattern in §1 is the only structural constraint on\n   150\tthem.\n   151\t\n   152\t### 3.1 Task events\n   153\t\n   154\tProduced atomically with the transitions defined in\n   155\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The `task_id` field on\n   156\tevery task-event payload names the task whose transition is being\n   157\trecorded.\n   158\t\n   159\t| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |\n   160\t|---|---|---|\n   161\t| `task.created` | — → `pending` | `task_id`, `kind` |\n   162\t| `task.claimed` | `pending` → `claimed` | `task_id`, `worker_id` |\n   163\t| `task.submitted` | `claimed` → `submitted` | `task_id` |\n   164\t| `task.completed` | `submitted` → `completed` | `task_id` |\n   165\t| `task.failed` | `submitted` → `failed` | `task_id`, `reason` |\n   166\t| `task.reclaimed` | `claimed`/`submitted` → `pending` | `task_id`, `cause` |\n   167\t\n   168\tPayload field definitions:\n   169\t\n   170\t- `task_id` — the `task_id` of the transitioning task.\n   171\t- `kind` — the task's `kind`\n   172\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1); one of `plan`,\n   173\t  `implement`, `evaluate`.\n   174\t- `worker_id` — the `claim.worker_id` recorded on the successful\n   175\t  claim ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.2).\n   176\t- `reason` — one of the strings `\"worker_error\"` (the worker's\n   177\t  submission declared failure), `\"validation_error\"` (the orchestrator\n   178\t  rejected the result as malformed or non-conforming), or\n   179\t  `\"policy_limit\"` (a policy such as retry budget caused the failure).\n   180\t  The literal set is closed for v0; an implementation that needs\n   181\t  finer granularity MAY add a separate operator-level event under its\n   182\t  own type (§3.5).\n   183\t- `cause` — one of `\"expired\"` (claim `expires_at` passed),\n   184\t  `\"operator\"` (explicit operator action), or `\"health_policy\"`\n   185\t  (task store health policy declared the worker unreachable). The\n   186\t  literal set is closed for v0 on the same terms as `reason`.\n   187\t\n   188\tThe payload MAY include additional fields beyond those required;\n   189\tsubscribers MUST tolerate them. A conforming orchestrator SHOULD\n   190\tinclude the submitting worker's `worker_id` on `task.submitted`,\n   191\t`task.completed`, and `task.failed` events when known, as an\n   192\toperational convenience — but this is not required because the\n   193\tworker-task binding is already recoverable from the preceding\n   194\t`task.claimed` event.\n   195\t\n   196\t### 3.2 Proposal events\n   197\t\n   198\tProduced atomically with the proposal `state` transitions defined in\n   199\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5 and\n   200\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2. Composite commits that bind a\n   201\tproposal event to a task event are enumerated in §2.2.\n   202\t\n   203\t| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |\n   204\t|---|---|---|\n   205\t| `proposal.drafted` | — → `drafting` | `proposal_id` |\n   206\t| `proposal.ready` | `drafting` → `ready` | `proposal_id` |\n   207\t| `proposal.dispatched` | `ready` → `dispatched` | `proposal_id`, `task_id` |\n   208\t| `proposal.completed` | `dispatched` → `completed` | `proposal_id`, `task_id` |\n   209\t\n   210\tPayload field definitions:\n   211\t\n   212\t- `proposal_id` — the `proposal_id` of the transitioning proposal.\n   213\t- `task_id` — for `proposal.dispatched`, the `task_id` of the\n   214\t  `implement` task created by the same commit (§2.2). For\n   215\t  `proposal.completed`, the `task_id` of the `implement` task whose\n   216\t  terminal transition is being recorded.\n   217\t\n   218\t### 3.3 Trial events\n   219\t\n   220\tProduced atomically with trial `status` transitions and with the\n   221\tintegrator's promotion write. The trial's lifecycle is defined in\n   222\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7 and\n   223\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3–§4; the promotion step is in\n   224\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n   225\t\n   226\t| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |\n   227\t|---|---|---|\n   228\t| `trial.started` | — → `starting` | `trial_id`, `proposal_id` |\n   229\t| `trial.succeeded` | `starting` → `success` | `trial_id`, `commit_sha` |\n   230\t| `trial.errored` | `starting` → `error` | `trial_id` |\n   231\t| `trial.eval_errored` | `starting` → `eval_error` | `trial_id` |\n   232\t| `trial.integrated` | — (integrator writes `trial_commit_sha`) | `trial_id`, `trial_commit_sha` |\n   233\t\n   234\tPayload field definitions:\n   235\t\n   236\t- `trial_id` — the `trial_id` of the transitioning trial.\n   237\t- `proposal_id` — the trial's `proposal_id`\n   238\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n   239\t- `commit_sha` — the worker-branch tip recorded on the trial at the\n   240\t  moment of success ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n   241\t- `trial_commit_sha` — the canonical-lineage SHA the integrator wrote\n   242\t  ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). A 40-hex SHA-1 or a\n   243\t  64-hex SHA-256; the same pattern as commits elsewhere in the data\n   244\t  model.\n   245\t\n   246\t`trial.integrated` is not a trial-`status` transition — integration\n   247\tdoes not change the trial's `status` field, only its\n   248\t`trial_commit_sha`. The event marks promotion so subscribers can\n   249\treconstruct the canonical lineage without reading git directly.\n   250\t\n   251\t### 3.4 Subscriber observability guarantee\n   252\t\n   253\tEvery protocol-owned state-machine transition in v0 is covered by\n   254\texactly one event type in §3.1–§3.3. A subscriber that consumes\n   255\tevery event with a registered `type`, in log order, MUST be able to\n   256\treconstruct the **lifecycle history** of every task, proposal, and\n   257\ttrial in the experiment: which entities exist, in what states, in\n   258\twhat order. A conforming implementation MUST NOT expose a state-\n   259\tmachine transition that is not marked by its corresponding\n   260\tregistered event.\n   261\t\n   262\tRegistered event payloads carry only the fields needed to *identify*\n   263\tthe transitioning entity and any cross-entity references (e.g.\n   264\t`proposal.dispatched` carries the implement `task_id`). They do\n   265\tnot carry full entity snapshots. A subscriber that needs the content\n   266\tof an entity (the proposal's `parent_commits` and `artifacts_uri`,\n   267\tthe trial's `metrics` and `completed_at`) MUST read that entity from\n   268\tits store using the identifier the event carries; the read returns\n   269\tthe entity's current state\n   270\t([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.7). This boundary is deliberate:\n   271\tevents mark what happened; the entity stores hold what the entity\n   272\t*is*. Coupling the two into a full event-sourced projection — a\n   273\tsubscriber reconstructing every intermediate entity value from\n   274\tevents alone — is a deployment MAY implement on top of v0 but is not\n   275\ta protocol requirement, since v0 does not mandate historical reads\n   276\ton the entity stores.\n   277\t\n   278\tThe transactional invariant (§2) combined with the atomic event +\n   279\tstate-change rule guarantees that any entity reachable via an event's\n   280\tidentifier is already durable at read time.\n   281\t\n   282\t### 3.5 Non-registered event types\n   283\t\n   284\tImplementations MAY emit events whose `type` is not listed in\n   285\t§3.1–§3.3, provided the type conforms to the `^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*(\\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)+$`\n   286\tpattern. Such events carry no protocol-defined semantics and MUST NOT\n   287\tbe required by any conforming subscriber.\n   288\t\n   289\tA non-registered event MUST NOT carry a registered `type` with a\n   290\tnon-conforming payload; that is a protocol violation, not an\n   291\textension. The registry is closed at v0: a future spec lineage\n   292\t(`v1`) MAY add or revise registered types.\n   293\t\n   294\t## 4. Delivery guarantees\n   295\t\n   296\tA conforming event log MUST provide each subscriber the following\n   297\tguarantees, which subscribers MAY rely on without additional\n   298\tcoordination.\n   299\t\n   300\t### 4.1 Total order per experiment\n   301\t\n   302\tWithin a single experiment, a conforming event log MUST present a\n   303\tsingle total order to subscribers. Two subscribers reading the same\n   304\texperiment's stream MUST observe every pair of events in the same\n   305\trelative order. The ordering mechanism (monotonic sequence, log\n   306\tposition, vector clock) is a storage concern; the observable\n   307\tguarantee is not.\n   308\t\n   309\t### 4.2 Causal consistency\n   310\t\n   311\tWithin the per-experiment total order, the sequence MUST respect\n   312\tcausality: if event B describes a state change that depended on a\n   313\tstate recorded by event A, A MUST precede B in the log\n   314\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3). In particular, for\n   315\tevery composite commit enumerated in §2.2, the events produced by\n   316\tthat commit MAY appear in any order relative to each other — they\n   317\twere written atomically and neither causes the other — but they MUST\n   318\tall precede any event that causally depends on the committed state.\n   319\t\n   320\t### 4.3 At-least-once delivery\n   321\t\n   322\tA conforming log MUST deliver every appended event to every active\n   323\tsubscriber at least once. A subscriber MAY observe the same\n   324\t`event_id` more than once under recovery, restart, or reconnect;\n   325\tsubscribers MUST dedupe by `event_id` when exact-once semantics are\n   326\trequired for their projection.\n   327\t\n   328\tImplementations MAY offer stronger delivery than at-least-once (e.g.\n   329\texactly-once via offset commits), but MUST NOT offer weaker than\n   330\tat-least-once.\n   331\t\n   332\t### 4.4 Replayability\n   333\t\n   334\tFor the **lifetime of an experiment** — from registration until the\n   335\texperiment reaches its operator-declared terminal state (Phase 12\n   336\tcontrol-plane concern; for v0, treat this as \"until the deployment\n   337\texplicitly archives the experiment\") — a conforming log MUST retain\n   338\tevery appended event for that experiment and MUST allow any\n   339\tsubscriber to replay the full stream from the experiment's first\n   340\tevent. This is what lets late subscribers reconstruct history and\n   341\tlets existing subscribers recover from projection corruption. The\n   342\tlog MAY allow starting from a caller-supplied position (event ID or\n   343\toffset) as an optimization, but the complete replay is required.\n   344\t\n   345\tA conforming deployment MAY compact or archive an experiment's\n   346\tevents *only* after the experiment has reached its operator-declared\n   347\tterminal state. Once compacted, the log MUST either preserve full\n   348\treplay from an archive (preferred), or reject replays older than the\n   349\tcompaction horizon with a well-defined error. A log MUST NOT silently\n   350\tdrop events from a replay during an experiment's active lifetime.\n   351\t\n   352\t### 4.5 Durability\n   353\t\n   354\tA successful append MUST be durable through the next crash of the\n   355\tlog implementation. An event the log has acknowledged to the writer\n   356\tMUST NOT later disappear from any subscriber's stream. Durability\n   357\trequirements for the log as a store are pinned in\n   358\t[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §3.\n   359\t\n   360\t### 4.6 No cross-experiment ordering\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
