When the\n   114\t  orchestrator persists this transition as a state change on the\n   115\t  trial, it MUST emit `trial.eval_errored` atomically.\n   116\t- **Trial promotion** — the integrator's write of a `trial/*` commit\n   117\t  and the `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial\n   118\t  ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). Event:\n   119\t  `trial.integrated`.\n   120\t\n   121\tA subscriber processing any of these composite events MUST therefore\n   122\teither observe the full set or observe none; partial visibility is a\n   123\tprotocol violation.\n   124\t\n   125\t### 2.3 What is *not* a state change\n   126\t\n   127\tOperations that do not change protocol-owned state do not require\n   128\tevents:\n   129\t\n   130\t- Reading a task, proposal, trial, or event.\n   131\t- Uploading artifacts to the artifact store before they are referenced\n   132\t  from a protocol-owned object. (Populating `artifacts_uri` on a\n   133\t  proposal or trial *is* a state change and is covered by the events\n   134\t  on those objects.)\n   135\t- Worker-internal progress (scratch files, local logs).\n   136\t\n   137\tImplementations MAY expose operational telemetry through other\n   138\tchannels without running it through the event log.\n   139\t\n   140\t## 3. Event registry\n   141\t\n   142\tEvery v0 event `type` defined by the protocol is listed below. For\n   143\teach, the `data` object's required fields are pinned; optional fields\n   144\tare called out per entry. The JSON Schema in\n   145\t[`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json) enforces\n   146\tthese shapes via `if/then` dispatch on `type`.\n   147\t\n   148\tImplementations MAY emit additional event types outside this registry\n   149\t(§3.5). The `type` pattern in §1 is the only structural constraint on\n   150\tthem.\n   151\t\n   152\t### 3.1 Task events\n   153\t\n   154\tProduced atomically with the transitions defined in\n   155\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The `task_id` field on\n   156\tevery task-event payload names the task whose transition is being\n   157\trecorded.\n   158\t\n   159\t| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |\n   160\t|---|---|---|\n   161\t| `task.created` | — → `pending` | `task_id`, `kind` |\n   162\t| `task.claimed` | `pending` → `claimed` | `task_id`, `worker_id` |\n   163\t| `task.submitted` | `claimed` → `submitted` | `task_id` |\n   164\t| `task.completed` | `submitted` → `completed` | `task_id` |\n   165\t| `task.failed` | `submitted` → `failed` | `task_id`, `reason` |\n   166\t| `task.reclaimed` | `claimed`/`submitted` → `pending` | `task_id`, `cause` |\n   167\t\n   168\tPayload field definitions:\n   169\t\n   170\t- `task_id` — the `task_id` of the transitioning task.\n   171\t- `kind` — the task's `kind`\n   172\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1); one of `plan`,\n   173\t  `implement`, `evaluate`.\n   174\t- `worker_id` — the `claim.worker_id` recorded on the successful\n   175\t  claim ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.2).\n   176\t- `reason` — one of the strings `\"worker_error\"` (the worker's\n   177\t  submission declared failure), `\"validation_error\"` (the orchestrator\n   178\t  rejected the result as malformed or non-conforming), or\n   179\t  `\"policy_limit\"` (a policy such as retry budget caused the failure).\n   180\t  The literal set is closed for v0; an implementation that needs\n   181\t  finer granularity MAY add a separate operator-level event under its\n   182\t  own type (§3.5).\n   183\t- `cause` — one of `\"expired\"` (claim `expires_at` passed),\n   184\t  `\"operator\"` (explicit operator action), or `\"health_policy\"`\n   185\t  (task store health policy declared the worker unreachable). The\n   186\t  literal set is closed for v0 on the same terms as `reason`.\n   187\t\n   188\tThe payload MAY include additional fields beyond those required;\n   189\tsubscribers MUST tolerate them. A conforming orchestrator SHOULD\n   190\tinclude the submitting worker's `worker_id` on `task.submitted`,\n   191\t`task.completed`, and `task.failed` events when known, as an\n   192\toperational convenience — but this is not required because the\n   193\tworker-task binding is already recoverable from the preceding\n   194\t`task.claimed` event.\n   195\t\n   196\t### 3.2 Proposal events\n   197\t\n   198\tProduced atomically with the proposal `state` transitions defined in\n   199\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5 and\n   200\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2. Composite commits that bind a\n   201\tproposal event to a task event are enumerated in §2.2.\n   202\t\n   203\t| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |\n   204\t|---|---|---|\n   205\t| `proposal.drafted` | — → `drafting` | `proposal_id` |\n   206\t| `proposal.ready` | `drafting` → `ready` | `proposal_id` |\n   207\t| `proposal.dispatched` | `ready` → `dispatched` | `proposal_id`, `task_id` |\n   208\t| `proposal.completed` | `dispatched` → `completed` | `proposal_id`, `task_id` |\n   209\t\n   210\tPayload field definitions:\n   211\t\n   212\t- `proposal_id` — the `proposal_id` of the transitioning proposal.\n   213\t- `task_id` — for `proposal.dispatched`, the `task_id` of the\n   214\t  `implement` task created by the same commit (§2.2). For\n   215\t  `proposal.completed`, the `task_id` of the `implement` task whose\n   216\t  terminal transition is being recorded.\n   217\t\n   218\t### 3.3 Trial events\n   219\t\n   220\tProduced atomically with trial `status` transitions and with the\n   221\tintegrator's promotion write. The trial's lifecycle is defined in\n   222\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7 and\n   223\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3–§4; the promotion step is in\n   224\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n   225\t\n   226\t| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |\n   227\t|---|---|---|\n   228\t| `trial.started` | — → `starting` | `trial_id`, `proposal_id` |\n   229\t| `trial.succeeded` | `starting` → `success` | `trial_id`, `commit_sha` |\n   230\t| `trial.errored` | `starting` → `error` | `trial_id` |\n   231\t| `trial.eval_errored` | `starting` → `eval_error` | `trial_id` |\n   232\t| `trial.integrated` | — (integrator writes `trial_commit_sha`) | `trial_id`, `trial_commit_sha` |\n   233\t\n   234\tPayload field definitions:\n   235\t\n   236\t- `trial_id` — the `trial_id` of the transitioning trial.\n   237\t- `proposal_id` — the trial's `proposal_id`\n   238\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n   239\t- `commit_sha` — the worker-branch tip recorded on the trial at the\n   240\t  moment of success ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n   241\t- `trial_commit_sha` — the canonical-lineage SHA the integrator wrote\n   242\t  ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). A 40-hex SHA-1 or a\n   243\t  64-hex SHA-256; the same pattern as commits elsewhere in the data\n   244\t  model.\n   245\t\n   246\t`trial.integrated` is not a trial-`status` transition — integration\n   247\tdoes not change the trial's `status` field, only its\n   248\t`trial_commit_sha`. The event marks promotion so subscribers can\n   249\treconstruct the canonical lineage without reading git directly.\n   250\t\n   251\t### 3.4 Subscriber reconstruction guarantee\n   252\t\n   253\tEvery protocol-owned state change in v0 is covered by exactly one\n   254\tevent type in §3.1–§3.3. A subscriber that consumes every event with\n   255\ta registered `type`, in log order, MUST be able to reconstruct the\n   256\tfull history of every task, proposal, and trial in the experiment\n   257\twithout reading any other store. A conforming implementation MUST\n   258\tNOT expose a state that is not recoverable this way.\n   259\t\n   260\t### 3.5 Non-registered event types\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:20:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_9","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba spec/v0/06-integrator.md | sed -n '1,260p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Integrator\n     2\t\n     3\tThis chapter specifies the integrator role in full: the git topology\n     4\tit operates on, the promotion trigger, the squash rule, the\n     5\teval-manifest shape, and the invariants it preserves across the\n     6\tcanonical trial lineage.\n     7\t\n     8\tThe integrator is introduced in [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §5,\n     9\twhich pins its boundary rules — exclusive authority over `trial/*`,\n    10\texclusive authority over `trial_commit_sha`. This chapter specifies\n    11\twhat the integrator *produces* inside those boundaries.\n    12\t\n    13\tThe three-namespace topology (`main`, `trial/*`, `work/*`) is defined\n    14\tin [`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9. This chapter pins the\n    15\tinvariants on each namespace and the transformation from worker\n    16\tbranch to canonical trial commit.\n    17\t\n    18\t## 1. Topology invariants\n    19\t\n    20\tA conforming EDEN deployment operates on a single git repository per\n    21\texperiment. That repository carries three ref namespaces:\n    22\t\n    23\t### 1.1 `main`\n    24\t\n    25\t- Names the experiment's starting commit.\n    26\t- MUST be present at experiment registration time and MUST name a\n    27\t  commit reachable by every proposal's `parent_commits`\n    28\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.2).\n    29\t- Is **immutable** for the duration of the experiment. A conforming\n    30\t  integrator MUST NOT move, rewrite, or delete `main`; neither MAY\n    31\t  any worker. External tooling (e.g. repo maintainers merging work\n    32\t  into `main` outside the experiment) is the operator's concern and\n    33\t  lies outside the protocol. An experiment MAY tolerate such\n    34\t  external moves only if it does not depend on `main` as a\n    35\t  persistent parent; conforming implementations SHOULD record the\n    36\t  experiment's starting commit SHA explicitly and use that SHA, not\n    37\t  the current `main`, when validating parent reachability.\n    38\t\n    39\t### 1.2 `trial/*`\n    40\t\n    41\t- Holds the canonical trial lineage, one commit per integrated trial.\n    42\t- The integrator is the **sole writer** ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md)\n    43\t  §5.1). No other role — planner, implementer, evaluator,\n    44\t  orchestrator, operator tooling invoked through protocol-owned\n    45\t  channels — MAY write to `trial/*`.\n    46\t- Each `trial/*` branch MUST name exactly one trial. Branch naming\n    47\t  conforms to §3.1.\n    48\t- A `trial/*` branch MUST NOT be deleted or rewritten once written.\n    49\t  If an integrator discovers a bug in an earlier promotion, it MAY\n    50\t  record corrective state in subsequent events or operator-level\n    51\t  channels, but MUST NOT rewrite history on `trial/*`.\n    52\t\n    53\t### 1.3 `work/*`\n    54\t\n    55\t- Holds per-trial worker branches written by implementers\n    56\t  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3.3).\n    57\t- Each `work/*` branch MUST be unique to a single trial. A trial's\n    58\t  `branch` field ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1)\n    59\t  records the exact branch name.\n    60\t- Worker branches are **inputs** to the integrator, not normative\n    61\t  outputs. A conforming deployment MAY retain them for audit and\n    62\t  debugging after promotion, MAY garbage-collect them after a\n    63\t  retention window, and MAY delete them eagerly once a trial is\n    64\t  integrated — the retention policy is a deployment concern. No\n    65\t  protocol-owned reader MAY depend on a worker branch after the\n    66\t  trial's promotion.\n    67\t- The evaluator reads a worker branch at the trial's `commit_sha`\n    68\t  during evaluation ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.1); the\n    69\t  implementer's read-your-writes guarantee applies only until\n    70\t  promotion, after which the worker branch's reachability is no\n    71\t  longer a protocol-owned concern.\n    72\t\n    73\t### 1.4 Reachability rule\n    74\t\n    75\tEvery commit on a `work/*` branch MUST descend from the proposal's\n    76\tdeclared `parent_commits` ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3.3). The\n    77\tintegrator MUST reject a trial whose `commit_sha` does not satisfy\n    78\tthis rule; rejection produces no `trial/*` commit and no\n    79\t`trial.integrated` event. The orchestrator MAY transition the trial\n    80\tto `error` via the normal channels if a reachability violation is\n    81\tdiscovered, but the integrator MUST NOT fabricate a `trial.errored`\n    82\tevent itself — events are paired with state changes, and the\n    83\tintegrator is not the writer for trial `status`.\n    84\t\n    85\t## 2. Promotion trigger\n    86\t\n    87\tThe integrator promotes a trial iff:\n    88\t\n    89\t- The trial's `status` is `success`\n    90\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n    91\t- The trial's `commit_sha` is set and resolves to a commit on the\n    92\t  trial's `branch` in the experiment repository.\n    93\t- The trial has not yet been promoted (`trial_commit_sha` is absent).\n    94\t\n    95\tThe mechanism by which the integrator observes the trigger —\n    96\tsubscribing to `trial.succeeded` events, polling the trial store, or\n    97\treceiving a dispatch call — is a binding concern and is not pinned.\n    98\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §5.2.\n    99\t\n   100\tA conforming integrator MUST NOT promote trials in any other status.\n   101\tIn particular, `error`, `eval_error`, and `starting` trials MUST NOT\n   102\treceive a `trial/*` commit.\n   103\t\n   104\tThe integrator MUST NOT promote a trial whose `metrics` do not\n   105\tvalidate against the experiment's `metrics_schema`\n   106\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.2,\n   107\t[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §4). The orchestrator's acceptance\n   108\tof a `success` submission is the primary guard for this; the\n   109\tintegrator MAY additionally re-validate as defense in depth but MUST\n   110\tNOT silently drop or coerce invalid metrics.\n   111\t\n   112\t## 3. Promotion output\n   113\t\n   114\t### 3.1 `trial/*` branch name\n   115\t\n   116\tThe integrator creates a branch under `trial/` named:\n   117\t\n   118\t```text\n   119\ttrial/<trial_id>-<slug>\n   120\t```\n   121\t\n   122\twhere `<trial_id>` is the trial's `trial_id`\n   123\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1) and `<slug>` is the\n   124\t`slug` of the trial's parent proposal\n   125\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1). The slug is already\n   126\tconstrained to `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*$`, and `trial_id` is opaque; the\n   127\tresulting branch name MUST be valid under git's ref-format rules\n   128\t([git check-ref-format]). If an implementation's `trial_id` choice\n   129\tcould violate ref-format rules, the implementation MUST choose a\n   130\tdifferent `trial_id` representation — it MUST NOT mutate the stored\n   131\t`trial_id` in the trial object to accommodate its branch-naming\n   132\tscheme.\n   133\t\n   134\t[git check-ref-format]: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-check-ref-format\n   135\t\n   136\t### 3.2 Squash rule\n   137\t\n   138\tThe trial's worker branch MAY contain multiple commits (implementer\n   139\tstep-by-step work, partial progress, iterated attempts). The\n   140\tcanonical trial commit MUST be a **single commit** whose tree matches\n   141\tthe worker branch's tip tree.\n   142\t\n   143\tConcretely: the integrator MUST produce a commit `T` with:\n   144\t\n   145\t- `tree(T) == tree(commit_sha)`, where `commit_sha` is the trial's\n   146\t  recorded worker-branch tip.\n   147\t- `parents(T) == <parent_commits>` of the trial, in the order\n   148\t  recorded on the trial\n   149\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1 inherits from the\n   150\t  proposal, §5.1).\n   151\t- A commit message whose first line (subject) records the trial\n   152\t  identity in a machine-parseable form. §3.3 pins the format.\n   153\t- An eval manifest attached per §4.\n   154\t\n   155\tThe squash is a single merge-commit-shaped object (parents may be\n   156\tone or many, per the proposal's `parent_commits`); intermediate\n   157\twork-branch commits MUST NOT appear in `T`'s ancestor chain along a\n   158\tfirst-parent path when read from `trial/*`. Readers of the\n   159\tcanonical lineage see one commit per trial, not the worker's\n   160\tintermediate history.\n   161\t\n   162\tA conforming implementation MAY additionally preserve the worker\n   163\tbranch's history elsewhere (retained `work/*` ref, archive) for\n   164\taudit, but that preservation MUST NOT make intermediate commits\n   165\treachable from `trial/*`.\n   166\t\n   167\t### 3.3 Commit message\n   168\t\n   169\tThe `trial/*` commit message's first line (subject) MUST match:\n   170\t\n   171\t```text\n   172\ttrial: <trial_id> <slug>\n   173\t```\n   174\t\n   175\tThe subject MUST carry both the `trial_id` and the parent proposal's\n   176\t`slug`, separated by a single space. The body MAY include additional\n   177\thuman-readable context. The machine-parseable header allows a\n   178\tsubscriber reading git alone — without the event log — to recover the\n   179\t`trial_id` of any `trial/*` commit.\n   180\t\n   181\t### 3.4 Atomic write\n   182\t\n   183\tThe promotion MUST be atomic with two other state changes:\n   184\t\n   185\t1. Writing the `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial to the SHA of\n   186\t   the new `trial/*` commit\n   187\t   ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).\n   188\t2. Appending a `trial.integrated` event to the event log\n   189\t   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §3.3).\n   190\t\n   191\tIf any of the three steps fails — git ref write, trial-object\n   192\tfield write, event append — the integrator MUST roll back any\n   193\talready-performed step. In particular, a dangling `trial/*` ref with\n   194\tno `trial_commit_sha` field and no `trial.integrated` event is a\n   195\tprotocol violation. Implementations MAY use store-level\n   196\ttransactions, outbox patterns, compensating deletes, or any other\n   197\tmechanism; the observable invariant is that a reader of any one of\n   198\tthe three artifacts (ref, field, event) MUST observe the other two.\n   199\t\n   200\t## 4. Eval manifest\n   201\t\n   202\tEach `trial/*` commit MUST carry an **eval manifest** at a fixed path\n   203\tinside its tree. The manifest is the canonical, git-embedded record\n   204\tof the trial's evaluator outputs: what was measured, which worker\n   205\tcommit was measured, where the supporting artifacts live.\n   206\t\n   207\t### 4.1 Path\n   208\t\n   209\tThe eval manifest is a JSON file at:\n   210\t\n   211\t```text\n   212\t.eden/trials/<trial_id>/eval.json\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\trelative to the root of the committed tree. The `<trial_id>`\n   216\tcomponent matches the trial's `trial_id`. An integrator MUST NOT add\n   217\tany file under `.eden/trials/<other_trial_id>/` to a trial's commit;\n   218\teach `trial/*` commit carries exactly one eval-manifest path,\n   219\tcorresponding to its own trial.\n   220\t\n   221\t### 4.2 Shape\n   222\t\n   223\tThe manifest is a JSON object with the following required fields:\n   224\t\n   225\t| Field | Type | Meaning |\n   226\t|---|---|---|\n   227\t| `trial_id` | string | MUST equal the trial's `trial_id`. |\n   228\t| `proposal_id` | string | MUST equal the trial's `proposal_id`. |\n   229\t| `commit_sha` | string | The worker-branch tip the evaluator measured; MUST equal the trial's recorded `commit_sha`. |\n   230\t| `parent_commits` | array of string | MUST equal the trial's `parent_commits`, in order. |\n   231\t| `metrics` | object | The evaluator's metrics payload, conforming to the experiment's `metrics_schema`. |\n   232\t| `artifacts` | object | `uri` → content hash of every external artifact the evaluator referenced. §4.3. |\n   233\t| `evaluator` | object | Identity and version of the evaluator that produced the metrics. §4.4. |\n   234\t| `evaluated_at` | timestamp | When the evaluator completed. UTC, RFC 3339 profile as elsewhere in the data model. |\n   235\t\n   236\tOptional fields:\n   237\t\n   238\t| Field | Type | Meaning |\n   239\t|---|---|---|\n   240\t| `description` | string | The trial's `description`, copied from the trial object if present. |\n   241\t\n   242\tThe manifest MUST NOT contain fields whose values differ from the\n   243\ttrial object's corresponding fields at the moment of promotion.\n   244\tImplementations MAY include additional informational fields beyond\n   245\tthose listed; consumers MUST tolerate them.\n   246\t\n   247\t### 4.3 `artifacts` shape\n   248\t\n   249\tEach entry is:\n   250\t\n   251\t```json\n   252\t{\n   253\t  \"uri\": \"<URI the evaluator uploaded the artifact to>\",\n   254\t  \"sha256\": \"<hex sha256 of the artifact's byte content>\",\n   255\t  \"bytes\": <non-negative integer>\n   256\t}\n   257\t```\n   258\t\n   259\tThe map's keys are operator-chosen short labels (e.g. `\"log\"`,\n   260\t`\"output.csv\"`); they MUST be unique within the manifest. The\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:22:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_10","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba spec/v0/08-storage.md | sed -n '1,260p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Storage\n     2\t\n     3\tThis chapter specifies the three durable stores an EDEN deployment\n     4\tdepends on — the **task store**, the **event log**, and the\n     5\t**artifact store** — as protocol-level contracts. It pins the\n     6\toperations each store MUST expose, the consistency and durability\n     7\tproperties those operations MUST honor, and the invariants spanning\n     8\tstores that a conforming deployment MUST preserve.\n     9\t\n    10\tThe stores are introduced in [`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §10.\n    11\tThe entities they hold are defined in\n    12\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md); the behavioral contracts\n    13\tconsuming them are in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md),\n    14\t[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md), and\n    15\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md). This chapter specifies the\n    16\tstorage-side contract those behaviors rely on.\n    17\t\n    18\tA store is specified by **what observable guarantees it provides**,\n    19\tnot by any particular mechanism. A conforming task store MAY be\n    20\tbacked by a relational database, an embedded key-value engine, or an\n    21\tin-memory structure; the same is true of the event log and artifact\n    22\tstore. Conformance is behavioral.\n    23\t\n    24\t## 1. Task store\n    25\t\n    26\tThe task store holds the set of tasks and their current state for an\n    27\texperiment. It MUST support the operations below, each with the\n    28\tlisted atomicity and failure semantics. Wire-level transport is a\n    29\tbinding concern and is not pinned here.\n    30\t\n    31\t### 1.1 Operations\n    32\t\n    33\t| Operation | Arguments | Effect |\n    34\t|---|---|---|\n    35\t| `create_task` | fully-formed `task` object with `state == \"pending\"` | Atomically inserts the task and appends `task.created` to the event log ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)). |\n    36\t| `claim` | `task_id`, `worker_id`, OPTIONAL `expires_at` | Atomically transitions a `pending` task to `claimed`, issues a fresh claim token, and appends `task.claimed`. |\n    37\t| `submit` | `task_id`, `token`, role-specific result | Atomically transitions a `claimed` task to `submitted`, persists the result, and appends `task.submitted`. Idempotent per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2. |\n    38\t| `accept` | `task_id`, role-specific acceptance payload | Atomically transitions `submitted → completed`, applies the role-specific effect (e.g. proposal completion, trial status write), clears `claim`, and appends `task.completed` plus any composite events. |\n    39\t| `reject` | `task_id`, `reason`, role-specific failure payload | Atomically transitions `submitted → failed`, applies the role-specific effect, clears `claim`, and appends `task.failed` plus any composite events. |\n    40\t| `reclaim` | `task_id`, OPTIONAL `cause` | Atomically transitions `claimed`/`submitted` back to `pending` (where permitted; [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5.1), invalidates the token, clears `claim`, performs §2.2 composite effects where applicable, and appends `task.reclaimed`. |\n    41\t| `read_task` | `task_id` | Returns the current task object or a well-defined not-found signal. |\n    42\t| `list_tasks` | filter (`kind`, `state`, `experiment_id`, …) | Returns matching tasks. Ordering is implementation-defined. |\n    43\t\n    44\t\"Atomically\" means: the state change, the result-payload write, any\n    45\tcross-entity effect listed in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)\n    46\t§2.2, and the event appends are all durable together or none of them\n    47\tis ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)).\n    48\t\n    49\t### 1.2 Atomic claim (serialization)\n    50\t\n    51\tFor any given `pending` task, at most one concurrent `claim`\n    52\tinvocation MUST succeed. A conforming task store MUST expose this as\n    53\ta linearizable operation: after a successful claim, every subsequent\n    54\treader of the task observes `state == \"claimed\"` with the new claim\n    55\tobject; no reader observes an intermediate state without the claim.\n    56\t\n    57\tThe serialization mechanism (row lock + update, compare-and-set on\n    58\tversion, `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) is implementation-defined.\n    59\tThe observable guarantee is not.\n    60\t\n    61\t### 1.3 Token-authorization rule\n    62\t\n    63\t`submit` and any other claim-holder operation MUST reject calls whose\n    64\tpresented token does not equal the token currently recorded on the\n    65\ttask's `claim` object\n    66\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.3). A conforming\n    67\ttask store MUST NOT use `worker_id` as a substitute for the token\n    68\twhen authorizing.\n    69\t\n    70\t### 1.4 Idempotent resubmit\n    71\t\n    72\tThe idempotency rule for `submit` is specified normatively in\n    73\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2. A conforming task\n    74\tstore MUST implement it: a resubmit carrying the current token, with\n    75\ta content-equivalent payload, MUST succeed without mutating state;\n    76\tan inconsistent resubmit MUST be rejected.\n    77\t\n    78\t### 1.5 Terminal immutability\n    79\t\n    80\tOnce a task is `completed` or `failed`\n    81\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.4), no subsequent\n    82\toperation MAY change any task field except audit-only metadata the\n    83\ttask store documents outside the normative schema. In particular,\n    84\t`reclaim` MUST be rejected on terminal tasks.\n    85\t\n    86\t### 1.6 Per-task serialization\n    87\t\n    88\tAll mutating operations on a single task MUST be serialized: the\n    89\tobservable history of a task is a total order\n    90\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §6.1). A conforming\n    91\ttask store MUST NOT expose a state that has not yet been accompanied\n    92\tby its event ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)).\n    93\t\n    94\t## 2. Event log\n    95\t\n    96\tThe event log holds the per-experiment event stream. It MUST support\n    97\tthe operations below and MUST honor the delivery guarantees specified\n    98\tin [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.\n    99\t\n   100\t### 2.1 Operations\n   101\t\n   102\t| Operation | Arguments | Effect |\n   103\t|---|---|---|\n   104\t| `append` | fully-formed event object | Atomically appends the event, together with the state change it describes ([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)). |\n   105\t| `read_range` | experiment_id, OPTIONAL cursor | Returns a durable, ordered range of events. |\n   106\t| `subscribe` | experiment_id, OPTIONAL starting cursor | Returns a stream that delivers events in log order until cancelled. |\n   107\t| `replay` | experiment_id | Returns the full stream from the first event ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.4). |\n   108\t\n   109\t`append` MUST NOT be exposed as a standalone operation that the task\n   110\tstore, proposal store, or trial store can call independently of the\n   111\tstate change the event describes. The atomicity contract\n   112\t([§6](#6-transactional-guarantees)) requires the append to travel\n   113\twith the state change; the event log operation is a primitive of the\n   114\tcomposite transaction, not an independent one.\n   115\t\n   116\t### 2.2 Ordering and causality\n   117\t\n   118\tThe log MUST present a single total order per experiment\n   119\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.1) that respects\n   120\tcausality ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.2). The\n   121\tordering mechanism is implementation-defined; the observable\n   122\tguarantee is not.\n   123\t\n   124\tThe log MAY provide a global ordering across experiments as an\n   125\toptimization, but MUST NOT require subscribers to depend on it\n   126\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.6).\n   127\t\n   128\t### 2.3 Delivery\n   129\t\n   130\tAt-least-once delivery is the minimum contract\n   131\t([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.3).\n   132\tImplementations MAY offer exactly-once via durable offset commits.\n   133\t\n   134\t### 2.4 Retention\n   135\t\n   136\tA conforming event log MUST retain every event for a deployment-\n   137\tdefined retention window that is at least long enough to support\n   138\tsubscriber replay from the oldest event a still-connected subscriber\n   139\trequires ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.4). The\n   140\tlog MAY compact or archive older events. If compaction discards\n   141\tevents, the log MUST either (a) reject replays older than the\n   142\tcompaction horizon with a well-defined error, or (b) serve those\n   143\treplays from an archive that preserves order and content. It MUST\n   144\tNOT silently drop events from a replay.\n   145\t\n   146\t## 3. Durability\n   147\t\n   148\tThe following applies uniformly to the task store and the event log.\n   149\t\n   150\t### 3.1 Write durability\n   151\t\n   152\tAn operation that the store acknowledges to its caller MUST survive\n   153\ta subsequent crash of the store's host. \"Acknowledgement\" is the\n   154\treturn value indicating success; a store MUST NOT return success for\n   155\ta write that has not yet been persisted to durable media (or the\n   156\tequivalent quorum in a replicated implementation).\n   157\t\n   158\t### 3.2 Read-after-write consistency\n   159\t\n   160\tAfter a successful write, every subsequent read from any reader MUST\n   161\tobserve the effect of the write. A conforming store MUST NOT expose a\n   162\tstale view of data after acknowledging the write that would update\n   163\tit. (Subscribers receive events via the log's delivery mechanism,\n   164\tnot by polling the task store, so the read-after-write rule applies\n   165\tto direct reads; event delivery is governed by §4 of the event\n   166\tprotocol.)\n   167\t\n   168\t### 3.3 Crash recovery\n   169\t\n   170\tA store that crashes mid-operation MUST recover to a state in which\n   171\tevery acknowledged write is visible and no partially-applied\n   172\toperation is visible. Implementations that use multi-step\n   173\ttransactions MUST ensure rollback on failure so that partial state\n   174\tnever becomes observable.\n   175\t\n   176\t### 3.4 No fabrication\n   177\t\n   178\tA store MUST NOT materialize an event, a task, a proposal, or a trial\n   179\tfrom nothing. Every persisted object MUST originate from a caller-\n   180\tdriven operation. In particular, a conforming event log MUST NOT\n   181\tsynthesize events the caller did not `append`.\n   182\t\n   183\t## 4. Per-experiment metrics schemas\n   184\t\n   185\tEvery experiment declares a metrics schema in its `experiment_config`\n   186\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3,\n   187\t[`schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json`](schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json)).\n   188\tThe task store (or an equivalently-scoped component in a conforming\n   189\tdeployment) MUST enforce the following at every write that touches\n   190\ttrial metrics.\n   191\t\n   192\t### 4.1 Registration\n   193\t\n   194\tAt experiment registration time, a conforming deployment MUST persist\n   195\tthe experiment's metrics schema durably and atomically with the\n   196\texperiment's other configuration. A subsequent write of a\n   197\ttrial's `metrics` field MUST be validated against the schema\n   198\tregistered for that experiment; no write MAY bypass this validation.\n   199\t\n   200\t### 4.2 Immutability during an experiment\n   201\t\n   202\tA metrics schema MUST NOT be mutated for the lifetime of the\n   203\texperiment. An operator who wishes to change the metrics set MUST\n   204\tregister a new experiment (Phase 12 defines the control-plane\n   205\toperations that govern this, but the invariant applies from v0\n   206\tonward). A conforming deployment MUST reject any in-flight mutation\n   207\tof an existing experiment's metrics schema.\n   208\t\n   209\tThe rationale is canonicality: comparing trials across an experiment\n   210\tonly has meaning if the metric definitions they are compared on did\n   211\tnot move during the experiment.\n   212\t\n   213\t### 4.3 Per-metric type checks\n   214\t\n   215\tA successful write of a `trial.metrics` payload MUST satisfy:\n   216\t\n   217\t- Every key in `metrics` is present in the experiment's metrics\n   218\t  schema.\n   219\t- Every value either satisfies the declared type of its key — per\n   220\t  the type mapping in [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3\n   221\t  (`integer`, `real`, `text`) — or is `null`.\n   222\t- No reserved name ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §6.2)\n   223\t  appears as a key.\n   224\t\n   225\tA write that violates any of these MUST be rejected; the store MUST\n   226\tNOT record a trial as `success` with an invalid metrics payload\n   227\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.2).\n   228\t\n   229\t## 5. Artifact store\n   230\t\n   231\tThe artifact store holds files the roles produce (plan text, code\n   232\tdiffs, evaluator outputs, logs) and exposes them by URI. It is less\n   233\tload-bearing than the task store and event log — the protocol uses\n   234\tit only for content referenced by `artifacts_uri` on proposals and\n   235\ttrials — and its contract is accordingly narrower.\n   236\t\n   237\t### 5.1 Operations\n   238\t\n   239\tA conforming artifact store MUST support:\n   240\t\n   241\t- **Upload** — given content bytes and a proposed identity (naming\n   242\t  scheme is implementation-defined), persist the bytes and return a\n   243\t  URI that will later resolve to them.\n   244\t- **Fetch** — given a URI the store issued, return the byte content\n   245\t  the upload persisted.\n   246\t\n   247\tURIs MUST be RFC 3986–conformant\n   248\t([`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json)\n   249\tenforces this on the proposal side).\n   250\t\n   251\t### 5.2 Durability\n   252\t\n   253\tOnce the artifact store returns a URI to an uploader, the content at\n   254\tthat URI MUST remain resolvable until the experiment's retention\n   255\twindow elapses. A conforming deployment MUST define its retention\n   256\twindow explicitly. A URI recorded on a `trial/*` commit's eval\n   257\tmanifest ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §4.3) MUST be\n   258\tresolvable for at least as long as that `trial/*` commit is retained.\n   259\t\n   260\t### 5.3 Content integrity\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:24:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_11","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba spec/v0/01-concepts.md | sed -n '1,260p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Core Concepts\n     2\t\n     3\tThis chapter introduces the vocabulary used throughout the\n     4\tspecification. It defines each concept at the level of *what it is*\n     5\tand *what role it plays in the system*, not *how it behaves over\n     6\ttime*. Behavioral contracts — state machines, transactional\n     7\tguarantees, claim semantics — are defined in subsequent chapters.\n     8\t\n     9\t## 1. Experiment\n    10\t\n    11\tAn **experiment** is a single directed-code-evolution run. It has:\n    12\t\n    13\t- A starting commit on a designated branch of a git repository.\n    14\t- An **objective** — a scalar function of a trial's measured metrics,\n    15\t  to be either maximized or minimized.\n    16\t- A declared **metrics schema** — the names and types of the metrics\n    17\t  evaluators will report.\n    18\t- Role configurations — how to run the planner, implementer, and\n    19\t  evaluator for this experiment.\n    20\t- Termination conditions (trial-count, wall-clock, convergence\n    21\t  target).\n    22\t\n    23\tThe experiment's full configuration is specified by an\n    24\t**experiment-config** object (Chapter\n    25\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md); schema:\n    26\t[`schemas/experiment-config.schema.json`](schemas/experiment-config.schema.json)).\n    27\t\n    28\t## 2. Roles\n    29\t\n    30\tEDEN defines four role contracts. A conforming implementation of a\n    31\trole MUST honor the protocol surface defined for that role in\n    32\tChapter [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md). Within this chapter the roles\n    33\tare introduced at the concept level only.\n    34\t\n    35\t### 2.1 Planner\n    36\t\n    37\tThe **planner** proposes what to try. Its output is a **proposal** —\n    38\ta document describing a change to attempt, along with metadata\n    39\t(parent commits, priority, artifacts) needed to execute it. Planners\n    40\tare free in their internal strategy (LLM, search, human); the\n    41\tprotocol constrains only what a proposal looks like and when one is\n    42\tconsidered ready.\n    43\t\n    44\t### 2.2 Implementer\n    45\t\n    46\tThe **implementer** turns a proposal into a working-tree change. It\n    47\tconsumes a proposal, produces a commit (or commit sequence) on a\n    48\tper-trial branch, and signals completion. It does **not** judge\n    49\twhether the change is good — it only reifies the plan.\n    50\t\n    51\t### 2.3 Evaluator\n    52\t\n    53\tThe **evaluator** measures an implemented trial against the\n    54\texperiment's metrics schema. Its output is a set of metrics plus a\n    55\tstatus indicator (success, error). The evaluator MUST NOT modify the\n    56\ttrial branch; it observes only.\n    57\t\n    58\t### 2.4 Integrator\n    59\t\n    60\tThe **integrator** decides how evaluated trials enter the canonical\n    61\ttrial lineage. It squashes a per-trial worker branch into a\n    62\ttrial-shaped commit on a canonical branch, attaches an eval manifest,\n    63\tand exposes the resulting commit to downstream consumers. The\n    64\tintegrator is the sole writer of the canonical lineage.\n    65\t\n    66\t## 3. Proposal\n    67\t\n    68\tA **proposal** is the planner's output: a human-readable description\n    69\tof a change plus the metadata required to dispatch work against it.\n    70\tSchema: [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json).\n    71\tProposals have a lifecycle (drafting → ready → dispatched →\n    72\tcompleted) that is defined in\n    73\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). This chapter treats a\n    74\tproposal as a value object; the task protocol defines how its\n    75\tlifecycle advances.\n    76\t\n    77\t## 4. Trial\n    78\t\n    79\tA **trial** is a single attempt to improve the objective. A trial\n    80\treferences:\n    81\t\n    82\t- The proposal that was implemented.\n    83\t- The resulting commit(s) on the worker branch and the squashed\n    84\t  commit on the canonical branch.\n    85\t- The metrics reported by the evaluator.\n    86\t- A status (starting, success, error, eval_error).\n    87\t\n    88\tTrials are the unit of progress: a terminated experiment is\n    89\tsummarized by the sequence of its completed trials and their scores\n    90\tunder the objective. Schema:\n    91\t[`schemas/trial.schema.json`](schemas/trial.schema.json).\n    92\t\n    93\t## 5. Task\n    94\t\n    95\tA **task** is a unit of work dispatched to a role. Tasks are the\n    96\tprotocol's primary verb: \"implementer, please realize this\n    97\tproposal\"; \"evaluator, please score this branch.\" Every task has:\n    98\t\n    99\t- A **kind** (plan, implement, evaluate).\n   100\t- A **payload** shaped per-kind (a proposal reference, a commit to\n   101\t  evaluate, etc.).\n   102\t- A **state** advancing through a state machine\n   103\t  ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md)).\n   104\t- A **claim token** that grants a specific worker temporary exclusive\n   105\t  right to execute it.\n   106\t\n   107\tSchema: [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json). The\n   108\ttask is *stored data*; the task protocol is *how state transitions\n   109\thappen*.\n   110\t\n   111\t## 6. Event\n   112\t\n   113\tAn **event** records a state change in the system — a task claimed,\n   114\ta proposal submitted, a trial evaluated. Events are immutable, append\n   115\tto an event log, and are read by subscribers (UIs, schedulers,\n   116\tother roles).\n   117\t\n   118\tA central EDEN invariant: **every state change that is observable via\n   119\ttasks or trials MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event, and\n   120\tthe event write MUST be atomic with the state change it describes.**\n   121\tSubscribers rely on this to reconstruct the system's history without\n   122\tgaps. The event schema and the transactional invariant are defined\n   123\tin [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md); the event\n   124\tenvelope itself is fixed in\n   125\t[`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json).\n   126\t\n   127\t## 7. Objective and metrics schema\n   128\t\n   129\tAn experiment declares:\n   130\t\n   131\t- A **metrics schema** — the names and storage types of the metrics\n   132\t  its evaluator reports. Types are drawn from a small value-type set\n   133\t  (integer, real, text). Schema:\n   134\t  [`schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json`](schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json).\n   135\t- An **objective** — a scalar expression over the declared metrics,\n   136\t  plus a direction (maximize or minimize).\n   137\t\n   138\tConforming implementations MUST validate every reported metrics\n   139\tpayload against the experiment's declared schema. A metrics payload\n   140\tthat names a field absent from the schema, or that supplies a value\n   141\tincompatible with the declared type, MUST be rejected.\n   142\t\n   143\t## 8. Claim token\n   144\t\n   145\tA **claim token** is a value issued when a worker claims a task. It\n   146\taccompanies every subsequent request the worker makes about that\n   147\ttask (progress reports, completion submissions, artifact uploads).\n   148\tThe token is the system's mechanism for guaranteeing that two\n   149\tworkers cannot complete the same task: once a task is claimed, the\n   150\ttask store MUST reject any completion attempt that does not present\n   151\tthe same token. A task store MAY reclaim a task from a worker that\n   152\thas become unresponsive; reclamation invalidates the prior token.\n   153\t\n   154\tThe token's concrete shape is implementation-defined; the protocol\n   155\trequires only that it be unforgeable by other workers and bound to\n   156\ta single claim.\n   157\t\n   158\t## 9. Canonical trial lineage\n   159\t\n   160\tEDEN defines a git topology with three namespaces:\n   161\t\n   162\t- **`main`** — the experiment's starting point. Immutable during an\n   163\t  experiment.\n   164\t- **`trial/*`** — the canonical lineage, one commit per successfully\n   165\t  integrated trial. Written only by the integrator.\n   166\t- **`work/*`** — per-attempt worker branches. Multiple implementers\n   167\t  may write here concurrently; these branches are inputs to the\n   168\t  integrator and are not normative outputs.\n   169\t\n   170\tConsumers (evaluators, external observers, later experiments) read\n   171\tthe canonical lineage on `trial/*`. Chapter\n   172\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) defines the exact invariants\n   173\tthe integrator preserves across this topology.\n   174\t\n   175\t## 10. Storage components\n   176\t\n   177\tThree durable stores support an experiment:\n   178\t\n   179\t- **Task store** — holds tasks and their state. Provides atomic\n   180\t  claim, idempotent submission, and reclamation.\n   181\t- **Event log** — appends events in causal order. Provides subscribe\n   182\t  and replay.\n   183\t- **Artifact store** — holds per-trial files (plans, code diffs,\n   184\t  evaluator outputs, logs). Addressed by URI.\n   185\t\n   186\tThe protocol defines what each store's operations guarantee; it does\n   187\tnot define how they are implemented. Chapter\n   188\t[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) gives the durability and\n   189\tconsistency requirements each store must meet.\n   190\t\n   191\t## 11. Orchestrator\n   192\t\n   193\tThe **orchestrator** is the component that dispatches tasks to\n   194\tworkers and advances the protocol's state machine in response to\n   195\tsubmissions. It has no unique authority beyond what the protocol\n   196\tgrants it: in particular, it MUST persist state changes through the\n   197\ttask store and event log like any other component, and an\n   198\texperiment MAY run with multiple cooperating orchestrators provided\n   199\tthey share a conforming task store and event log.\n   200\t\n   201\t## 12. Relationships\n   202\t\n   203\tThe concepts above fit together as follows. A full specification\n   204\tchapter exists for each of the behavioral contracts implied here.\n   205\t\n   206\t- An **experiment** configures three **roles** and targets a **git\n   207\t  repository** with a starting commit.\n   208\t- An **orchestrator** dispatches **tasks** to role workers via the\n   209\t  **task store**.\n   210\t- A worker of role X claims a task, performs its role-specific\n   211\t  work, and submits; the submission produces a result object\n   212\t  (proposal, trial, metrics payload) persisted to the appropriate\n   213\t  store, and an **event** is appended to the event log.\n   214\t- The **integrator** consumes completed evaluations and advances\n   215\t  the **canonical trial lineage**.\n   216\t- The experiment terminates per its configured conditions; its\n   217\t  outcome is the sequence of trials on the canonical lineage.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:26:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_12","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba spec/v0/02-data-model.md | sed -n '1,260p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Data Model\n     2\t\n     3\tThis chapter defines the shape of the values that flow through the\n     4\tEDEN protocol. For each entity it gives the intent, the fields, the\n     5\trequired/optional split, and the structural invariants that MUST\n     6\thold independent of any behavioral contract.\n     7\t\n     8\tEach entity also has a companion JSON Schema under\n     9\t[`schemas/`](schemas/). The prose in this chapter and the schema are\n    10\tnormatively paired: an implementation's data MUST satisfy both. Where\n    11\tone is more restrictive than the other, the stricter constraint\n    12\tapplies.\n    13\t\n    14\tBehavioral concerns — when fields may change, which transitions are\n    15\tpermitted, what guarantees the surrounding store must offer — are\n    16\tspecified in Chapters [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md),\n    17\t[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md),\n    18\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md), and\n    19\t[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md).\n    20\t\n    21\t## 1. Common types\n    22\t\n    23\t### 1.1 Identifiers\n    24\t\n    25\tEntity identifiers (`task_id`, `trial_id`, `proposal_id`,\n    26\t`event_id`) are opaque strings. They MUST be unique within the store\n    27\tthat issues them. The protocol does not mandate a specific format;\n    28\timplementations MAY use integers rendered as strings, UUIDs, or\n    29\tother opaque values, so long as equality comparison is\n    30\twell-defined.\n    31\t\n    32\t### 1.2 Timestamps\n    33\t\n    34\tAll timestamps in protocol messages MUST be RFC 3339 strings in\n    35\tUTC with second-or-better precision (e.g. `2026-04-23T18:04:01Z`).\n    36\tOffset timestamps (e.g. `…-07:00`) MUST NOT be used on the wire:\n    37\tevery persisted timestamp MUST end with the literal `Z` suffix. A\n    38\tconsumer MUST NOT assume timestamps are strictly monotonic across\n    39\tentities; ordering is established by the event log, not by\n    40\ttimestamps.\n    41\t\n    42\t### 1.3 Metric values\n    43\t\n    44\tA metric value is one of: a JSON number (for `integer` or `real`\n    45\ttypes), a JSON string (for `text` type), or `null` (when the\n    46\tevaluator declined to report). Implementations MUST reject metric\n    47\tvalues whose JSON type does not match the declared metrics-schema\n    48\ttype.\n    49\t\n    50\tThe `integer` and `real` metric types both travel the wire as JSON\n    51\tnumbers; they are distinguished by what values each admits:\n    52\t\n    53\t- **`integer`** — a JSON number whose value is an integer (no\n    54\t  fractional component). Wire forms such as `3`, `-7`, or `1.0` are\n    55\t  accepted; `1.5` MUST be rejected. A conforming orchestrator MUST\n    56\t  validate this constraint when persisting a metrics payload.\n    57\t- **`real`** — any JSON number, including values with a fractional\n    58\t  component.\n    59\t- **`text`** — a JSON string.\n    60\t- **`null`** — allowed for any declared type when the evaluator\n    61\t  declined to report that metric.\n    62\t\n    63\t### 1.4 Git references\n    64\t\n    65\tCommit SHAs are lowercase hexadecimal strings of one of the two\n    66\tlengths git supports: **40 characters** (SHA-1) or **64 characters**\n    67\t(SHA-256). Every schema that embeds a commit SHA validates against\n    68\tthis exact pair of lengths; a v0 implementation MUST NOT emit SHAs\n    69\tof other lengths on the wire. Branch names follow the namespaces\n    70\tdefined in [`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9.\n    71\t\n    72\t### 1.5 URIs\n    73\t\n    74\tArtifact locations are URIs. The protocol does not mandate a scheme;\n    75\tfile URLs, S3 URLs, or custom schemes are permitted. A conforming\n    76\tartifact store MUST document which schemes it issues.\n    77\t\n    78\t## 2. Experiment config\n    79\t\n    80\tSchema: [`schemas/experiment-config.schema.json`](schemas/experiment-config.schema.json).\n    81\t\n    82\tAn experiment config is the declarative input to an experiment. It\n    83\tis authored by a user (or generated by tooling) and persisted\n    84\talongside the experiment.\n    85\t\n    86\tThe protocol defines only the **host-mechanism-independent core** of\n    87\tthe config — fields that every conforming implementation needs to\n    88\tobserve the same semantics. How roles are bound (local subprocess,\n    89\tHTTP service, in-process adapter, …) is outside this chapter's\n    90\tscope; role binding is specified alongside the role contracts in\n    91\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) (Phase 2). Until then, implementations\n    92\tMAY carry role-binding information as additional top-level fields\n    93\twithout violating this chapter.\n    94\t\n    95\t### 2.1 Required fields\n    96\t\n    97\t| Field | Type | Description |\n    98\t|---|---|---|\n    99\t| `parallel_trials` | integer ≥ 1 | Maximum number of trials that MAY be in flight simultaneously. |\n   100\t| `max_trials` | integer ≥ 1 | Hard ceiling on the number of trials attempted. |\n   101\t| `max_wall_time` | duration | Hard ceiling on elapsed wall time. Format: a positive integer followed by one of `s`, `m`, `h`, `d`. |\n   102\t| `metrics_schema` | object | A metrics schema (§6). MUST be non-empty. |\n   103\t| `objective` | object | An objective spec (§2.2). |\n   104\t\n   105\t### 2.2 Objective spec\n   106\t\n   107\t| Field | Type | Description |\n   108\t|---|---|---|\n   109\t| `expr` | string | A scalar expression over fields of `metrics_schema`. |\n   110\t| `direction` | string | Either `\"maximize\"` or `\"minimize\"`. |\n   111\t\n   112\tThe expression language is implementation-defined but MUST treat\n   113\tmetric field names as the free variables. A conforming orchestrator\n   114\tMUST reject an objective expression that references a name absent\n   115\tfrom `metrics_schema`.\n   116\t\n   117\t### 2.3 Optional fields\n   118\t\n   119\t| Field | Type | Default | Description |\n   120\t|---|---|---|---|\n   121\t| `convergence_window` | integer ≥ 1 | (none) | Terminate if the objective has not improved in this many trials. |\n   122\t| `target_condition` | string | (none) | A condition over metrics; when satisfied, the experiment terminates early. |\n   123\t\n   124\t### 2.4 Additional top-level fields\n   125\t\n   126\tImplementations MAY define additional top-level fields for role\n   127\tbinding and implementation-specific tuning. The experiment-config\n   128\tschema does not restrict unknown top-level fields; a conforming\n   129\torchestrator MUST NOT reject a config solely because it carries\n   130\tfields this chapter does not define. This forward-compatibility\n   131\trule is intentional: it lets role-binding semantics land in\n   132\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) without breaking previously-valid\n   133\tconfigs.\n   134\t\n   135\tAn implementation SHOULD document which additional top-level fields\n   136\tit consumes.\n   137\t\n   138\t## 3. Task\n   139\t\n   140\tSchema: [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json).\n   141\t\n   142\tA task is a work item. It is persisted in the task store and\n   143\tadvances through a state machine defined in\n   144\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md); this section defines\n   145\tonly its shape.\n   146\t\n   147\t### 3.1 Fields\n   148\t\n   149\t| Field | Required | Type | Description |\n   150\t|---|---|---|---|\n   151\t| `task_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier. |\n   152\t| `kind` | yes | string | One of `\"plan\"`, `\"implement\"`, `\"evaluate\"`. |\n   153\t| `state` | yes | string | See §3.2. |\n   154\t| `payload` | yes | object | Kind-specific payload (§3.3). |\n   155\t| `claim` | no | object | Present iff the task is currently claimed (§3.4). |\n   156\t| `created_at` | yes | timestamp | When the task was created. |\n   157\t| `updated_at` | yes | timestamp | Most recent state change. |\n   158\t\n   159\t### 3.2 State values\n   160\t\n   161\tThe `state` field MUST be one of `\"pending\"`, `\"claimed\"`,\n   162\t`\"submitted\"`, `\"completed\"`, `\"failed\"`. The legal transitions\n   163\tbetween these states are defined in\n   164\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The data model\n   165\tconstrains only that the value be one of the listed strings.\n   166\t\n   167\t### 3.3 Payload shape\n   168\t\n   169\tThe `payload` object's shape depends on `kind`.\n   170\t\n   171\t- `\"plan\"` — MUST contain `experiment_id` (string). MAY contain\n   172\t  planner-specific hints.\n   173\t- `\"implement\"` — MUST contain `proposal_id` (string) referring to\n   174\t  a proposal with `state == \"ready\"` at the time of dispatch.\n   175\t- `\"evaluate\"` — MUST contain `trial_id` (string) referring to a\n   176\t  trial with `status == \"starting\"` and a `commit_sha` set.\n   177\t\n   178\t### 3.4 Claim object\n   179\t\n   180\t| Field | Required | Type | Description |\n   181\t|---|---|---|---|\n   182\t| `token` | yes | string | Opaque unforgeable claim token. |\n   183\t| `worker_id` | yes | string | Opaque identifier for the claiming worker. |\n   184\t| `claimed_at` | yes | timestamp | When the claim was issued. |\n   185\t| `expires_at` | no | timestamp | When the task store MAY reclaim. |\n   186\t\n   187\tThe `claim` field MUST be present when `state ∈ {\"claimed\",\n   188\t\"submitted\"}` and MUST NOT be present for any other state. The\n   189\tclaim is retained through `\"submitted\"` so that the task protocol\n   190\tcan authorize resubmission by token\n   191\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2); it is cleared\n   192\ton reclamation and on the terminal transition to `\"completed\"` or\n   193\t`\"failed\"`. The schema in\n   194\t[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json) enforces this\n   195\tpresence rule.\n   196\t\n   197\t## 4. Event\n   198\t\n   199\tSchema: [`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json).\n   200\t\n   201\tAn event records a state change. Events are immutable once appended.\n   202\t\n   203\t### 4.1 Envelope\n   204\t\n   205\tEvery event MUST provide this envelope:\n   206\t\n   207\t| Field | Required | Type | Description |\n   208\t|---|---|---|---|\n   209\t| `event_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier within the log. |\n   210\t| `type` | yes | string | A dotted type name, e.g. `task.claimed`, `trial.evaluated`. |\n   211\t| `occurred_at` | yes | timestamp | When the state change happened. |\n   212\t| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment this event belongs to. |\n   213\t| `data` | yes | object | Type-specific payload (§4.2). |\n   214\t\n   215\t### 4.2 Type-specific payload\n   216\t\n   217\tEach event `type` corresponds to a shape under `data`. The full\n   218\tenumeration of types is defined in\n   219\t[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md); at this layer, the\n   220\tdata model requires only that `data` be a JSON object and that its\n   221\tshape match the registered schema for the event's `type`.\n   222\t\n   223\t### 4.3 Ordering\n   224\t\n   225\tEvents within a single experiment MUST form a total order consistent\n   226\twith causality: if event B describes a state change that depended\n   227\ton a state recorded by event A, A MUST precede B in the log. The\n   228\tordering mechanism is a storage concern\n   229\t([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).\n   230\t\n   231\t## 5. Proposal\n   232\t\n   233\tSchema: [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json).\n   234\t\n   235\tA proposal is the planner's output.\n   236\t\n   237\t### 5.1 Fields\n   238\t\n   239\t| Field | Required | Type | Description |\n   240\t|---|---|---|---|\n   241\t| `proposal_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier. |\n   242\t| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment this proposal belongs to. |\n   243\t| `slug` | yes | string | Human-readable short label. MUST match `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*$`. |\n   244\t| `priority` | yes | number | Ordering hint; higher dispatches earlier. |\n   245\t| `parent_commits` | yes | array of SHA | One or more commit SHAs the proposal is based on. |\n   246\t| `artifacts_uri` | yes | string (URI) | Where the planner's output documents live. |\n   247\t| `state` | yes | string | One of `\"drafting\"`, `\"ready\"`, `\"dispatched\"`, `\"completed\"`. `\"completed\"` is terminal and means the implementer's attempt has finished (successfully or not); the trial's `status` records the outcome (see [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §7). |\n   248\t| `created_at` | yes | timestamp | When the proposal was created. |\n   249\t\n   250\t### 5.2 Parent commits\n   251\t\n   252\t`parent_commits` MUST contain at least one SHA. Each SHA MUST name a\n   253\tcommit reachable from the experiment's starting commit on `main` or\n   254\tfrom an already-completed trial on the canonical trial lineage.\n   255\t\n   256\t## 6. Metrics schema\n   257\t\n   258\tSchema: [`schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json`](schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json).\n   259\t\n   260\tA metrics schema is a mapping from metric name to storage type. It\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:28:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_13","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba spec/v0/03-roles.md | sed -n '1,280p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Role Contracts\n     2\t\n     3\tThis chapter defines the four role contracts that a conforming EDEN\n     4\tdeployment implements: **planner**, **implementer**, **evaluator**,\n     5\tand **integrator**. For each, it pins what the role observes, what\n     6\tit produces, and which invariants its output MUST honor.\n     7\t\n     8\tA role contract does **not** specify how the role is hosted. A\n     9\tconforming planner MAY run as a local subprocess, a long-lived\n    10\tservice reached over HTTP, an in-process adapter, or a human at a\n    11\tkeyboard. The protocol constrains the *observable effects* a role\n    12\thas on the task store, the event log, and the artifact store; how a\n    13\trole is invoked is a deployment concern and is deferred to a\n    14\tlater-phase *role-binding* specification. Within v0, implementations\n    15\tMAY document their own binding by defining additional top-level\n    16\tfields on the experiment config per\n    17\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4.\n    18\t\n    19\tThe behavioral state machine that all worker roles participate in —\n    20\tclaim, execute, submit, reclaim — is defined in\n    21\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). This chapter describes\n    22\tonly the *role-specific* part of each worker's job: what it reads,\n    23\twhat it produces, and what guarantees its output must carry. Where a\n    24\trule depends on a state-machine transition, this chapter cites the\n    25\ttransition by name and points to Chapter 04 for its semantics.\n    26\t\n    27\t## 1. Common worker-role lifecycle\n    28\t\n    29\tThe planner, implementer, and evaluator are **worker roles**: they\n    30\tparticipate in the task protocol as consumers. Each worker role\n    31\tprogresses through the same outer lifecycle for every task it\n    32\thandles:\n    33\t\n    34\t1. **Discover.** The worker becomes aware of a pending task whose\n    35\t   `kind` matches its role. Mechanisms include polling the task\n    36\t   store, subscribing to an event stream, or receiving a dispatch\n    37\t   call — all are permitted; none are mandated.\n    38\t2. **Claim.** The worker atomically moves the task from `pending`\n    39\t   to `claimed` and receives a claim token. Semantics are in\n    40\t   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.\n    41\t3. **Execute.** The worker performs its role-specific work using the\n    42\t   task payload and any context the protocol grants it (§2–§4\n    43\t   below). The worker MAY report progress. It MUST NOT mutate any\n    44\t   protocol-owned object other than (a) the task it holds a claim\n    45\t   on and (b) the outputs its role is specified to produce (§2.2,\n    46\t   §3.2, §4.2). The restrictions in §1.2 apply to every worker role\n    47\t   in addition to the per-role rules below.\n    48\t4. **Submit.** The worker atomically moves the task from `claimed`\n    49\t   to `submitted`, presenting its claim token and a result (§2–§4\n    50\t   below). A submitted task then advances to `completed` or `failed`\n    51\t   per the rules in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.\n    52\t5. **Release on reclaim.** If the task store reclaims the task\n    53\t   before submit (task store policy per\n    54\t   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5), the worker's\n    55\t   token is invalidated. A worker that discovers its token has been\n    56\t   invalidated MUST NOT subsequently attempt to submit against that\n    57\t   task; it MUST discard any partial result.\n    58\t\n    59\tIntegrator behavior does **not** follow this worker-role lifecycle.\n    60\tThe integrator is event-driven and is defined separately in §5 below\n    61\tand in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n    62\t\n    63\t### 1.1 What a worker MAY assume about its inputs\n    64\t\n    65\tBefore a task is dispatched to a worker-role queue, the orchestrator\n    66\tMUST ensure the task's payload references (proposal, trial,\n    67\tparent commits) satisfy the dispatch preconditions listed per role\n    68\tbelow. A worker MAY therefore rely on those preconditions and is not\n    69\trequired to re-validate them. A worker that nevertheless discovers a\n    70\tviolation (e.g. a referenced proposal has been retracted) MUST fail\n    71\tthe task with an error status; it MUST NOT repair store state\n    72\titself.\n    73\t\n    74\t### 1.2 What a worker MUST NOT do\n    75\t\n    76\t- A worker MUST NOT write to any protocol-owned object other than\n    77\t  the task it holds a claim on and the outputs that task's role is\n    78\t  defined to produce (§2–§4).\n    79\t- A worker MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage (`trial/*`);\n    80\t  that is the integrator's exclusive authority\n    81\t  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9,\n    82\t  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).\n    83\t- A worker MUST NOT advance a task's state beyond `submitted`.\n    84\t  Transitions to `completed` or `failed` are the orchestrator's\n    85\t  responsibility ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4).\n    86\t\n    87\t## 2. Planner\n    88\t\n    89\tThe planner proposes what to try. Every planner invocation consumes\n    90\tone `plan` task and produces zero or more proposals.\n    91\t\n    92\t### 2.1 Inputs\n    93\t\n    94\tA planner receives:\n    95\t\n    96\t- The task object (`kind == \"plan\"`,\n    97\t  `payload.experiment_id == E`).\n    98\t- Read access to the experiment config for `E`\n    99\t  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2), including the\n   100\t  `metrics_schema` and `objective`.\n   101\t- Read access to the set of proposals and trials already persisted\n   102\t  for `E`. The mechanism (direct store read, orchestrator-supplied\n   103\t  snapshot, event-log replay) is a role-binding concern.\n   104\t\n   105\tA planner MAY additionally use any context the experiment config\n   106\tsurfaces to it via role-binding-specific fields. The protocol does\n   107\tnot require the planner to read beyond what is listed above.\n   108\t\n   109\t### 2.2 Outputs\n   110\t\n   111\tFor each proposal the planner produces, it MUST:\n   112\t\n   113\t1. Persist a proposal object (via the protocol's proposal store —\n   114\t   mechanism is binding-specific) that validates against\n   115\t   [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json),\n   116\t   with `experiment_id == E` and `state == \"drafting\"`.\n   117\t2. Upload any artifacts (plan text, rationale, supporting files) to\n   118\t   the artifact store and populate the proposal's `artifacts_uri`.\n   119\t3. Transition the proposal's `state` to `\"ready\"` once the proposal\n   120\t   is dispatchable. The transition from `drafting` to `ready`\n   121\t   signals that the proposal's metadata is stable and that the\n   122\t   implementer MAY consume it.\n   123\t\n   124\tA planner MAY create multiple proposals under a single `plan` task.\n   125\tA planner MAY also produce zero proposals if it has no viable\n   126\tchange to suggest; the task still completes normally (see §2.4).\n   127\t\n   128\t### 2.3 Proposal invariants\n   129\t\n   130\tThe planner MUST honor the structural invariants of proposals\n   131\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5):\n   132\t\n   133\t- `parent_commits` MUST contain at least one SHA that names a\n   134\t  commit reachable from either the experiment's starting commit on\n   135\t  `main` or a completed trial on the canonical lineage\n   136\t  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9).\n   137\t- `slug` MUST match the documented pattern.\n   138\t- `priority` is the planner's own ordering hint; higher values\n   139\t  SHOULD dispatch earlier.\n   140\t\n   141\t### 2.4 Submission\n   142\t\n   143\tWhen its work on a `plan` task is complete, the planner MUST submit\n   144\tthe task (§1 step 4). The submission payload MUST include:\n   145\t\n   146\t- `proposal_ids` — the set of identifiers of every proposal the\n   147\t  planner created under this task. Order is not significant;\n   148\t  dispatch ordering is determined by each proposal's `priority`\n   149\t  field ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1). MAY be\n   150\t  empty.\n   151\t- `status` — one of `\"success\"` (the planner completed normally,\n   152\t  regardless of proposal count) or `\"error\"` (the planner could\n   153\t  not complete; any partially-written proposals MUST remain in\n   154\t  `\"drafting\"` state and MUST NOT be dispatched).\n   155\t\n   156\tA planner MUST NOT submit a `plan` task while any of its\n   157\treferenced proposals is still in `\"drafting\"` state.\n   158\t\n   159\t## 3. Implementer\n   160\t\n   161\tThe implementer realizes a proposal as a working-tree change on a\n   162\tper-trial branch.\n   163\t\n   164\t### 3.1 Inputs\n   165\t\n   166\tAn implementer receives:\n   167\t\n   168\t- The task object (`kind == \"implement\"`,\n   169\t  `payload.proposal_id == P`).\n   170\t- Read access to the proposal `P`. The orchestrator MUST ensure\n   171\t  `P.state == \"ready\"` before dispatch.\n   172\t- Read and write access to the git repository associated with\n   173\t  `P.experiment_id`, at a revision reachable from\n   174\t  `P.parent_commits`. How the repository is located and how\n   175\t  access is granted is a role-binding concern (§6,\n   176\t  [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4); the protocol\n   177\t  constrains only that the implementer's writes land under\n   178\t  `work/*` and that no commit is introduced whose history does\n   179\t  not descend from `P.parent_commits` (§3.3).\n   180\t- Read access to the artifacts at `P.artifacts_uri`.\n   181\t\n   182\t### 3.2 Outputs\n   183\t\n   184\tThe implementer produces a **trial** object\n   185\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7). It MUST:\n   186\t\n   187\t1. Persist a trial with `status == \"starting\"`,\n   188\t   `experiment_id == P.experiment_id`, `proposal_id == P`, and\n   189\t   `parent_commits == P.parent_commits`, before making any\n   190\t   repository write observable to other roles.\n   191\t2. Write the implementation on a branch under `work/*`\n   192\t   ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9) whose parent(s) are the\n   193\t   proposal's `parent_commits`. Set the trial's `branch` field to\n   194\t   this branch name.\n   195\t3. On successful completion, set the trial's `commit_sha` to the\n   196\t   tip of the `work/*` branch. Optionally set `artifacts_uri` and\n   197\t   `description`.\n   198\t\n   199\tThe implementer MUST NOT write to `trial/*` or `main`; those are\n   200\towned by the integrator. The `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial\n   201\tMUST NOT be set by the implementer.\n   202\t\n   203\t### 3.3 Worker-branch invariants\n   204\t\n   205\t- The `work/*` branch MUST be unique to this trial. Two trials\n   206\t  MUST NOT share a worker branch name.\n   207\t- Every commit on the worker branch MUST be reachable from the\n   208\t  declared `parent_commits`. An implementer MUST NOT introduce\n   209\t  commits whose history does not descend from the proposal's\n   210\t  declared parents.\n   211\t- The implementer MAY produce multiple commits on the worker\n   212\t  branch. The evaluator consumes only the tip (`commit_sha`); the\n   213\t  integrator's squash rule is defined in\n   214\t  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n   215\t\n   216\t### 3.4 Submission\n   217\t\n   218\tThe implementer submits with:\n   219\t\n   220\t- `trial_id` — the trial it created.\n   221\t- `status` — one of:\n   222\t  - `\"success\"` — the `work/*` branch tip is `commit_sha` and is\n   223\t    ready to be evaluated.\n   224\t  - `\"error\"` — the implementer could not realize the proposal.\n   225\t    The trial MUST be persisted with `status == \"error\"`. No\n   226\t    evaluate task is dispatched against an errored trial.\n   227\t- `commit_sha` — required when `status == \"success\"`; MUST equal\n   228\t  the tip of the worker branch.\n   229\t\n   230\tA resubmission of the same `implement` task MUST be idempotent: a\n   231\tduplicate submit presenting the same `trial_id` and `commit_sha`\n   232\tMUST be accepted without side effect. A duplicate submit that\n   233\tdisagrees with the already-recorded result MUST be rejected\n   234\t([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2).\n   235\t\n   236\t## 4. Evaluator\n   237\t\n   238\tThe evaluator measures a completed trial against the experiment's\n   239\tmetrics schema.\n   240\t\n   241\t### 4.1 Inputs\n   242\t\n   243\tAn evaluator receives:\n   244\t\n   245\t- The task object (`kind == \"evaluate\"`,\n   246\t  `payload.trial_id == T`).\n   247\t- Read access to the trial `T`. The orchestrator MUST ensure\n   248\t  `T.status == \"starting\"` and `T.commit_sha` is set before\n   249\t  dispatch.\n   250\t- Read access to the git repository at `T.commit_sha` on the\n   251\t  worker branch (`T.branch`). Repository location and access are\n   252\t  a role-binding concern, as for the implementer (§3.1).\n   253\t- Read access to the experiment's `metrics_schema` and\n   254\t  `objective`.\n   255\t\n   256\t### 4.2 Outputs\n   257\t\n   258\tThe evaluator MUST:\n   259\t\n   260\t1. Produce a `metrics` object whose keys are a subset of the\n   261\t   declared `metrics_schema` keys and whose values satisfy the\n   262\t   per-metric type rules\n   263\t   ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3, §7.2).\n   264\t2. Optionally upload supporting artifacts (logs, captured outputs,\n   265\t   diagnostic files).\n   266\t\n   267\tThe evaluator MUST NOT modify the worker branch or any\n   268\tprotocol-owned mutable state other than the trial fields the\n   269\tsubmission writes (§4.4) and the task it holds a claim on. In\n   270\tparticular, the evaluator MUST NOT write to the trial's\n   271\t`completed_at`, `metrics`, `artifacts_uri`, `description`, or\n   272\t`status` directly; those writes are performed by the orchestrator\n   273\twhen the submitted task reaches its terminal state (§4.4,\n   274\t[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).\n   275\t\n   276\t### 4.3 Non-interference\n   277\t\n   278\tThe evaluator reads the repository at the trial's worker-branch tip\n   279\tbut MUST NOT push, rewrite, or delete that branch, and MUST NOT\n   280\twrite to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:30:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_14","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md | sed -n '1,320p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Task Protocol\n     2\t\n     3\tThis chapter specifies the behavioral contract for tasks: the state\n     4\tmachine they advance through, the claim tokens that grant exclusive\n     5\texecution, the idempotency rules for submission, and the\n     6\treclamation policy that bounds worker liveness. It pins *what*\n     7\tobservable behavior a conforming task store and a conforming\n     8\torchestrator must exhibit; it does not pin *how* either is\n     9\timplemented.\n    10\t\n    11\tThe task object's shape is defined in\n    12\t[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3; its JSON Schema is\n    13\t[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json). The role\n    14\tcontracts that drive the transitions defined here are in\n    15\t[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md).\n    16\t\n    17\t## 1. State machine\n    18\t\n    19\t### 1.1 States\n    20\t\n    21\tA task's `state` field takes one of five values. Each has a\n    22\twell-defined meaning in the state machine:\n    23\t\n    24\t| State | Meaning |\n    25\t|---|---|\n    26\t| `pending` | The task has been created and is eligible to be claimed. No worker currently holds it. |\n    27\t| `claimed` | A worker holds an active claim on the task and is expected to execute it. The task carries a `claim` object. |\n    28\t| `submitted` | The claiming worker has presented a result. The task awaits processing by the orchestrator. |\n    29\t| `completed` | The orchestrator has accepted the result. The task is terminal. |\n    30\t| `failed` | The orchestrator has rejected the result, the worker declared failure, or a policy limit (e.g. retry budget) has been exceeded. The task is terminal. |\n    31\t\n    32\t`completed` and `failed` are **terminal**: a conforming task store\n    33\tMUST NOT transition a task out of either state.\n    34\t\n    35\t### 1.2 Transitions\n    36\t\n    37\tThe permitted transitions are exactly:\n    38\t\n    39\t```text\n    40\t          claim                 submit                accept\n    41\tpending ─────────▶ claimed ─────────────▶ submitted ─────────▶ completed\n    42\t   ▲                 │                       │\n    43\t   │ reclaim         │ reclaim               │ reject\n    44\t   └─────────────────┴───────────────────────┤           ──▶ failed\n    45\t                                             │\n    46\t```\n    47\t\n    48\t- **claim** — `pending → claimed`. Atomic. Issues a fresh claim\n    49\t  token. §3.\n    50\t- **submit** — `claimed → submitted`. Requires presenting the\n    51\t  current claim token. Idempotent under identical payload.\n    52\t  Performed for every completed attempt, whether the worker\n    53\t  declares success or failure. §4.\n    54\t- **reclaim** — `claimed → pending` or `submitted → pending`.\n    55\t  Invalidates the prior claim token. §5.\n    56\t- **accept** — `submitted → completed`. Orchestrator-initiated.\n    57\t  §4.3.\n    58\t- **reject** — `submitted → failed`. Orchestrator-initiated,\n    59\t  including when the worker's own submission declared failure.\n    60\t  §4.3.\n    61\t\n    62\tAny transition not listed above MUST be rejected by the task store.\n    63\tA conforming task store MUST treat concurrent transition attempts\n    64\tas serialized: whichever attempt wins the race succeeds, and the\n    65\tothers MUST receive a well-defined rejection that lets the caller\n    66\tdistinguish \"invalid\" from \"raced\".\n    67\t\n    68\t### 1.3 Event emission\n    69\t\n    70\tEvery state transition MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event\n    71\tappended to the event log, atomically with the state change. The\n    72\tevent types (e.g. `task.claimed`, `task.submitted`, `task.failed`)\n    73\tand the transactional invariant are specified in\n    74\t[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md). This chapter does\n    75\tnot enumerate the event shapes; it requires only that they exist\n    76\tand that no state change is observable without its event.\n    77\t\n    78\t## 2. Creating a task\n    79\t\n    80\tA task enters the system in the `pending` state.
