A conforming\n    81\torchestrator MUST:\n    82\t\n    83\t- Populate `task_id`, `kind`, `state = \"pending\"`, `payload`,\n    84\t  `created_at`, `updated_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md)\n    85\t  §3.1).\n    86\t- Ensure the payload satisfies the dispatch preconditions for the\n    87\t  task's `kind`:\n    88\t  - `plan` — `payload.experiment_id` names an experiment the\n    89\t    planner is authorized to work on.\n    90\t  - `implement` — `payload.proposal_id` names a proposal with\n    91\t    `state == \"ready\"` at creation time. Creating the implement\n    92\t    task and transitioning the proposal from `\"ready\"` to\n    93\t    `\"dispatched\"` MUST be atomic; a proposal's `\"dispatched\"`\n    94\t    state signals that exactly one implement task has been\n    95\t    created for it.\n    96\t  - `evaluate` — `payload.trial_id` names a trial with\n    97\t    `status == \"starting\"` and `commit_sha` set at creation time.\n    98\t- Emit the corresponding `task.created` event (\n    99\t  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) atomically with\n   100\t  the task insert.\n   101\t\n   102\tA worker MAY rely on these preconditions ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md)\n   103\t§1.1). A task created without them is a protocol violation by the\n   104\torchestrator.\n   105\t\n   106\t## 3. Claim\n   107\t\n   108\t### 3.1 Atomicity\n   109\t\n   110\tThe `pending → claimed` transition MUST be atomic with respect to\n   111\tcompeting claim attempts: at most one worker MAY succeed for any\n   112\tgiven task. A conforming task store MUST provide this guarantee as\n   113\tpart of its durability contract\n   114\t([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).\n   115\t\n   116\t### 3.2 Claim token\n   117\t\n   118\tA successful claim issues a `claim` object\n   119\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4) containing:\n   120\t\n   121\t- `token` — an opaque value that MUST be:\n   122\t  - **unique** — no two distinct claims (on the same or different\n   123\t    tasks) share the same token within an experiment;\n   124\t  - **unforgeable** — a worker that did not receive the token MUST\n   125\t    NOT be able to construct a byte-equal value with practical\n   126\t    effort. Implementations commonly use a cryptographically\n   127\t    random value; the protocol does not mandate the mechanism.\n   128\t- `worker_id` — an identifier the worker supplies at claim time.\n   129\t  The task store MAY record it for audit but MUST NOT use it as a\n   130\t  substitute for the token when authorizing subsequent operations.\n   131\t- `claimed_at` — the time the claim was issued.\n   132\t- `expires_at` — OPTIONAL. If present, the task store MAY reclaim\n   133\t  the task when the current time exceeds this value (§5).\n   134\t\n   135\t### 3.3 Authorization by token\n   136\t\n   137\tEvery subsequent operation on a claimed task (progress update,\n   138\tsubmit, release) MUST present the current claim token. The task\n   139\tstore MUST reject any such operation whose token does not match the\n   140\ttoken currently recorded on the task. This is the sole mechanism\n   141\tthe protocol relies on to guarantee single-writer access to a\n   142\tclaimed task.\n   143\t\n   144\t### 3.4 No re-claim while claimed\n   145\t\n   146\tA conforming task store MUST reject a claim attempt against a task\n   147\twhose `state` is not `pending`. In particular, a claimed task\n   148\tcannot be \"re-claimed\" by a different worker; the prior claim must\n   149\tfirst be released (by submission) or invalidated (by reclamation).\n   150\t\n   151\t## 4. Submit\n   152\t\n   153\t### 4.1 Submission operation\n   154\t\n   155\tA submit operation requires:\n   156\t\n   157\t- The `task_id`.\n   158\t- The current claim `token`.\n   159\t- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind`\n   160\t  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).\n   161\t\n   162\tA successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` atomically\n   163\twith persisting the result payload. The terminal transition to\n   164\t`completed` or `failed` is the orchestrator's subsequent step\n   165\t(§4.3), even when the submission itself declared failure.\n   166\t\n   167\t### 4.2 Idempotency\n   168\t\n   169\tA resubmission is a second submit carrying the token recorded on\n   170\tthe task's `claim` object (which is retained through `submitted`\n   171\tper [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). A resubmit against\n   172\ta task whose claim has been cleared — because the task was\n   173\treclaimed or reached a terminal state — MUST be rejected\n   174\tregardless of the presented token (§4.4, §5.2).\n   175\t\n   176\tWhen the token matches, the task store MUST handle the\n   177\tresubmission as follows:\n   178\t\n   179\t- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to\n   180\t  the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and\n   181\t  MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. \"Content\n   182\t  equivalence\" means the normative fields identified per role\n   183\t  agree:\n   184\t  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (compared as sets; order\n   185\t    is not significant per [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4) and\n   186\t    `status`.\n   187\t  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha`\n   188\t    (when present).\n   189\t  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as\n   190\t    JSON values; key order does not matter).\n   191\t- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent,\n   192\t  the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is\n   193\t  the committed result.\n   194\t\n   195\tThis rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a\n   196\tnetwork or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or\n   197\tcorrupting the recorded result.\n   198\t\n   199\t### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal\n   200\t\n   201\tA submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions\n   202\tto a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:\n   203\t\n   204\t- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's\n   205\t  `status` is `\"success\"` and the result satisfies the role's\n   206\t  success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a\n   207\t  reachable `commit_sha`).\n   208\t- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:\n   209\t  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed\n   210\t    payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).\n   211\t  - The worker itself declared failure:\n   212\t    - `plan` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`.\n   213\t    - `implement` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`.\n   214\t    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`\n   215\t      AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to\n   216\t      `\"error\"`.\n   217\t    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"eval_error\"` →\n   218\t      `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain\n   219\t      `\"starting\"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad;\n   220\t      only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh\n   221\t      `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the\n   222\t      protocol does not mandate automatic retry. If the\n   223\t      orchestrator's policy exhausts retries for this trial\n   224\t      (or the operator abandons it), the orchestrator MUST\n   225\t      transition the trial's `status` from `\"starting\"` to\n   226\t      `\"eval_error\"`, at which point the trial status is\n   227\t      terminal.\n   228\t\n   229\tEvery terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event\n   230\tin the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) and\n   231\tMUST clear the task's `claim` object\n   232\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). The orchestrator MAY\n   233\timplement the `claimed → submitted → completed` (or `failed`)\n   234\tsequence as two distinct persisted states or as a single store\n   235\ttransaction whose event stream includes both transitions, but\n   236\tsubscribers MUST observe both events in order.\n   237\t\n   238\t### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited\n   239\t\n   240\tOnce a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its\n   241\ttask fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task\n   242\tstore chooses to expose outside the normative schema\n   243\t([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a\n   244\tresubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of\n   245\tcontent equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal\n   246\ttransition is no longer valid.\n   247\t\n   248\t## 5. Reclamation\n   249\t\n   250\t### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted\n   251\t\n   252\tA conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from\n   253\t`claimed` back to `pending` — in any of the following cases:\n   254\t\n   255\t- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed.\n   256\t- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.\n   257\t- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker\n   258\t  is unreachable.\n   259\t\n   260\tA task in `submitted` state MAY be reclaimed **only** by an\n   261\texplicit operator action; automatic reclaim (expires_at, health\n   262\tpolicy) MUST NOT apply to `submitted` tasks. After submit the\n   263\tworker's reachability is no longer a relevant liveness signal; the\n   264\torchestrator is expected to advance the task to a terminal state\n   265\t(§4.3).\n   266\t\n   267\tA task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.\n   268\t\n   269\t### 5.2 Effect of reclamation\n   270\t\n   271\tReclamation:\n   272\t\n   273\t1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations\n   274\t   presenting it MUST be rejected.\n   275\t2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.\n   276\t3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.\n   277\t4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log\n   278\t   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically\n   279\t   with the state change.\n   280\t\n   281\tAny partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is\n   282\tno longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker\n   283\tcreated store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting\n   284\ttrials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are\n   285\tsubject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not\n   286\tdelete them.\n   287\t\n   288\t### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation\n   289\t\n   290\tA worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its\n   291\ttask's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its\n   292\ttoken has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task\n   293\tand MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).\n   294\t\n   295\t### 5.4 Reclamation effect on role outputs\n   296\t\n   297\tWhen a reclaimed task had created role-owned objects during its\n   298\tprior execution, the orchestrator MUST reconcile those objects as\n   299\tfollows:\n   300\t\n   301\t- **Implement reclamation.** If the prior execution created a\n   302\t  trial with `status == \"starting\"`, the orchestrator MUST\n   303\t  transition that trial's `status` to `\"error\"` atomically with\n   304\t  the reclaim event. A subsequent re-claim of the implement task\n   305\t  produces a **new** trial; starting trials are never shared\n   306\t  across implementer attempts.\n   307\t- **Plan reclamation.** If the prior execution persisted\n   308\t  proposals in `\"drafting\"` state, those proposals remain in\n   309\t  `\"drafting\"` and are not dispatched. Implementations MAY expose\n   310\t  them to operators for inspection or removal; the task protocol\n   311\t  does not mandate automatic cleanup.\n   312\t- **Evaluate reclamation.** The trial the task referenced is\n   313\t  unchanged by reclamation (it was produced by the implementer,\n   314\t  not the evaluator).\n   315\t\n   316\t## 6. Ordering and concurrency\n   317\t\n   318\t### 6.1 Per-task serialization\n   319\t\n   320\tAll transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

Any side effects of evaluation\n   281\t(test caches, build outputs, captured logs) MUST NOT be observable\n   282\tthrough any protocol-owned store, ref, or artifact location except\n   283\tvia the evaluator's declared outputs (§4.2).\n   284\t\n   285\t### 4.4 Submission\n   286\t\n   287\tThe evaluator submits with:\n   288\t\n   289\t- `trial_id` — the trial it evaluated.\n   290\t- `status` — one of:\n   291\t  - `\"success\"` — the trial ran and produced metrics.\n   292\t  - `\"error\"` — the trial could not be evaluated for reasons\n   293\t    attributable to the trial's own code (build failure, test\n   294\t    failure, etc.). The evaluator MAY still include partial\n   295\t    metrics.\n   296\t  - `\"eval_error\"` — the evaluator itself failed for reasons\n   297\t    unrelated to the trial's code (infrastructure fault,\n   298\t    evaluator bug). While a fresh evaluate task MAY still be\n   299\t    created for this trial, the trial's status MUST remain\n   300\t    `\"starting\"`. If the orchestrator's retry policy is\n   301\t    exhausted (or the operator abandons evaluation), the\n   302\t    orchestrator MUST transition the trial's status to\n   303\t    `\"eval_error\"`, making that status terminal for the trial\n   304\t    ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).\n   305\t- `metrics` — the metrics object described in §4.2. MAY be absent\n   306\t  when `status == \"eval_error\"`.\n   307\t- `artifacts_uri` — OPTIONAL. A URI the evaluator uploaded\n   308\t  supporting artifacts to.\n   309\t\n   310\tOn a `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed` transition\n   311\t(per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3), the\n   312\torchestrator MUST write the following trial fields atomically with\n   313\tthe event:\n   314\t\n   315\t- `status` — the trial status implied by the submission: `\"success\"`\n   316\t  when the submission's `status == \"success\"`; `\"error\"` when the\n   317\t  submission's `status == \"error\"`; unchanged from `\"starting\"`\n   318\t  when the submission's `status == \"eval_error\"` (see §4.4 above\n   319\t  for the terminal-retry case).\n   320\t- `metrics` — set to the submission's `metrics` when\n   321\t  `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When `status == \"eval_error\"`\n   322\t  the orchestrator MUST NOT write `metrics` on the trial; any\n   323\t  submission-carried `metrics` is discarded.\n   324\t- `artifacts_uri` — set to the submission's `artifacts_uri` when\n   325\t  provided and `status ∈ {\"success\", \"error\"}`. When\n   326\t  `status == \"eval_error\"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write\n   327\t  `artifacts_uri` on the trial; any submission-carried\n   328\t  `artifacts_uri` is discarded. (An evaluator that wishes to\n   329\t  retain diagnostic artifacts from a failed attempt MAY reference\n   330\t  them in the `task.failed` event for that evaluate task; that\n   331\t  channel is defined in\n   332\t  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md).)\n   333\t- `completed_at` — set to the time of the terminal trial\n   334\t  transition, i.e. written exactly once, when the trial's status\n   335\t  leaves `\"starting\"` (either on a `\"success\"`/`\"error\"`\n   336\t  submission, or on the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"`\n   337\t  transition). Intermediate `eval_error` submissions MUST NOT\n   338\t  advance `completed_at`.\n   339\t\n   340\tOn the retry-exhausted `\"eval_error\"` terminal transition itself,\n   341\tthe orchestrator MUST NOT graft metrics or artifacts from any\n   342\tprior `eval_error` submission onto the trial; the trial's\n   343\t`metrics` and `artifacts_uri` fields remain unset. This keeps the\n   344\ttrial object canonical: a trial either carries the outputs of a\n   345\tsuccessful or code-level-failed evaluation, or it carries nothing.\n   346\t\n   347\tResubmission is idempotent under the same rules as §3.4: identical\n   348\t`trial_id` + `status` + `metrics` + `artifacts_uri` MUST be\n   349\taccepted; inconsistent resubmission MUST be rejected.\n   350\t\n   351\t## 5. Integrator\n   352\t\n   353\tThe integrator promotes successfully evaluated trials into the\n   354\tcanonical trial lineage.\n   355\t\n   356\tThe integrator's full contract — the squash rule, the eval-manifest\n   357\tshape, conflict-resolution policy — is deferred to\n   358\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md). This section pins only the\n   359\tboundary rules that every other role must honor.\n   360\t\n   361\t### 5.1 Exclusive authority\n   362\t\n   363\tThe integrator is the **sole writer** of the canonical trial lineage\n   364\t(`trial/*` branches, `trial_commit_sha` fields on trials). Every\n   365\tother role MUST treat `trial/*` as read-only and MUST NOT set or\n   366\tmodify a trial's `trial_commit_sha`.\n   367\t\n   368\t### 5.2 Inputs\n   369\t\n   370\tThe integrator observes trials transitioning to `status == \"success\"`\n   371\t(as recorded by the evaluator in §4.4). The mechanism of observation\n   372\t(event subscription, store poll, dispatch) is a binding concern.\n   373\t\n   374\t### 5.3 Outputs\n   375\t\n   376\tThe integrator writes a single commit on a `trial/*` branch for each\n   377\tpromoted trial and records the resulting SHA in the trial's\n   378\t`trial_commit_sha`. Exact topology invariants are in\n   379\t[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n   380\t\n   381\t### 5.4 Why integration is separate from evaluation\n   382\t\n   383\tEvaluation and integration are separate roles because a successful\n   384\tevaluation is a necessary but not sufficient condition for promoting\n   385\ta trial: the integrator applies experiment-level policy\n   386\t(squash shape, conflict resolution with concurrent trials, manifest\n   387\tattachment) that is not meaningful to the evaluator. A conforming\n   388\timplementation MAY collapse evaluator and integrator into the same\n   389\tprocess, but MUST still honor both contracts independently.\n   390\t\n   391\t## 6. Role binding (deferred)\n   392\t\n   393\tHow a role is invoked, addressed, or configured is **not specified\n   394\tin v0**. A conforming deployment MAY:\n   395\t\n   396\t- Run each role as a separate long-lived service reached over some\n   397\t  wire protocol.\n   398\t- Spawn each role as a short-lived subprocess per task.\n   399\t- Collapse multiple roles into a single process that consumes its\n   400\t  own task queue.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-4/20260423T123425/0.jsonl:70:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_35","type":"agent_message","text":"**Findings**\n\n1. Bug — [spec/v0/06-integrator.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/06-integrator.md:136) §3.2 conflicts with [spec/v0/06-integrator.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/06-integrator.md:200) §4. The squash rule says the canonical commit `T` MUST satisfy `tree(T) == tree(commit_sha)`, but §4 also requires `T` to contain `.eden/trials/<trial_id>/eval.json`. Those cannot both be true unless the worker branch already contains the manifest, which the implementer/evaluator contracts do not require and effectively forbid. Fix by changing the tree invariant to “worker tip tree plus the eval-manifest path” or by moving the manifest out of the committed tree; then update the repeat-promotion/idempotency check in §5.3 to match.\n\n2. Bug — [spec/v0/06-integrator.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/06-integrator.md:221) §4 requires eval-manifest fields the rest of the protocol never produces. `evaluator.{name,version}` and the per-artifact `{uri, sha256, bytes}` entries have no protocol-defined source: [spec/v0/03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:285) only gives evaluator submissions `trial_id`, `status`, `metrics`, and optional `artifacts_uri`, and [spec/v0/08-storage.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/08-storage.md:237) only defines artifact `upload`/`fetch`, not listing or metadata discovery. A conforming integrator cannot build the required manifest from protocol-owned state. Fix by carrying evaluator identity/version and artifact inventory/hash data in the evaluator submission/trial model, or relax the manifest requirements until those fields exist elsewhere in the spec.\n\n3. Bug — [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:251) §3.4 promises that registered events alone can reconstruct every protocol-owned state change, but the registry payloads do not carry the fields those transitions write. `proposal.drafted`/`proposal.ready` omit proposal contents, `trial.started` omits branch/parents/`started_at`, `trial.succeeded`/`trial.errored` omit metrics/artifacts/`completed_at`, and `task.created` omits task payload/timestamps. [spec/v0/schemas/event.schema.json](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/schemas/event.schema.json:43) and [reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/event.py](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/event.py:49) are in lockstep with that minimal registry, so schema/model parity is fine, but the protocol claim is not. Fix by either expanding required event payloads to include the full state delta/snapshot for each protocol-owned write, or narrowing the spec’s reconstruction guarantee to lifecycle transitions only and aligning the cross-chapter observability language.\n\n4. Risk — [spec/v0/08-storage.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/08-storage.md:24) specifies concrete operations only for tasks, events, and artifacts, even though [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:53) makes the transactional invariant apply equally to proposals and trials and [spec/v0/03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:109) requires durable proposal/trial writes. As written, a reimplementation can satisfy the storage chapter literally while leaving proposal/trial persistence semantics under-specified. Fix by defining the proposal/trial repository contract explicitly, or by stating which store owns those writes and that the same durability, read-after-write, and atomicity rules apply.\n\n5. Risk — [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:310) says replay from an experiment’s first event is required, but [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:335) and [spec/v0/08-storage.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/08-storage.md:134) allow compaction to reject older replays. That ambiguity weakens the replayability floor and leaves room for incompatible implementations. Fix by choosing one contract and stating it consistently: either lifetime full replay while an experiment is retained, or replay only from a declared retention horizon.\n\n**Overall Assessment**\n\nPhase 4 does land the planned 4a+4b+4c artifacts, and I did not find a schema↔model literal drift on the implemented 15-event registry: the enum values and commit-SHA pattern are aligned between `event.schema.json` and `event.py`. I would still request changes before calling the phase coherent. The main blockers are protocol-level: the integrator chapter contains two hard contradictions, and the event/storage chapters currently promise reconstruction and replay guarantees that the defined registry and storage contract do not actually make implementable."}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-3/20260423T112054/0.jsonl:7:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_1","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' AGENTS.md\"","aggregated_output":"# AGENTS.md\n\nThis file provides guidance to AI agents working with this repository.\n\n## What this repo is\n\nEDEN is a **protocol** for directed-code-evolution orchestration, not a\nsingle product. The repo contains three distinct kinds of artifact, each\nwith different change discipline:\n\n| Layer | Path | Authoritative? | Change discipline |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Protocol specification | [`spec/`](spec/) | Yes — the source of truth | RFC-style: versioned, normative (MUST/SHOULD/MAY), carefully reviewed |\n| Reference implementation | [`reference/`](reference/) | No — *one* valid impl | Normal code-review discipline |\n| Conformance suite | [`conformance/`](conformance/) | Normative for tests | Black-box; must be implementation-agnostic |\n\nNon-normative human docs live in [`docs/`](docs/).\n\n## Current phase\n\n**Phase 2 complete.** `spec/v0/03-roles.md` (planner, implementer,\nevaluator, integrator contracts) and `spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md`\n(state machine, claim tokens, idempotent submit, reclamation) have\nmerged to the protected `main`, alongside the Phase 1 core-concepts\nchapters and six JSON Schemas. Both CI checks (`docs-lint` and\n`schema-validity`) remain green. See\n[`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) for the full 13-phase plan.\nPhase 3 next ports a Pydantic-bound reference contracts package and\nwires schema-model parity into CI.\n\n## Commands\n\nAt Phase 3, markdown linting, JSON Schema validation, and the Python\ntoolchain for the `eden-contracts` reference package are wired up.\n\n| Command | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `npx --yes markdownlint-cli2@0.14.0 \"**/*.md\" \"#node_modules\" \"#.venv\" \"#docs/archive/**\" \"#docs/plans/review/**\"` | Lint all tracked markdown (pinned to CI's version; matches CI exactly) |\n| `pipx run 'check-jsonschema==0.29.4' --check-metaschema spec/v0/schemas/*.schema.json` | Validate each schema file against the Draft 2020-12 meta-schema (version pinned to CI) |\n| `pipx run 'check-jsonschema==0.29.4' --schemafile spec/v0/schemas/experiment-config.schema.json tests/fixtures/experiment/.eden/config.yaml` | Validate the fixture experiment config against its schema |\n| `uv sync` | Install/refresh the workspace virtualenv (root + `reference/packages/eden-contracts`) |\n| `uv run ruff check .` | Lint Python (config in root `pyproject.toml`) |\n| `uv run pyright` | Type-check the reference Python packages |\n| `uv run pytest -q` | Run the reference-package test suite (includes schema ↔ model parity) |\n| `uv run pytest reference/packages/eden-contracts/tests/test_schema_parity.py` | Run only the schema ↔ Pydantic model parity check |\n\n### Commands that will exist in later phases\n\nThese are listed for orientation; the tooling is not wired up yet.\n\n| Command | Lands in |\n|---|---|\n| `docker compose up` end-to-end | Phase 10 |\n\n## Contribution conventions\n\n### Spec edits\n\n+ Every cross-component contract (wire format, state machine, invariant)\n  is defined in `spec/` first, then implemented in `reference/`. When\n  they disagree, the spec wins and the impl gets a bug.\n+ Spec chapters use RFC 2119 language: MUST, SHOULD, MAY.\n+ Spec versions are frozen once stable (`spec/v0/` is a single lineage;\n  breaking changes go to `spec/v1/`).\n+ Schema changes must update the spec prose, the JSON Schema file, and\n  (from Phase 3 onward) the Pydantic bindings in lockstep. CI will\n  enforce schema ↔ model parity once both exist.\n\n### Reference implementation\n\n+ Normal code-review discipline.\n+ Normative behavior is tested against the conformance suite (once it\n  exists in Phase 11).\n+ The reference impl is explicitly not a monopoly on correctness. A\n  third-party implementation that passes conformance is equally valid.\n\n## Commit guidelines\n\n+ Short imperative subjects (e.g., \"Add event protocol chapter\",\n  \"Pin task state machine\").\n+ For pull requests, include: what the change is, which phase it\n  advances, and any spec ↔ impl implications.\n\n## Related docs\n\n+ [`docs/naming.md`](docs/naming.md) — what EDEN is, at the concept level.\n+ [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) — the 13-phase build-up plan with\n  unit-level decomposition.\n+ [`docs/plans/`](docs/plans/) — active implementation plans.\n+ [`docs/archive/`](docs/archive/) — historical reference docs.\n+ [`STYLE_GUIDE.md`](STYLE_GUIDE.md) — formatting and naming conventions.\n+ [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](CONTRIBUTING.md) — setup and PR workflow.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-3/20260423T112054/0.jsonl:17:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_7","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,320p' spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Task Protocol\n\nThis chapter specifies the behavioral contract for tasks: the state\nmachine they advance through, the claim tokens that grant exclusive\nexecution, the idempotency rules for submission, and the\nreclamation policy that bounds worker liveness. It pins *what*\nobservable behavior a conforming task store and a conforming\norchestrator must exhibit; it does not pin *how* either is\nimplemented.\n\nThe task object's shape is defined in\n[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3; its JSON Schema is\n[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json). The role\ncontracts that drive the transitions defined here are in\n[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md).\n\n## 1. State machine\n\n### 1.1 States\n\nA task's `state` field takes one of five values. Each has a\nwell-defined meaning in the state machine:\n\n| State | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `pending` | The task has been created and is eligible to be claimed. No worker currently holds it. |\n| `claimed` | A worker holds an active claim on the task and is expected to execute it. The task carries a `claim` object. |\n| `submitted` | The claiming worker has presented a result. The task awaits processing by the orchestrator. |\n| `completed` | The orchestrator has accepted the result. The task is terminal. |\n| `failed` | The orchestrator has rejected the result, the worker declared failure, or a policy limit (e.g. retry budget) has been exceeded. The task is terminal. |\n\n`completed` and `failed` are **terminal**: a conforming task store\nMUST NOT transition a task out of either state.\n\n### 1.2 Transitions\n\nThe permitted transitions are exactly:\n\n```text\n          claim                 submit                accept\npending ─────────▶ claimed ─────────────▶ submitted ─────────▶ completed\n   ▲                 │                       │\n   │ reclaim         │ reclaim               │ reject\n   └─────────────────┴───────────────────────┤           ──▶ failed\n                                             │\n```\n\n- **claim** — `pending → claimed`. Atomic. Issues a fresh claim\n  token. §3.\n- **submit** — `claimed → submitted`. Requires presenting the\n  current claim token. Idempotent under identical payload.\n  Performed for every completed attempt, whether the worker\n  declares success or failure. §4.\n- **reclaim** — `claimed → pending` or `submitted → pending`.\n  Invalidates the prior claim token. §5.\n- **accept** — `submitted → completed`. Orchestrator-initiated.\n  §4.3.\n- **reject** — `submitted → failed`. Orchestrator-initiated,\n  including when the worker's own submission declared failure.\n  §4.3.\n\nAny transition not listed above MUST be rejected by the task store.\nA conforming task store MUST treat concurrent transition attempts\nas serialized: whichever attempt wins the race succeeds, and the\nothers MUST receive a well-defined rejection that lets the caller\ndistinguish \"invalid\" from \"raced\".\n\n### 1.3 Event emission\n\nEvery state transition MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event\nappended to the event log, atomically with the state change. The\nevent types (e.g. `task.claimed`, `task.submitted`, `task.failed`)\nand the transactional invariant are specified in\n[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md). This chapter does\nnot enumerate the event shapes; it requires only that they exist\nand that no state change is observable without its event.\n\n## 2. Creating a task\n\nA task enters the system in the `pending` state. A conforming\norchestrator MUST:\n\n- Populate `task_id`, `kind`, `state = \"pending\"`, `payload`,\n  `created_at`, `updated_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md)\n  §3.1).\n- Ensure the payload satisfies the dispatch preconditions for the\n  task's `kind`:\n  - `plan` — `payload.experiment_id` names an experiment the\n    planner is authorized to work on.\n  - `implement` — `payload.proposal_id` names a proposal with\n    `state == \"ready\"` at creation time. Creating the implement\n    task and transitioning the proposal from `\"ready\"` to\n    `\"dispatched\"` MUST be atomic; a proposal's `\"dispatched\"`\n    state signals that exactly one implement task has been\n    created for it.\n  - `evaluate` — `payload.trial_id` names a trial with\n    `status == \"starting\"` and `commit_sha` set at creation time.\n- Emit the corresponding `task.created` event (\n  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) atomically with\n  the task insert.\n\nA worker MAY rely on these preconditions ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md)\n§1.1). A task created without them is a protocol violation by the\norchestrator.\n\n## 3. Claim\n\n### 3.1 Atomicity\n\nThe `pending → claimed` transition MUST be atomic with respect to\ncompeting claim attempts: at most one worker MAY succeed for any\ngiven task. A conforming task store MUST provide this guarantee as\npart of its durability contract\n([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).\n\n### 3.2 Claim token\n\nA successful claim issues a `claim` object\n([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4) containing:\n\n- `token` — an opaque value that MUST be:\n  - **unique** — no two distinct claims (on the same or different\n    tasks) share the same token within an experiment;\n  - **unforgeable** — a worker that did not receive the token MUST\n    NOT be able to construct a byte-equal value with practical\n    effort. Implementations commonly use a cryptographically\n    random value; the protocol does not mandate the mechanism.\n- `worker_id` — an identifier the worker supplies at claim time.\n  The task store MAY record it for audit but MUST NOT use it as a\n  substitute for the token when authorizing subsequent operations.\n- `claimed_at` — the time the claim was issued.\n- `expires_at` — OPTIONAL. If present, the task store MAY reclaim\n  the task when the current time exceeds this value (§5).\n\n### 3.3 Authorization by token\n\nEvery subsequent operation on a claimed task (progress update,\nsubmit, release) MUST present the current claim token. The task\nstore MUST reject any such operation whose token does not match the\ntoken currently recorded on the task. This is the sole mechanism\nthe protocol relies on to guarantee single-writer access to a\nclaimed task.\n\n### 3.4 No re-claim while claimed\n\nA conforming task store MUST reject a claim attempt against a task\nwhose `state` is not `pending`. In particular, a claimed task\ncannot be \"re-claimed\" by a different worker; the prior claim must\nfirst be released (by submission) or invalidated (by reclamation).\n\n## 4. Submit\n\n### 4.1 Submission operation\n\nA submit operation requires:\n\n- The `task_id`.\n- The current claim `token`.\n- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind`\n  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).\n\nA successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` atomically\nwith persisting the result payload. The terminal transition to\n`completed` or `failed` is the orchestrator's subsequent step\n(§4.3), even when the submission itself declared failure.\n\n### 4.2 Idempotency\n\nA resubmission is a second submit carrying the token recorded on\nthe task's `claim` object (which is retained through `submitted`\nper [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). A resubmit against\na task whose claim has been cleared — because the task was\nreclaimed or reached a terminal state — MUST be rejected\nregardless of the presented token (§4.4, §5.2).\n\nWhen the token matches, the task store MUST handle the\nresubmission as follows:\n\n- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to\n  the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and\n  MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. \"Content\n  equivalence\" means the normative fields identified per role\n  agree:\n  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (compared as sets; order\n    is not significant per [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4) and\n    `status`.\n  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha`\n    (when present).\n  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as\n    JSON values; key order does not matter).\n- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent,\n  the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is\n  the committed result.\n\nThis rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a\nnetwork or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or\ncorrupting the recorded result.\n\n### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal\n\nA submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions\nto a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:\n\n- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's\n  `status` is `\"success\"` and the result satisfies the role's\n  success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a\n  reachable `commit_sha`).\n- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:\n  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed\n    payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).\n  - The worker itself declared failure:\n    - `plan` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`.\n    - `implement` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`.\n    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"error\"` → `failed`\n      AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to\n      `\"error\"`.\n    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == \"eval_error\"` →\n      `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain\n      `\"starting\"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad;\n      only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh\n      `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the\n      protocol does not mandate automatic retry. If the\n      orchestrator's policy exhausts retries for this trial\n      (or the operator abandons it), the orchestrator MUST\n      transition the trial's `status` from `\"starting\"` to\n      `\"eval_error\"`, at which point the trial status is\n      terminal.\n\nEvery terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event\nin the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) and\nMUST clear the task's `claim` object\n([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). The orchestrator MAY\nimplement the `claimed → submitted → completed` (or `failed`)\nsequence as two distinct persisted states or as a single store\ntransaction whose event stream includes both transitions, but\nsubscribers MUST observe both events in order.\n\n### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited\n\nOnce a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its\ntask fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task\nstore chooses to expose outside the normative schema\n([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a\nresubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of\ncontent equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal\ntransition is no longer valid.\n\n## 5. Reclamation\n\n### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted\n\nA conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from\n`claimed` back to `pending` — in any of the following cases:\n\n- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed.\n- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.\n- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker\n  is unreachable.\n\nA task in `submitted` state MAY be reclaimed **only** by an\nexplicit operator action; automatic reclaim (expires_at, health\npolicy) MUST NOT apply to `submitted` tasks. After submit the\nworker's reachability is no longer a relevant liveness signal; the\norchestrator is expected to advance the task to a terminal state\n(§4.3).\n\nA task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.\n\n### 5.2 Effect of reclamation\n\nReclamation:\n\n1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations\n   presenting it MUST be rejected.\n2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.\n3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.\n4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log\n   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically\n   with the state change.\n\nAny partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is\nno longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker\ncreated store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting\ntrials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are\nsubject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not\ndelete them.\n\n### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation\n\nA worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its\ntask's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its\ntoken has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task\nand MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).\n\n### 5.4 Reclamation effect on role outputs\n\nWhen a reclaimed task had created role-owned objects during its\nprior execution, the orchestrator MUST reconcile those objects as\nfollows:\n\n- **Implement reclamation.** If the prior execution created a\n  trial with `status == \"starting\"`, the orchestrator MUST\n  transition that trial's `status` to `\"error\"` atomically with\n  the reclaim event. A subsequent re-claim of the implement task\n  produces a **new** trial; starting trials are never shared\n  across implementer attempts.\n- **Plan reclamation.** If the prior execution persisted\n  proposals in `\"drafting\"` state, those proposals remain in\n  `\"drafting\"` and are not dispatched. Implementations MAY expose\n  them to operators for inspection or removal; the task protocol\n  does not mandate automatic cleanup.\n- **Evaluate reclamation.** The trial the task referenced is\n  unchanged by reclamation (it was produced by the implementer,\n  not the evaluator).\n\n## 6. Ordering and concurrency\n\n### 6.1 Per-task serialization\n\nAll transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/plans/review/eden-protocol-bootstrap/impl/phase-3/20260423T112054/0.jsonl:18:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_8","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' spec/v0/03-roles.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Role Contracts\n\nThis chapter defines the four role contracts that a conforming EDEN\ndeployment implements: **planner**, **implementer**, **evaluator**,\nand **integrator**. For each, it pins what the role observes, what\nit produces, and which invariants its output MUST honor.\n\nA role contract does **not** specify how the role is hosted. A\nconforming planner MAY run as a local subprocess, a long-lived\nservice reached over HTTP, an in-process adapter, or a human at a\nkeyboard. The protocol constrains the *observable effects* a role\nhas on the task store, the event log, and the artifact store; how a\nrole is invoked is a deployment concern and is deferred to a\nlater-phase *role-binding* specification. Within v0, implementations\nMAY document their own binding by defining additional top-level\nfields on the experiment config per\n[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4.\n\nThe behavioral state machine that all worker roles participate in —\nclaim, execute, submit, reclaim — is defined in\n[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). This chapter describes\nonly the *role-specific* part of each worker's job: what it reads,\nwhat it produces, and what guarantees its output must carry. Where a\nrule depends on a state-machine transition, this chapter cites the\ntransition by name and points to Chapter 04 for its semantics.\n\n## 1. Common worker-role lifecycle\n\nThe planner, implementer, and evaluator are **worker roles**: they\nparticipate in the task protocol as consumers. Each worker role\nprogresses through the same outer lifecycle for every task it\nhandles:\n\n1. **Discover.** The worker becomes aware of a pending task whose\n   `kind` matches its role. Mechanisms include polling the task\n   store, subscribing to an event stream, or receiving a dispatch\n   call — all are permitted; none are mandated.\n2. **Claim.** The worker atomically moves the task from `pending`\n   to `claimed` and receives a claim token. Semantics are in\n   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.\n3. **Execute.** The worker performs its role-specific work using the\n   task payload and any context the protocol grants it (§2–§4\n   below). The worker MAY report progress. It MUST NOT mutate any\n   protocol-owned object other than (a) the task it holds a claim\n   on and (b) the outputs its role is specified to produce (§2.2,\n   §3.2, §4.2). The restrictions in §1.2 apply to every worker role\n   in addition to the per-role rules below.\n4. **Submit.** The worker atomically moves the task from `claimed`\n   to `submitted`, presenting its claim token and a result (§2–§4\n   below). A submitted task then advances to `completed` or `failed`\n   per the rules in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.\n5. **Release on reclaim.** If the task store reclaims the task\n   before submit (task store policy per\n   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5), the worker's\n   token is invalidated. A worker that discovers its token has been\n   invalidated MUST NOT subsequently attempt to submit against that\n   task; it MUST discard any partial result.\n\nIntegrator behavior does **not** follow this worker-role lifecycle.\nThe integrator is event-driven and is defined separately in §5 below\nand in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).\n\n### 1.1 What a worker MAY assume about its inputs\n\nBefore a task is dispatched to a worker-role queue, the orchestrator\nMUST ensure the task's payload references (proposal, trial,\nparent commits) satisfy the dispatch preconditions listed per role\nbelow. A worker MAY therefore rely on those preconditions and is not\nrequired to re-validate them. A worker that nevertheless discovers a\nviolation (e.g. a referenced proposal has been retracted) MUST fail\nthe task with an error status; it MUST NOT repair store state\nitself.\n\n### 1.2 What a worker MUST NOT do\n\n- A worker MUST NOT write to any protocol-owned object other than\n  the task it holds a claim on and the outputs that task's role is\n  defined to produce (§2–§4).\n- A worker MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage (`trial/*`);\n  that is the integrator's exclusive authority\n  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9,\n  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).\n- A worker MUST NOT advance a task's state beyond `submitted`.\n  Transitions to `completed` or `failed` are the orchestrator's\n  responsibility ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4).\n\n## 2. Planner\n\nThe planner proposes what to try. Every planner invocation consumes\none `plan` task and produces zero or more proposals.\n\n### 2.1 Inputs\n\nA planner receives:\n\n- The task object (`kind == \"plan\"`,\n  `payload.experiment_id == E`).\n- Read access to the experiment config for `E`\n  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2), including the\n  `metrics_schema` and `objective`.\n- Read access to the set of proposals and trials already persisted\n  for `E`. The mechanism (direct store read, orchestrator-supplied\n  snapshot, event-log replay) is a role-binding concern.\n\nA planner MAY additionally use any context the experiment config\nsurfaces to it via role-binding-specific fields. The protocol does\nnot require the planner to read beyond what is listed above.\n\n### 2.2 Outputs\n\nFor each proposal the planner produces, it MUST:\n\n1. Persist a proposal object (via the protocol's proposal store —\n   mechanism is binding-specific) that validates against\n   [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json),\n   with `experiment_id == E` and `state == \"drafting\"`.\n2. Upload any artifacts (plan text, rationale, supporting files) to\n   the artifact store and populate the proposal's `artifacts_uri`.\n3. Transition the proposal's `state` to `\"ready\"` once the proposal\n   is dispatchable. The transition from `drafting` to `ready`\n   signals that the proposal's metadata is stable and that the\n   implementer MAY consume it.\n\nA planner MAY create multiple proposals under a single `plan` task.\nA planner MAY also produce zero proposals if it has no viable\nchange to suggest; the task still completes normally (see §2.4).\n\n### 2.3 Proposal invariants\n\nThe planner MUST honor the structural invariants of proposals\n([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5):\n\n- `parent_commits` MUST contain at least one SHA that names a\n  commit reachable from either the experiment's starting commit on\n  `main` or a completed trial on the canonical lineage\n  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9).\n- `slug` MUST match the documented pattern.\n- `priority` is the planner's own ordering hint; higher values\n  SHOULD dispatch earlier.\n\n### 2.4 Submission\n\nWhen its work on a `plan` task is complete, the planner MUST submit\nthe task (§1 step 4). The submission payload MUST include:\n\n- `proposal_ids` — the set of identifiers of every proposal the\n  planner created under this task. Order is not significant;\n  dispatch ordering is determined by each proposal's `priority`\n  field ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1). MAY be\n  empty.\n- `status` — one of `\"success\"` (the planner completed normally,\n  regardless of proposal count) or `\"error\"` (the planner could\n  not complete; any partially-written proposals MUST remain in\n  `\"drafting\"` state and MUST NOT be dispatched).\n\nA planner MUST NOT submit a `plan` task while any of its\nreferenced proposals is still in `\"drafting\"` state.\n\n## 3. Implementer\n\nThe implementer realizes a proposal as a working-tree change on a\nper-trial branch.\n\n### 3.1 Inputs\n\nAn implementer receives:\n\n- The task object (`kind == \"implement\"`,\n  `payload.proposal_id == P`).\n- Read access to the proposal `P`. The orchestrator MUST ensure\n  `P.state == \"ready\"` before dispatch.\n- Read and write access to the git repository associated with\n  `P.experiment_id`, at a revision reachable from\n  `P.parent_commits`. How the repository is located and how\n  access is granted is a role-binding concern (§6,\n  [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4); the protocol\n  constrains only that the implementer's writes land under\n  `work/*` and that no commit is introduced whose history does\n  not descend from `P.parent_commits` (§3.3).\n- Read access to the artifacts at `P.artifacts_uri`.\n\n### 3.2 Outputs\n\nThe implementer produces a **trial** object\n([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7). It MUST:\n\n1. Persist a trial with `status == \"starting\"`,\n   `experiment_id == P.experiment_id`, `proposal_id == P`, and\n   `parent_commits == P.parent_commits`, before making any\n   repository write observable to other roles.\n2. Write the implementation on a branch under `work/*`\n   ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9) whose parent(s) are the\n   proposal's `parent_commits`. Set the trial's `branch` field to\n   this branch name.\n3. On successful completion, set the trial's `commit_sha` to the\n   tip of the `work/*` branch. Optionally set `artifacts_uri` and\n   `description`.\n\nThe implementer MUST NOT write to `trial/*` or `main`; those are\nowned by the integrator. The `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial\nMUST NOT be set by the implementer.\n\n### 3.3 Worker-branch invariants\n\n- The `work/*` branch MUST be unique to this trial. The ``kind`` field\n     4\tdiscriminates the payload shape, and the ``claim`` field is present iff\n     5\t``state`` is ``claimed`` or ``submitted`` (the state-dependent presence\n     6\tis enforced by a model validator because JSON Schema expresses it via\n     7\t``if/then/else`` at the root).\n     8\t\n     9\tState-machine semantics are defined in spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md.\n    10\t\"\"\"\n    11\t\n    12\tfrom __future__ import annotations\n    13\t\n    14\tfrom typing import Annotated, Literal, Self\n    15\t\n    16\tfrom pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field, TypeAdapter, model_validator\n    17\t\n    18\tfrom ._common import DateTimeStr\n    19\t\n    20\tTaskKind = Literal[\"plan\", \"implement\", \"evaluate\"]\n    21\tTaskState = Literal[\"pending\", \"claimed\", \"submitted\", \"completed\", \"failed\"]\n    22\t\n    23\t_CLAIM_STATES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({\"claimed\", \"submitted\"})\n    24\t\n    25\t\n    26\tclass TaskClaim(BaseModel):\n    27\t    \"\"\"Claim token issued by the task store when a worker claims a task.\"\"\"\n    28\t\n    29\t    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"allow\")\n    30\t\n    31\t    token: Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]\n    32\t    worker_id: Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]\n    33\t    claimed_at: DateTimeStr\n    34\t    expires_at: DateTimeStr | None = None\n    35\t\n    36\t\n    37\tclass PlanPayload(BaseModel):\n    38\t    \"\"\"Payload for ``kind=plan`` tasks.\"\"\"\n    39\t\n    40\t    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"allow\")\n    41\t\n    42\t    experiment_id: Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]\n    43\t\n    44\t\n    45\tclass ImplementPayload(BaseModel):\n    46\t    \"\"\"Payload for ``kind=implement`` tasks.\"\"\"\n    47\t\n    48\t    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"allow\")\n    49\t\n    50\t    proposal_id: Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]\n    51\t\n    52\t\n    53\tclass EvaluatePayload(BaseModel):\n    54\t    \"\"\"Payload for ``kind=evaluate`` tasks.\"\"\"\n    55\t\n    56\t    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"allow\")\n    57\t\n    58\t    trial_id: Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]\n    59\t\n    60\t\n    61\tclass _TaskBase(BaseModel):\n    62\t    \"\"\"Fields shared across every task kind.\"\"\"\n    63\t\n    64\t    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"allow\")\n    65\t\n    66\t    task_id: Annotated[str, Field(min_length=1)]\n    67\t    state: TaskState\n    68\t    claim: TaskClaim | None = None\n    69\t    created_at: DateTimeStr\n    70\t    updated_at: DateTimeStr\n    71\t\n    72\t    @model_validator(mode=\"after\")\n    73\t    def _claim_presence(self) -> Self:\n    74\t        if self.state in _CLAIM_STATES:\n    75\t            if self.claim is None:\n    76\t                raise ValueError(\n    77\t                    f\"claim is required when state is {self.state!r}\"\n    78\t                )\n    79\t        elif self.claim is not None:\n    80\t            raise ValueError(\n    81\t                f\"claim is forbidden when state is {self.state!r}\"\n    82\t            )\n    83\t        return self\n    84\t\n    85\t\n    86\tclass PlanTask(_TaskBase):\n    87\t    \"\"\"Task of kind ``plan``.\"\"\"\n    88\t\n    89\t    kind: Literal[\"plan\"]\n    90\t    payload: PlanPayload\n    91\t\n    92\t\n    93\tclass ImplementTask(_TaskBase):\n    94\t    \"\"\"Task of kind ``implement``.\"\"\"\n    95\t\n    96\t    kind: Literal[\"implement\"]\n    97\t    payload: ImplementPayload\n    98\t\n    99\t\n   100\tclass EvaluateTask(_TaskBase):\n   101\t    \"\"\"Task of kind ``evaluate``.\"\"\"\n   102\t\n   103\t    kind: Literal[\"evaluate\"]\n   104\t    payload: EvaluatePayload\n   105\t\n   106\t\n   107\tTask = Annotated[\n   108\t    PlanTask | ImplementTask | EvaluateTask,\n   109\t    Field(discriminator=\"kind\"),\n   110\t]\n   111\t\"\"\"Discriminated union keyed by ``kind`` — use :data:`TaskAdapter` to validate untyped input.\"\"\"\n   112\t\n   113\tTaskAdapter: TypeAdapter[Task] = TypeAdapter(Task)\n   114\t\"\"\"Pydantic adapter for validating arbitrary task objects into the union.\"\"\"\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

"""Backend-agnostic transition logic shared by every ``Store`` backend.

Every EDEN store backend — in-memory, SQLite, a future Postgres or
remote backend — has to enforce the same state-machine, the same
composite commits (``spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md`` §2.2), the same
token/idempotency/terminal-immutability rules, and the same metrics
validation. Factoring that logic out of any one backend and sharing
it is the only way to make "passes the same conformance suite"
mean what it says.

``_StoreBase`` owns:

- The public method surface (``claim``, ``submit``, ``accept``,
  ``reject``, ``reclaim``, ``create_*``, ``read_*``, ``list_*``,
  ``events``, ``validate_acceptance``, ``validate_terminal``,
  ``create_proposal``, ``mark_proposal_ready``, ``create_trial``,
  ``declare_trial_eval_error``, ``integrate_trial``).
- All validation, composite-commit staging, and event construction.

Subclasses own:

- ``_atomic_operation`` — the transaction scope. In-memory wraps an
  ``RLock``; SQLite wraps ``BEGIN IMMEDIATE``…``COMMIT``.
- ``_get_task``/``_get_proposal``/``_get_trial``/``_get_submission``
  — primitive lookups.
- ``_iter_tasks``/``_iter_proposals``/``_iter_trials``/``_iter_events``
  — ordered iteration.
- ``_apply_commit(tx)`` — apply the staged ``_Tx`` inside the already-
  open transaction, without committing it. The outer
  ``_atomic_operation`` context does the actual commit.

Every public method follows the same pattern: open an atomic
operation, perform reads + validations (which may raise before any
write), stage all writes into a ``_Tx`` object, and call
``self._apply_commit(tx)`` exactly once. If validation raises the
atomic operation aborts and no partial state becomes visible
(chapter 8 §6.1–§6.3).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import copy
import itertools
import math
import secrets
from collections.abc import Callable, Iterable, Iterator
from contextlib import AbstractContextManager
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import UTC, datetime
from typing import Any

from eden_contracts import (
    EvaluatePayload,
    EvaluateTask,
    Event,
    FailReason,
    ImplementPayload,
    ImplementTask,
    MetricsSchema,
    PlanPayload,
    PlanTask,
    Proposal,
    ReclaimCause,
    Task,
    TaskClaim,
    Trial,
)
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

from .errors import (
    AlreadyExists,
    ConflictingResubmission,
    IllegalTransition,
    InvalidPrecondition,
    NotFound,
    WrongToken,
)
from .submissions import (
    EvaluateSubmission,
    ImplementSubmission,
    PlanSubmission,
    Submission,
    submissions_equivalent,
)

    # bool is excluded from "integer" even though it is a Python int subclass.
    "integer": (int,),
    "real": (int, float),
    "text": (str,),

@dataclass
class _Tx:
    """Staged writes for a single atomic operation.

    A public method stages all mutations here and calls
    ``_apply_commit`` exactly once at the end. Any precondition
    failure raises before ``_apply_commit``, so readers never observe
    a partial state change. Subclasses apply the contents against
    whatever backing store they use (dicts, SQLite tables, …).
    """

    tasks: dict[str, Task] = field(default_factory=dict)
    proposals: dict[str, Proposal] = field(default_factory=dict)
    trials: dict[str, Trial] = field(default_factory=dict)
    submissions: dict[str, Submission] = field(default_factory=dict)
    task_deletes_submission: set[str] = field(default_factory=set)
    events: list[Event] = field(default_factory=list)

    onto a stored trial. Re-validating on every update is how every

def submit(self, task_id: str, token: str, submission: Submission) -> None:
        """Transition a claimed task to submitted, persisting the result.

        Idempotent per ``04-task-protocol.md`` §4.2: a resubmit with
        the current token and a content-equivalent payload MUST
        succeed without mutating state; an inconsistent resubmit MUST
        be rejected.
        """
        with self._atomic_operation():
            task = self._require_task(task_id)
            if task.state not in {"claimed", "submitted"}:
                raise IllegalTransition(
                    f"cannot submit task in state {task.state!r}"
                )
            if task.claim is None or task.claim.token != token:
                raise WrongToken(f"token does not match current claim on {task_id!r}")
            self._require_submission_kind_matches(task, submission)
            self._validate_submission_ref_binding(task, submission)

            if task.state == "submitted":
                prior = self._get_submission(task_id)
                if prior is None:
                    raise IllegalTransition(
                        f"task {task_id!r} is submitted but has no recorded submission"
                    )
                if not submissions_equivalent(prior, submission):
                    raise ConflictingResubmission(
                        f"resubmit of {task_id!r} disagrees with committed result"
                    )
                return

            now = self._ts()
            tx = _Tx()
            tx.tasks[task_id] = _validated_update(task, state="submitted", updated_at=now)
            tx.submissions[task_id] = copy.deepcopy(submission)
            tx.events.append(self._event("task.submitted", {"task_id": task_id}))
            self._apply_commit(tx)

        """Return an orchestrator-side reason to reject this submission, or ``None``.

        ``04-task-protocol.md`` §4.3 requires the orchestrator to
        transition a submitted task to ``failed`` when the result

    65	def submissions_equivalent(a: Submission, b: Submission) -> bool:
    66	    """Content equivalence per ``spec/v0/04-task-protocol.md`` §4.2.
    67	
    68	    The normative fields per role (§4.2):
    69	
    70	    - plan      — status + set of proposal_ids (order not significant).
    71	    - implement — status + trial_id + commit_sha.
    72	    - evaluate  — status + trial_id + metrics (as JSON values).
    73	
    74	    ``artifacts_uri`` is deliberately absent from evaluate equivalence:
    75	    §4.2 does not list it, so two submissions that agree on the
    76	    normative fields are equivalent even if they differ in
    77	    artifacts_uri, and the first submission's artifacts_uri is the
    78	    committed one.
    79	    """
    80	    if type(a) is not type(b):
    81	        return False
    82	    if isinstance(a, PlanSubmission) and isinstance(b, PlanSubmission):
    83	        return a.status == b.status and set(a.proposal_ids) == set(b.proposal_ids)
    84	    if isinstance(a, ImplementSubmission) and isinstance(b, ImplementSubmission):
    85	        return (
    86	            a.status == b.status
    87	            and a.trial_id == b.trial_id
    88	            and a.commit_sha == b.commit_sha
    89	        )
    90	    if isinstance(a, EvaluateSubmission) and isinstance(b, EvaluateSubmission):
    91	        return (
    92	            a.status == b.status
    93	            and a.trial_id == b.trial_id
    94	            and a.metrics == b.metrics
    95	        )
    96	    return False

1	# Data Model
     2	
     3	This chapter defines the shape of the values that flow through the EDEN protocol. For each entity it gives the intent, the fields, the required/optional split, and the structural invariants that MUST hold independent of any behavioral contract.
     4	
     5	Each entity also has a companion JSON Schema under [`schemas/`](schemas/). The prose in this chapter and the schema are normatively paired: an implementation's data MUST satisfy both. Where one is more restrictive than the other, the stricter constraint applies.
     6	
     7	Behavioral concerns — when fields may change, which transitions are permitted, what guarantees the surrounding store must offer — are specified in Chapters [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md), [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md), [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md), and [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md).
     8	
     9	## 1. Common types
    10	
    11	### 1.1 Identifiers
    12	
    13	Entity identifiers (`task_id`, `trial_id`, `proposal_id`, `event_id`) are opaque strings. They MUST be unique within the store that issues them. The protocol does not mandate a specific format; implementations MAY use integers rendered as strings, UUIDs, or other opaque values, so long as equality comparison is well-defined.
    14	
    15	### 1.2 Timestamps
    16	
    17	All timestamps in protocol messages MUST be RFC 3339 strings in UTC with second-or-better precision (e.g. `2026-04-23T18:04:01Z`). Offset timestamps (e.g. `…-07:00`) MUST NOT be used on the wire: every persisted timestamp MUST end with the literal `Z` suffix. A consumer MUST NOT assume timestamps are strictly monotonic across entities; ordering is established by the event log, not by timestamps.
    18	
    19	### 1.3 Metric values
    20	
    21	A metric value is one of: a JSON number (for `integer` or `real` types), a JSON string (for `text` type), or `null` (when the evaluator declined to report). Implementations MUST reject metric values whose JSON type does not match the declared metrics-schema type.
    22	
    23	The `integer` and `real` metric types both travel the wire as JSON numbers; they are distinguished by what values each admits:
    24	
    25	- **`integer`** — a JSON number whose value is an integer (no fractional component). Wire forms such as `3`, `-7`, or `1.0` are accepted; `1.5` MUST be rejected. A conforming orchestrator MUST validate this constraint when persisting a metrics payload.
    26	- **`real`** — any JSON number, including values with a fractional component.
    27	- **`text`** — a JSON string.
    28	- **`null`** — allowed for any declared type when the evaluator declined to report that metric.
    29	
    30	### 1.4 Git references
    31	
    32	Commit SHAs are lowercase hexadecimal strings of one of the two lengths git supports: **40 characters** (SHA-1) or **64 characters** (SHA-256). Every schema that embeds a commit SHA validates against this exact pair of lengths; a v0 implementation MUST NOT emit SHAs of other lengths on the wire. Branch names follow the namespaces defined in [`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9.
    33	
    34	### 1.5 URIs
    35

121	- The task object (`kind == "evaluate"`, `payload.trial_id == T`).
   122	- Read access to the trial `T`. The orchestrator MUST ensure `T.status == "starting"` and `T.commit_sha` is set before dispatch.
   123	- Read access to the git repository at `T.commit_sha` on the worker branch (`T.branch`). Repository location and access are a role-binding concern, as for the implementer (§3.1).
   124	- Read access to the experiment's `metrics_schema` and `objective`.
   125	
   126	### 4.2 Outputs
   127	
   128	The evaluator MUST:
   129	
   130	1. Produce a `metrics` object whose keys are a subset of the declared `metrics_schema` keys and whose values satisfy the per-metric type rules ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3, §7.2).
   131	2. Optionally upload supporting artifacts (logs, captured outputs, diagnostic files).
   132	
   133	The evaluator MUST NOT modify the worker branch or any protocol-owned mutable state other than the trial fields the submission writes (§4.4) and the task it holds a claim on. In particular, the evaluator MUST NOT write to the trial's `completed_at`, `metrics`, `artifacts_uri`, `description`, or `status` directly; those writes are performed by the orchestrator when the submitted task reaches its terminal state (§4.4, [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).
   134	
   135	### 4.3 Non-interference
   136	
   137	The evaluator reads the repository at the trial's worker-branch tip but MUST NOT push, rewrite, or delete that branch, and MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation (test caches, build outputs, captured logs) MUST NOT be observable through any protocol-owned store, ref, or artifact location except via the evaluator's declared outputs (§4.2).
   138	
   139	### 4.4 Submission
   140	
   141	The evaluator submits with:
   142	
   143	- `trial_id` — the trial it evaluated.
   144	- `status` — one of:
   145	  - `"success"` — the trial ran and produced metrics.
   146	  - `"error"` — the trial could not be evaluated for reasons attributable to the trial's own code (build failure, test failure, etc.). The evaluator MAY still include partial metrics.
   147	  - `"eval_error"` — the evaluator itself failed for reasons unrelated to the trial's code (infrastructure fault, evaluator bug). While a fresh evaluate task MAY still be created for this trial, the trial's status MUST remain `"starting"`. If the orchestrator's retry policy is exhausted (or the operator abandons evaluation), the orchestrator MUST transition the trial's status to `"eval_error"`, making that status terminal for the trial ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).
   148	- `metrics` — the metrics object described in §4.2. MAY be absent when `status == "eval_error"`.
   149	- `artifacts_uri` — OPTIONAL. A URI the evaluator uploaded supporting artifacts to.
   150	
   151	On a `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed` transition (per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3), the orchestrator MUST write the following trial fields atomically with the event:
   152	
   153	- `status` — the trial status implied by the submission: `"success"` when the submission's `status == "success"`; `"error"` when the submission's `status == "error"`; unchanged from `"starting"` when the submission's `status == "eval_error"` (see §4.4 above for the terminal-retry case).
   154	- `metrics` — set to the submission's `metrics` when `status ∈ {"success", "error"}`. When `status == "eval_error"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write `metrics` on the trial; any submission-carried `metrics` is discarded.
   155	- `artifacts_uri` — set to the submission's `artifacts_uri` when provided and `status ∈ {"success", "error"}`. When `status == "eval_error"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write `artifacts_uri` on the trial; any submission-carried `artifacts_uri` is discarded. (An evaluator that wishes to retain diagnostic artifacts from a failed attempt MAY reference them in the `task.failed` event for that evaluate task; that channel is defined in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md).)
   156	- `completed_at` — set to the time of the terminal trial transition, i.e. written exactly once, when the trial's status leaves `"starting"` (either on a `"success"`/`"error"` submission, or on the retry-exhausted `"eval_error"` transition). Intermediate `eval_error` submissions MUST NOT advance `completed_at`.
   157	
   158	On the retry-exhausted `"eval_error"` terminal transition itself, the orchestrator MUST NOT graft metrics or artifacts from any prior `eval_error` submission onto the trial; the trial's `metrics` and `artifacts_uri` fields remain unset. This keeps the trial object canonical: a trial either carries the outputs of a successful or code-level-failed evaluation, or it carries nothing.

82	- Read and write access to the git repository associated with `P.experiment_id`, at a revision reachable from `P.parent_commits`. How the repository is located and how access is granted is a role-binding concern (§6, [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4); the protocol constrains only that the implementer's writes land under `work/*` and that no commit is introduced whose history does not descend from `P.parent_commits` (§3.3).
    83	- Read access to the artifacts at `P.artifacts_uri`.
    84	
    85	### 3.2 Outputs
    86	
    87	The implementer produces a **trial** object ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7). It MUST:
    88	
    89	1. Persist a trial with `status == "starting"`, `experiment_id == P.experiment_id`, `proposal_id == P`, and `parent_commits == P.parent_commits`, before making any repository write observable to other roles.
    90	2. Write the implementation on a branch under `work/*` ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9) whose parent(s) are the proposal's `parent_commits`. Set the trial's `branch` field to this branch name.
    91	3. On successful completion, set the trial's `commit_sha` to the tip of the `work/*` branch. Optionally set `artifacts_uri` and `description`.
    92	
    93	The implementer MUST NOT write to `trial/*` or `main`; those are owned by the integrator. The `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial MUST NOT be set by the implementer.
    94

item.text:
**1. Missing Context**

Assessment: The plan is mostly scoped clearly, but it leaves out operator-facing context the evaluator likely needs.

- The plan makes the evaluator module unconditional and explicitly avoids any repo flag or git proxying ([eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:17), [eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:36), [eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:419)), but the evaluator contract still assumes read access to the repository at the trial tip ([03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:121)). The plan should say how a human evaluator learns the repo location in this deployment, or at least document that as an explicit prerequisite in the UI/README.
- The draft page context is underspecified for real evaluation. The plan says the trial’s own `artifacts_uri` is “therefore always `None`” while evaluating ([eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:132)), but the implementer contract explicitly allows setting `artifacts_uri` and `description` on a successful `starting` trial ([03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:87)). That means the evaluator may need existing trial-side context that this plan currently omits.

**2. Feasibility**

Assessment: I do not think the plan is implementable as written. There are a couple of concrete contract mismatches that need to be resolved first.

- The proposed evaluator `description` field has nowhere to go. The plan adds it to the route/form/template surface ([eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:100), [eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:219), [eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:365)), but `EvaluateSubmission` only carries `status`, `trial_id`, `metrics`, and `artifacts_uri` ([submissions.py](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/reference/packages/eden-storage/src/eden_storage/submissions.py:53)), and the spec does not give the evaluator any submission field that writes `description` ([03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:141)). As written, that field would be silently dropped. The plan needs to either remove it or explicitly widen the spec/storage shape.
- The retry/read-back design is not actually safe if `WrongToken` and `IllegalTransition` are treated as definitive short-circuits ([eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:280)). `Store.submit` raises `IllegalTransition` once a previously committed submission has already been terminalized, and `WrongToken` when the claim no longer matches ([ _base.py ](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/reference/packages/eden-storage/src/eden_storage/_base.py:583)). Both can happen after a successful first submit whose response was lost. In that case, short-circuiting skips the very read-back reconciliation the plan says it wants. Those exceptions should feed into read-back when they occur after an indeterminate submit attempt, not bypass it.
- The “metrics validation parity” claim is inaccurate. The plan says it duplicates the spec/store rules ([eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:174)), but its proposed `EvaluateDraft.metrics` type cannot represent `null` at all ([eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/docs/plans/eden-phase-9d-evaluator-module.md:219)), while the data model explicitly allows `null` metric values when the evaluator declines to report ([02-data-model.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/02-data-model.md:19)). If the UI is intentionally stricter than the protocol, the plan should say that explicitly; otherwise the parser design needs to change.

I’m stopping here because those feasibility issues are significant enough that I would not spend review time on alternatives/completeness/edge cases until they’re fixed.

**Overall Assessment**

The route/template/test shape is directionally reasonable, but the plan is not ready as-is. I would revise it to remove or re-spec the evaluator `description`, correct the retry/read-back logic so committed submits are not misclassified after terminalization, and clarify the evaluator’s real input context, especially repository location and existing trial-side artifacts/description.
